transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Delgado — I'm Dr. Okafor. You were referred over for a screening colonoscopy. Is this your first one?
PT: Yes, my very first. My doctor said now that I'm fifty-two it's time.
DR: That's right, that's the standard recommendation. Before we schedule, I want to make sure this is really just screening. Any belly pain, any changes in your bowel habits?
PT: No, nothing like that. Everything's normal, regular.
DR: Any blood in the stool, or black stools? Anything on the toilet paper?
PT: No, none of that. I'd have noticed.
DR: Any unexplained weight loss, or fatigue?
PT: No, I feel good, honestly. Same as always.
DR: Great. And any family history of colon cancer or polyps — parents, siblings?
PT: No, no one in my family that I know of.
NURSE: Let me get her vitals down — height's five foot four, weight one hundred forty-six pounds, temp thirty-six point eight, heart rate seventy-two, respirations fourteen, oxygen ninety-eight percent on room air, and blood pressure today was 124 over 78.
DR: Perfect, that's right at goal. Let me go over your medications. The chart shows lisinopril, ten milligrams, once a day. Still taking that?
PT: Yes, every morning for my blood pressure.
DR: Anything else — any aspirin, any blood thinners like warfarin or Eliquis, or fish oil, anything like that?
PT: No, nothing like that. Just the lisinopril and a multivitamin.
DR: Good — that makes the prep and the procedure simpler, nothing to hold beforehand. Any allergies to medications?
PT: No, none that I know of.
DR: Wonderful. So this is a straightforward average-risk screening. Your bloodwork from May looked normal too. I'll have you keep taking your lisinopril, even the morning of, with a small sip of water. The team will send you the bowel prep instructions and a date.
PT: Sounds good. How long does the prep take?
DR: We'll walk you through it — it's the evening before and the morning of. Any questions, just call us.
PT: Thank you, doctor.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Delgado is a 52-year-old woman referred to GI clinic for her first, average-risk screening colonoscopy. She is asymptomatic: denies hematochezia, melena, abdominal pain, weight loss, change in bowel habits, or fatigue. No personal history of polyps or colorectal cancer, and no family history of colorectal cancer or polyps. Past medical history is notable for hypertension, well controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily (confirmed by patient). She denies use of aspirin, antiplatelet agents, or anticoagulants, including over-the-counter fish oil; takes only a daily multivitamin in addition to lisinopril. No known drug allergies.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 124/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 66 kg (146 lb), BMI 24.8. Recent labs (2026-05-04): hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL, platelets 268 x10^3/uL, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL — all within normal limits.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Average-risk colorectal cancer screening, first colonoscopy — appropriate indication; asymptomatic on interview.
- No anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy — nothing to hold; no bridging or anesthesia anticoagulation concern.
- Hypertension, well controlled — continue lisinopril 10 mg daily; may take morning of procedure with a sip of water.
- No known drug allergies; normal baseline labs.
- Schedule screening colonoscopy; bowel prep instructions and date to follow. Return precautions reviewed.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your first colonoscopy for routine colon cancer screening

• You are not on any blood thinners

Next steps

• Keep taking your lisinopril each morning, including the morning of the procedure with a small sip of water.
• No medications need to be stopped before the procedure.
• Follow the bowel prep instructions the evening before and the morning of.
• Call us with any questions, or if you develop new rectal bleeding, belly pain, or other concerns before the procedure.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Whitfield. You're here so we can plan your screening colonoscopy — your primary care doctor sent you over. Sounds like this would be your first one?
MA: Before the doctor digs in, let me get your vitals down, Mr. Whitfield. Height's 5 foot 10, weight 185 pounds. Temp 98.2, heart rate 72, respirations 14, blood pressure 128 over 78, and your oxygen is 98 percent on room air.
PT: That's right. I've been meaning to get it done for years, honestly. I turned sixty-one and my doctor finally put his foot down.
DR: Better late than never. Before we schedule, let me make sure I understand how you're feeling. Any blood in the stool, black or tarry stools, anything like that?
PT: No, nothing like that. No blood, no black stools.
DR: Any change in your bowel habits, belly pain, or weight loss you didn't expect?
PT: No, none of that. I feel fine, really. That's why I kept putting it off.
DR: That's reassuring — this stays a routine screening. Let me go over your medications. I have atorvastatin, forty milligrams at night?
PT: Yes, for cholesterol. Every night.
DR: And a baby aspirin, eighty-one milligrams daily?
PT: Yep, one a day. I started that myself a couple years back, for the heart. Never had a heart attack or a stent or anything, just figured it couldn't hurt.
DR: Good to know — that's primary prevention, and for a low-dose aspirin like that we don't need to stop it for the procedure. Are you on any blood thinners?
PT: Just the blood thinner my buddy recommended — the fish oil. I take one of those every morning.
DR: Ah — okay, that's important to be clear on. Fish oil is a supplement, not a true blood thinner like warfarin or Eliquis. You're not on anything like that?
PT: No, no, nothing prescription. Just the fish oil, the aspirin, and the cholesterol pill.
DR: Perfect, that makes it simple. Anything over the counter for aches or pain?
PT: I take ibuprofen for my knee — four hundred milligrams, maybe three or four times a week when it acts up.
DR: Thanks, that one I do want you to hold around the procedure since it can increase bleeding if we remove any polyps. I'll write down exactly when to stop it. Otherwise your labs look great and there's nothing that holds us up.
PT: Sounds good. What do I need to do to get ready?
DR: The team will send your prep instructions and the schedule. Keep the statin and aspirin going, pause the ibuprofen when we tell you, and we'll get you booked.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Whitfield is a 61-year-old man presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan a first-time, average-risk screening colonoscopy (overdue; no prior colonoscopy on record). He is asymptomatic — denies melena, hematochezia, change in bowel habits, abdominal pain, and unintentional weight loss. He confirms atorvastatin 40 mg nightly for hyperlipidemia and self-initiated aspirin 81 mg daily for primary prevention (no prior cardiac event or stent). When asked about anticoagulants he described his over-the-counter fish oil as a 'blood thinner'; on clarification he takes only OTC fish oil (~1000 mg daily) and no prescription anticoagulant/antiplatelet beyond low-dose aspirin. He additionally reports OTC ibuprofen 400 mg several times weekly for knee pain, not previously documented.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 128/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 84 kg (185 lb), BMI 26.5. Recent labs — hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL, hematocrit 43.1%, platelets 244 x10^3/uL (2026-06-20); LDL 92 mg/dL (2026-01-10). No anemia; hematologic parameters within normal limits.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Average-risk screening colonoscopy, overdue — schedule screening colonoscopy; asymptomatic with reassuring labs.
- Antithrombotic review: low-dose aspirin 81 mg for primary prevention — continue, no hold indicated per protocol. 'Blood thinner' clarified to be OTC fish oil, not a therapeutic anticoagulant — no hold required; add to supplement list.
- OTC ibuprofen (NSAID) several times weekly — advise holding ~5-7 days before procedure given possible polypectomy/bleeding risk; provide explicit stop date.
- Hyperlipidemia — continue atorvastatin 40 mg nightly.
- Update medication/supplement list: add OTC fish oil and OTC ibuprofen (PRN).
- Bowel prep instructions and scheduling to follow; return precautions reviewed.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Planning your first screening colonoscopy (you are due for it)
• You are feeling well with no warning symptoms
• Your medications and supplements

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy; the team will send prep instructions and the schedule.
• Keep taking your atorvastatin at night and your low-dose aspirin each day — you do NOT need to stop the aspirin for this procedure.
• Your fish oil is a supplement, not a true blood thinner — we added it to your record.
• Stop the over-the-counter ibuprofen before the procedure on the date we give you, since it can increase bleeding.
• Call if you develop rectal bleeding, black stools, belly pain, or unexpected weight loss before your visit.

Findings: two diminutive-to-small polyps — a 6 mm sessile polyp in the ascending colon and a 4 mm sessile polyp in the descending colon, both removed by cold snare and retrieved. No other lesions; no mass. Pathology (separate report): both tubular adenomas without high-grade dysplasia. Recommendation: repeat colonoscopy in 5 years (2026). No complications.

Routine chronic disease follow-up. Type 2 diabetes well controlled on metformin 1000 mg BID; most recent A1c 6.9%. Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; TSH within range. No GI complaints — denies rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits, or weight loss. Reminded that colon polyp surveillance colonoscopy is due in 2026; referral to GI placed.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Okafor. You're here so we can plan your surveillance colonoscopy — you had a couple of polyps removed back in 2021, and you're due for the five-year check. How are you feeling?
MA: Before the doctor gets into it — let me just get your vitals. You're 5 foot 5, 159 pounds. Blood pressure 124 over 78, heart rate 74, temp is 98.2, and your oxygen's 98 percent.
PT: Feeling fine, honestly. No complaints. My doctor just said it was time.
DR: That's what I like to hear. Let me ask the important stuff — any rectal bleeding, blood in the stool, dark stools?
PT: No, nothing like that.
DR: Any change in your bowel habits, belly pain, unintentional weight loss?
PT: No. Everything's been normal. Same as always.
DR: Good. Now, I want to double-check something in your chart, because it looks a little off. It has colon cancer listed under your own diagnoses. Have you ever been told you had colon cancer yourself?
PT: No, no — never. I've never had cancer. That must be my dad. He had colon cancer.
DR: Ah — that makes much more sense. Do you remember about how old he was?
PT: He was 72, I think. He did fine after, actually.
DR: Okay, thank you — that's family history, not your own, and I'll fix that in the record. It still matters for you, but it's a different thing than having had it yourself. Now your polyps in 2021 — the pathology showed two small tubular adenomas, which is exactly why we do the five-year follow-up.
PT: Right.
DR: Let me confirm your medicines. Metformin, one thousand milligrams twice a day?
PT: Yes, morning and night.
DR: And levothyroxine, 75 micrograms every morning?
PT: Yes, first thing, empty stomach.
DR: Perfect. Your sugars and thyroid both looked well controlled on your December labs — A1c was 6.9. Any allergies to medications?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: Great. We'll get the colonoscopy scheduled, and the team will send your bowel prep instructions and go over what to do with the metformin the day before.
PT: Sounds good. Thank you.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Okafor is a 58-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan a surveillance colonoscopy. She has a history of two tubular adenomas (ascending and descending colon) removed on screening colonoscopy in 2021, with a recommended 5-year surveillance interval (due 2026). She is asymptomatic — denies rectal bleeding, melena, change in bowel habits, abdominal pain, and unintentional weight loss. She confirms taking metformin 1000 mg twice daily and levothyroxine 75 mcg every morning. No known drug allergies.

Family history: On review, the chart listed colon cancer as one of the patient's own diagnoses. The patient clarified she has NO personal history of any cancer; the colon cancer is her father's, diagnosed at approximately age 72. This is a single first-degree relative diagnosed after age 60.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 74, RR 14, BP 124/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 165 cm (5'5"), Wt 72 kg (159 lb), BMI 26.4. Recent labs (2025-12-05): Hemoglobin A1c 6.9%, TSH 2.1 mIU/L, hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL — diabetes and thyroid well controlled, no anemia.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Surveillance colonoscopy indicated for prior 2 tubular adenomas (2021), due 2026 — patient asymptomatic. Proceed with scheduling.
- Correct problem list: remove 'Malignant tumor of colon' as a personal diagnosis (charting error); it is FAMILY history — father, colon cancer at ~72. Retain as FamilyMemberHistory.
- Type 2 diabetes — well controlled (A1c 6.9%); continue metformin 1000 mg BID; hold metformin on prep/procedure day per pre-procedure instructions.
- Hypothyroidism — stable (TSH 2.1); continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily.
- No known drug allergies documented; confirmed with patient.
- Colonoscopy scheduling + bowel prep instructions to follow; standard return precautions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your follow-up (surveillance) colonoscopy, due this year after polyps removed in 2021
• You are feeling well with no bowel symptoms
• Your diabetes and thyroid, both well controlled
• A correction to your medical record about family history

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy; the team will send bowel prep instructions and tell you how to adjust your metformin around the procedure.
• We corrected your record: colon cancer was listed as your own diagnosis by mistake — it is your father's history (diagnosed around age 72). You have not had cancer yourself.
• Keep taking metformin 1000 mg twice daily and levothyroxine 75 mcg each morning.
• Call us if you develop rectal bleeding, belly pain, or other new symptoms before the procedure.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Nakamura. You're here so we can plan your follow-up colonoscopy. How are you doing?
MA: Before the doctor gets started, let me grab your vitals. Height's five foot nine, weight's 176 pounds. Blood pressure 126 over 74, heart rate 70, temp 98.2, respirations 14, and your oxygen is 98 percent on room air.
PT: Doing well, thanks. No complaints, really. My doctor said it was time for the repeat one.
DR: That's right — back in 2023 you had a polyp removed, a sessile serrated one, and the recommendation was to repeat in three years. Does that match your memory?
PT: Yes, they found one, took it out, and told me come back in three years. So here I am.
DR: Perfect. Let me ask a few things. Any rectal bleeding — blood on the paper or in the bowl, or any black or tarry stools?
PT: No, nothing like that. No bleeding at all. Bowels are regular, no changes.
DR: Good. Any belly pain, weight loss, that kind of thing?
PT: No, none of that.
DR: Let me go over your medications. I have amlodipine, five milligrams, for blood pressure — still taking that?
PT: Yes, every day. Blood pressure's been good.
DR: And I see fish oil on your list. Anything else?
PT: Well, that's the thing — I stopped my blood thinner already, on my own, because I read online you're supposed to stop it before a colonoscopy. I didn't want to cause a problem.
DR: When you say blood thinner, which medication do you mean?
PT: The fish oil. The omega-3 capsules. That's my blood thinner, right?
DR: Ah — I'm glad you brought that up. Fish oil is a supplement, not a true blood thinner like warfarin or a prescription anticoagulant. It doesn't actually need to be held for the procedure. You're not on any prescription blood thinner or aspirin?
PT: No, nothing like that. Just the fish oil and the blood pressure pill.
DR: Then you're in good shape — you can even keep taking the fish oil if you like. Any allergies to medications?
PT: No, none that I know of.
DR: Great. So this is a straightforward surveillance scope. We'll get you scheduled and the team will send the prep instructions.
PT: Sounds good. Thank you for clearing that up.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Nakamura is a 67-year-old man presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan surveillance colonoscopy. History of a 6 mm sessile serrated lesion in the ascending colon removed in 2023 (no dysplasia), with a recommended 3-year surveillance interval (due 2026). He denies rectal bleeding, melena, abdominal pain, weight loss, or change in bowel habits — consistent with the referral. He confirms taking amlodipine 5 mg daily for hypertension. He reports he stopped his over-the-counter fish oil on his own, believing it to be a 'blood thinner' that must be held before colonoscopy; on clarification this is an omega-3 supplement, not a prescription anticoagulant or antiplatelet. He is on no prescription anticoagulant, antiplatelet, or aspirin. No known drug allergies. Past surgical history notable for appendectomy (1988).

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 70, RR 14, BP 126/74, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 80 kg (176 lb), BMI 26.1. Recent hemoglobin (2026-07-02) 14.6 g/dL (normal), no evidence of anemia.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Surveillance colonoscopy indicated for prior sessile serrated lesion (2023, no dysplasia); interval-appropriate at 3 years. Asymptomatic — no alarm features (no bleeding, no anemia).
- Anticoagulation/anesthesia clearance: no prescription anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy. Over-the-counter fish oil does NOT require holding for colonoscopy; patient reassured he may resume/continue it. No bridging needed.
- Medication list: amlodipine 5 mg daily — continue. Fish oil — supplement, no procedural hold required; correct patient's 'blood thinner' misunderstanding in the record.
- Hypertension — well controlled; continue amlodipine, hold on the morning of the procedure per standard NPO/anesthesia guidance only if instructed.
- No known drug allergies confirmed.
- Schedule surveillance colonoscopy; bowel prep instructions and NPO guidance to follow.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your follow-up (surveillance) colonoscopy, due this year
• Your 2023 polyp (a sessile serrated polyp that was removed)
• Your medications and whether anything needs to be stopped
• No rectal bleeding or bowel changes — a reassuring, routine check

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy; the team will send prep instructions and what to do the day before.
• Good news about the fish oil: it is a supplement, not a true blood thinner, so it does not need to be stopped for the procedure — you can keep taking it.
• You are not on any prescription blood thinner or aspirin, so no special holding or bridging is needed.
• Keep taking your amlodipine for blood pressure as usual (we'll tell you what to do on the morning of the procedure).
• Call us if you develop rectal bleeding, black stools, belly pain, or unusual tiredness before your appointment.

transcript:
DR: Hi Ms. Tran, I'm one of the GI doctors — you were referred for a colonoscopy because of the low iron. Before we schedule, I want to hear the story in your own words. How have you been feeling?
MA: Before the doctor goes on, let me get your vitals — you're 5 foot 4 and 146 pounds today, blood pressure 122 over 76, heart rate 86, temp 98.2, and your oxygen is 98 percent on room air.
PT: Tired, mostly. I get winded going up the stairs now, which isn't like me. My primary doctor said my blood count and iron were low again.
DR: They are — your hemoglobin was about nine-eight and your ferritin was six, which is quite low. The referral mentions heavy periods in the past. Where do things stand with that now?
PT: Oh, that's all done. I had the ablation done, back in 2024, and since then my periods are basically nothing — maybe a little spotting once in a while. So it's not that anymore.
DR: That's actually important — because if the periods aren't the cause, we need to make sure nothing in the GI tract is quietly bleeding. Have you noticed any blood, or dark or black bowel movements?
PT: Not blood-blood. Sometimes they look darker, kind of black, but I figured that was the iron pills doing it, so I didn't think much of it.
DR: Good that you mentioned it — dark stools can be the iron, but they can also mean bleeding higher up, so we don't want to assume. Let me go through your medicines. The chart has the iron tablet and omeprazole.
PT: Yes, I take the omeprazole every morning for my reflux, that helps. And the iron I do take, usually with my tea in the afternoon.
DR: Anything else — even over-the-counter or things you take on your own?
PT: I take a baby aspirin every day. My dad had a heart attack young, so I just started it myself to be safe. And ibuprofen for my knee, a couple hundred milligrams, few times a week when it flares.
DR: Okay — those two matter a lot for the picture, I'll come back to them. Any allergies?
PT: The sulfa antibiotics — I got a rash from one. I don't think it's in your system.
DR: I'll add that. So here's my thinking: low iron, no more menstrual source, plus daily aspirin and regular ibuprofen, and some dark stools — I'd want to look both directions, not just the colonoscopy. Let's plan for that and I'll go over what to hold beforehand.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Linda Tran is a 49-year-old woman referred to GI clinic for evaluation of iron-deficiency anemia (Hgb 9.8 g/dL, ferritin 6 ug/L, MCV 74 fL) with a request for diagnostic colonoscopy. She reports progressive fatigue and exertional dyspnea. History of menorrhagia is now RESOLVED following endometrial ablation in 2024 — menses are minimal to absent, so a menstrual source no longer explains the ongoing severe iron deficiency. On directed questioning she describes intermittently dark/black stools that she attributed to oral iron, without overt hematochezia. She confirms daily omeprazole 20 mg for reflux (good effect) and daily ferrous sulfate 325 mg (taken with tea, imperfect tolerance). She discloses self-initiated daily aspirin 81 mg (family history of early MI in her father) and regular OTC ibuprofen 200-400 mg several times weekly for knee pain — neither documented in the chart. She reports a sulfa antibiotic (sulfamethoxazole) rash, not on file.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 86, RR 14, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 66 kg (146 lb), BMI 24.8. Labs 2026-06-25: hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL (low), ferritin 6 ug/L (low), MCV 74 fL (low), platelets 240 k/uL — microcytic iron-deficiency anemia.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Iron-deficiency anemia in a 49-year-old woman with a resolved menstrual source — unexplained IDA. Regular NSAID and daily aspirin use plus dark stools raise concern for occult GI blood loss. Plan BIDIRECTIONAL endoscopy (EGD + colonoscopy) rather than colonoscopy alone; will document rationale to expand the referral.
- Reconcile medications: ADD aspirin 81 mg daily (self-directed) and OTC ibuprofen — advise holding NSAIDs; discuss peri-procedure aspirin management (low cardiac risk, self-started — reasonable to hold before polypectomy).
- Continue omeprazole 20 mg daily (confirmed).
- Continue ferrous sulfate 325 mg; counsel to separate from tea/coffee and take with vitamin C to improve absorption.
- Add allergy: sulfamethoxazole (rash).
- Dark stools noted — do not presume iron effect; upper endoscopy will assess. Consider fecal occult testing if procedures delayed.
- Scheduling and split bowel prep to follow; return precautions for overt bleeding, lightheadedness, syncope.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Low iron and low blood count (iron-deficiency anemia) and feeling tired
• Your periods are no longer a likely cause since your 2024 procedure
• Darker stools you had noticed
• The daily baby aspirin and the ibuprofen you take for your knee
• Your reflux and current medicines

Next steps
• We plan to examine both the upper and lower digestive tract (an upper endoscopy in addition to the colonoscopy) to find the source of blood/iron loss. The team will schedule these and send prep instructions.
• We will tell you which medicines to hold before the procedures — this includes stopping ibuprofen and likely pausing the daily aspirin; please wait for our specific instructions.
• Keep taking omeprazole each morning.
• Keep taking your iron, but separate it from tea or coffee and take it with a vitamin C source or orange juice.
• We updated your record: added daily aspirin and ibuprofen use, and a sulfa antibiotic allergy (rash).
• Call for any large amount of blood in the stool, black tarry stools, lightheadedness, or fainting.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Hobbs — thanks for coming in. Your primary care doctor sent you over so we can plan a colonoscopy. Your daughter's here with you too?
MA: I've got Mr. Hobbs' rooming vitals — height 5 foot 9, weight 173 pounds, blood pressure 126 over 78, pulse 76, temp 98.2, respirations 14, oxygen 97 percent on room air.
FAMILY: Yes, I drove him. I wanted to make sure everything gets mentioned.
DR: Perfect. So tell me what's been going on.
PT: Well, the last three months or so my bowels have been off. The stools are — this is a little embarrassing — they've gotten thin, like a pencil, and things have just been looser and different than normal.
DR: That's exactly the kind of change we need to look into. And I see a note about weight — has that changed?
PT: Yeah. I haven't been trying, but my belt's on a tighter notch. Probably eight pounds since spring.
FAMILY: His clothes are definitely looser. And Dad, tell him about Grandpa.
PT: Oh — my father had colon cancer, in his sixties. I don't think anybody ever wrote that down for me.
DR: I'm really glad you mentioned it — that matters and I'll add it. Let me go over your medications. The chart shows tamsulosin for your prostate.
PT: Right, one every night. That's been fine.
DR: Anything else, including things you buy over the counter?
PT: I take a blood thinner.
DR: Okay — that's important before a scope. Which one, do you know the name?
PT: The fish oil. My neighbor said it thins the blood, keeps things flowing.
DR: Ah — so that's an omega-3 fish oil supplement, not a prescription blood thinner. Good to know the difference; that changes what we'd worry about. Any actual prescription anticoagulant?
PT: No. Just — oh, I do take a baby aspirin every day. Started it myself a couple years ago for my heart.
DR: Thank you — that one does matter for the procedure, and it isn't in your chart, so I'll note it. You quit smoking a while back?
PT: 2010. Smoked about thirty years before that.
DR: Got it. With the weight loss and the stool changes, we're going to move quickly on the colonoscopy. The team will give you prep instructions and tell you exactly what to hold beforehand.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Hobbs is a 71-year-old man referred for diagnostic colonoscopy after ~3 months of change in bowel habits with narrower-caliber ('pencil-thin') stools and looser bowel movements. He confirms unintentional weight loss of approximately 8 lb over the same period (belt tighter, clothes looser). He denies overt hematochezia or melena. New family history elicited today: father with colon cancer in his 60s (not previously documented). Medication reconciliation: confirms tamsulosin 0.4 mg nightly for BPH. He initially reported taking a 'blood thinner,' which on questioning is over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3) — not a prescription anticoagulant. He also reports self-initiated daily low-dose aspirin 81 mg, which was not on the chart. Former smoker, 30 pack-years, quit 2010 — confirmed.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 76, RR 14, BP 126/78, SpO2 97% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 78.5 kg (173 lb), BMI 25.6 (down from ~82 kg / 181 lb ~3 months prior). Labs (2026-07-01): hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL, MCV 81 fL, fecal occult blood Positive. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no palpable mass; no acute distress.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Change in bowel habits with narrowed stool caliber and unintentional weight loss (alarm features) plus positive FOBT and family history of colorectal cancer — concerning for colorectal neoplasm; expedite diagnostic colonoscopy.
- Add family history: father with colon cancer (60s).
- Medication reconciliation: 'blood thinner' clarified as OTC fish oil (omega-3) — not a therapeutic anticoagulant; does not by itself require periprocedural anticoagulation management. Add daily aspirin 81 mg to medication list — provide pre-procedure hold guidance per polypectomy bleeding-risk protocol.
- Continue tamsulosin 0.4 mg nightly for BPH.
- Colonoscopy scheduling + bowel prep instructions to follow; clear return precautions for bleeding, obstruction, or worsening symptoms.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Change in your bowel habits and thinner (pencil-shaped) stools
• Unintentional weight loss (about 8 pounds)
• A stool test that came back positive for blood
• Family history: your father's colon cancer
• Planning a colonoscopy soon

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy promptly; the team will send prep instructions and tell you exactly what to stop before the procedure.
• We clarified your medicines: the 'blood thinner' you mentioned is fish oil (a supplement), and we added your daily baby aspirin to your record. We will tell you whether to pause the aspirin before the procedure.
• Keep taking your tamsulosin at night.
• Call us for any rectal bleeding, black stools, severe belly pain, vomiting, or inability to pass stool or gas.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Yuen. Your primary care doctor sent you over so we can plan a screening colonoscopy, and I understand you wanted to talk things through before we schedule anything.
MA: Before we get started, let me grab your vitals — you're 5 foot 4, 146 pounds; blood pressure 132 over 84, heart rate 78, temp 36.8, breathing 14, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Yes, thank you. I'll be honest, it's not the colonoscopy itself — it's the sedation. That's what I'm anxious about. I actually took one of my lorazepam this morning just to get myself here.
NURSE: That's noted — she took a 0.5 milligram lorazepam around eight this morning.
DR: Okay, good to know, that's exactly the sort of thing we want documented. Can you tell me what happened with anesthesia before? The chart mentions motion sickness.
PT: It says motion sickness? That's not really it. When I had my knee scoped back in 2019, I was fully under, and when I woke up I was throwing up — over and over in the recovery room. It wasn't the car ride or anything, it was the anesthesia. They had to give me something in my IV.
DR: That's an important distinction. That's what we call post-operative nausea and vomiting, and it changes how we plan. For your procedure we can use anti-nausea medicine ahead of time and choose the sedation carefully. Does that ease things a bit?
PT: A lot, actually. As long as I'm not going to wake up sick again.
DR: We'll build the plan around that. Let me check your medicines. Sertraline — the chart says 100 milligrams.
PT: My psychiatrist bumped me up to 150 about two months ago. And the lorazepam, just now and then, maybe once a month.
DR: I'll update the sertraline to 150. Any family history of colon problems? The referral has you down as average risk.
PT: Well — my dad had colon cancer. He was around 68 when they found it.
DR: That's significant — a parent with colon cancer actually puts you above average risk, so I'm glad you mentioned it. It doesn't change that we're doing the colonoscopy, but it does affect how often we repeat it. Let's get you scheduled, and I'll arrange the anti-nausea plan and prep.
PT: Thank you. That makes me feel better about all of it.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Yuen is a 55-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consultation regarding screening colonoscopy. She has no GI symptoms — denies rectal bleeding, weight loss, or change in bowel habits. Her primary concern is anxiety about sedation. History of generalized anxiety disorder; she took lorazepam 0.5 mg this morning for appointment-related anxiety and reports PRN use roughly monthly. She reports her sertraline was increased to 150 mg daily approximately 2 months ago (chart lists 100 mg). Regarding prior anesthesia: the chart characterizes her 2019 reaction as motion sickness, but she describes waking from general anesthesia during a knee arthroscopy and vomiting repeatedly in the recovery room requiring IV anti-emetic — consistent with postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) rather than motion sickness. On direct questioning she reports her father was diagnosed with colon cancer at approximately age 68 — not captured on the referral, which listed her as average risk.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 78, RR 14, BP 132/84, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 66 kg (146 lb), BMI 24.8. Recent hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL (2026-06-30), within normal limits. Well-appearing, in no distress; mildly anxious affect.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Screening colonoscopy — reclassify risk: first-degree relative (father) with colorectal cancer at ~68 raises patient above average risk; plan colonoscopy now and shorten surveillance interval per findings/guidelines.
- PONV history (previously mischaracterized as motion sickness) — flag for anesthesia/sedation; plan peri-procedural anti-emetic prophylaxis and discuss sedation approach with proceduralist/anesthesia.
- Sedation anxiety / GAD — reassurance provided; continue current psychiatric care; document benzodiazepine use.
- Medication reconciliation: update sertraline to 150 mg daily; continue lorazepam 0.5 mg PRN. Note lorazepam taken this morning — reinforce escort/no-driving instructions for procedure day.
- Bowel prep instructions and scheduling to follow.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Planning your screening colonoscopy
• Your worry about sedation
• The nausea you had after anesthesia in the past
• Your family history and your medicines

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy and the team will send bowel prep instructions.
• Because you got sick after anesthesia before, we will give anti-nausea medicine ahead of time and plan your sedation carefully.
• Because your father had colon cancer, you are above average risk — this affects how often we repeat the test, and we will explain the schedule after your procedure.
• We updated your record: sertraline changed to 150 mg daily; your anesthesia reaction noted as post-operative nausea (not motion sickness).
• You will need someone to drive you home; do not drive the day of the procedure.
• Call us with any questions before your appointment.

transcript:
DR: Mr. Carter, good to meet you. Your doctor sent you over so we can plan a colonoscopy to look into the bleeding. Tell me what's been happening.
MA: Before we get into it, let me get your vitals down — you're 5 foot 10, 185 pounds, temp's 98.2, heart rate 74, breathing 14, blood pressure 122 over 78, and oxygen 98 percent on room air. All looks good.
PT: Yeah, for about two weeks now there's been blood when I go. Bright red, and it's kind of coating the stool — not just a little on the paper like before.
DR: That distinction matters, thank you. You said 'like before' — have you had bleeding in the past?
PT: On and off. My regular doctor told me it was probably hemorrhoids, but honestly nobody ever looked. This time it feels different, more of it, and it's on the stool itself.
DR: Understood — so the hemorrhoid label was never actually confirmed with an exam or scope?
PT: Right, never scoped. First time for me.
DR: Any dizziness, passing out, short of breath?
PT: No, nothing like that. I feel fine otherwise, energy's good.
DR: Good. The chart says you take no medications. Is that right?
PT: No prescriptions, yeah. I do take ibuprofen for my back though — maybe three, four times a week, sometimes two at a time when it's bad.
DR: Okay, that's important — ibuprofen is a medication that can irritate the gut and contribute to bleeding, so I need that on your list. Anything else, even over the counter or supplements?
PT: Just my blood thinner. The fish oil. I take it every day.
DR: Ah — fish oil. That's a supplement, not a true blood thinner like warfarin, so it doesn't change our anesthesia plan the same way, but I'll note it. You're not on any actual anticoagulant?
PT: No, no, nothing like that. Just the fish oil.
DR: Perfect. Family history — any colon cancer or polyps in the family?
PT: My dad, actually. He had colon cancer, I think around fifty.
DR: That's very relevant, and I'm glad you mentioned it — a parent with colon cancer changes how we think about this. So: two weeks of blood coating the stool, ibuprofen a few times a week, and your dad's history — we'll get you scheduled for the colonoscopy and go over the prep and what to hold.
PT: Sounds good. Should I stop the ibuprofen?
DR: Yes, let's hold it — the team will give you the full prep instructions.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Carter is a 45-year-old man referred to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult and planning of diagnostic colonoscopy. He reports approximately 2 weeks of hematochezia — bright red blood coating the stool (a change from prior occasional blood only on the paper), low-volume, ongoing. He denies lightheadedness, syncope, or dyspnea. He has a longstanding patient-reported history of 'hemorrhoids' but has never undergone anoscopy or colonoscopy; the current bleeding is described as different in character and volume. Contrary to the referral (documented as 'no medications' and 'denies NSAID use'), he reports taking OTC ibuprofen 400-600 mg roughly 3-4 times per week for chronic low back pain. He also takes daily fish oil, which he refers to as his 'blood thinner'; he takes no prescription anticoagulant. He reports his father was diagnosed with colon cancer around age 50 — not previously documented.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 74, RR 14, BP 122/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 84 kg (185 lb), BMI 26.5. Recent labs (2026-06-28): hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL, hematocrit 42.0%, platelets 262 x10^3/uL — no anemia or thrombocytopenia. Hemodynamically well by history.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Hematochezia x2 weeks, blood coating stool, in a 45-year-old man with a first-degree relative with colorectal cancer — proceed with diagnostic colonoscopy. Do not anchor on unconfirmed hemorrhoid diagnosis; evaluate full colon.
- Regular NSAID use (ibuprofen 3-4x/week) — reconcile onto medication list (chart erroneously stated none/denied NSAID); advise holding ibuprofen periprocedurally and consider as a contributor to bleeding.
- Fish oil supplement — document as supplement; not a therapeutic anticoagulant, no special anticoagulation hold required beyond routine.
- Add family history: father with colorectal cancer (~age 50); relevant to workup and future surveillance interval.
- Update problem list: hemorrhoids remain unconfirmed (never scoped) — do not treat as established diagnosis.
- Colonoscopy scheduling and bowel prep instructions to follow; return precautions for heavy bleeding or hemodynamic symptoms.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Bright red blood coating your stool for about 2 weeks
• Planning a colonoscopy to find the cause
• Your family history of colon cancer (father)
• The medicines and supplements you take

Next steps
• We will schedule your diagnostic colonoscopy; the team will send prep instructions and a list of what to stop before the procedure.
• Please hold the ibuprofen — it can irritate the gut and add to bleeding. We added it to your medication list.
• We noted your daily fish oil as a supplement; it is not a true blood thinner, so it does not change the plan, and you are not on any prescription blood thinner.
• We added your father's history of colon cancer to your record.
• We did not treat the past 'hemorrhoids' as a confirmed diagnosis, since it was never examined — the colonoscopy will look at everything.
• Call for heavy bleeding, lightheadedness, fainting, or shortness of breath.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Rosenbaum. I'm Dr. Okafor. Your new primary care doctor sent you over to talk about a screening colonoscopy. How are you feeling today?
MA: Before the doctor came in I took your vitals, Ms. Rosenbaum — you're 5 foot 4 and 141 pounds, blood pressure 130 over 74, heart rate 68, temperature 98.2, breathing 14, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Very well, thank you. I feel healthy, honestly. I walk a couple of miles every morning, I do all my own cooking and errands. My daughter says I run her ragged.
DR: That's wonderful to hear. So let me make sure I understand — have you ever had any colon cancer screening before? A colonoscopy, or one of the stool tests?
PT: No, never. Not once. I know I probably should have years ago, but I always felt fine so I put it off. Now Dr. Lin says I really ought to.
DR: Okay. And any symptoms at all — any bleeding, change in your bathroom habits, belly pain, weight loss, anything like that?
PT: No, nothing like that. Everything's regular. No blood, no pain.
DR: Good, and your bloodwork last month was reassuring — your blood count was completely normal, no sign of anemia. Let me be straightforward with you about one thing, because I want you involved in the decision. You're 76. For someone your age, the guidelines don't say everyone should be screened automatically — it becomes an individual decision that depends on your overall health and how the benefits and risks line up for you.
PT: I understand. But I'm in good shape, aren't I?
DR: You genuinely are — that's exactly the point. Because you're so active and healthy, and you've never been screened at all, you're likely to benefit more than the average person your age. The main risks are from the prep and the sedation, but those are low for you. So it's reasonable to proceed if you'd like to.
PT: I would. I'd rather know than wonder.
DR: That's a very reasonable choice, and I'll document that we talked it through. Let me confirm your medications — the chart shows losartan 50 for blood pressure and a calcium with vitamin D. Still taking both?
PT: Yes, the losartan every morning, and the calcium tablet with dinner.
DR: Any allergies to medications?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: Perfect. No blood thinners, aspirin, anything like that?
PT: No, none of those.
DR: Then we'll get you scheduled and send the prep instructions. The team will walk you through everything.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Rosenbaum is a 76-year-old woman referred by a new PCP for a first-time screening colonoscopy. She has never undergone any colorectal cancer screening (no prior colonoscopy, sigmoidoscopy, FIT, or stool DNA test). She is asymptomatic — denies hematochezia, melena, change in bowel habits, abdominal pain, and unintentional weight loss. She is functionally robust: fully independent in ADLs/IADLs and walks ~2 miles daily. PMH notable for well-controlled essential hypertension and osteopenia. Medications confirmed: losartan 50 mg every morning and calcium carbonate/vitamin D with dinner. No known drug allergies. Denies antiplatelet or anticoagulant use.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 68, RR 14, BP 130/74, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 64 kg (141 lb), BMI 24.1. Recent labs (2026-06-18): hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL (no anemia), platelets 244 x10^3/uL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >60). No laboratory evidence of occult GI blood loss.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- First-time average-risk colorectal cancer screening in a 76-year-old. Per USPSTF, routine screening in adults 76-85 is a Grade C recommendation — individualized rather than automatic. Given her excellent functional status, estimated life expectancy well beyond 10 years, and complete absence of prior screening, the expected benefit favors screening. Shared decision-making performed; risks of prep and sedation reviewed; patient understands and elects to proceed.
- Plan screening colonoscopy; standard bowel prep. No anticoagulation/antiplatelet agents to hold. Anesthesia/sedation risk low given robust status and normal renal function.
- Hypertension — continue losartan 50 mg daily; hold morning dose per pre-procedure instructions on prep/procedure day as directed.
- Osteopenia — continue calcium/vitamin D.
- No medication or allergy discrepancies identified; medication list and nil-known-allergy status confirmed with patient.
- Team to schedule procedure and send prep instructions with clear-liquid-diet and medication hold guidance.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your first colon cancer screening (colonoscopy)
• How this is an individual decision at your age, and why screening still makes sense for you
• Your blood pressure and bone-health medicines
• Your recent bloodwork, which was normal

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy; the team will send bowel-prep instructions and tell you which pills to take or hold on prep and procedure days.
• Keep taking your losartan each morning and your calcium/vitamin D with dinner, except where the prep instructions say otherwise.
• You have no known medication allergies and take no blood thinners, which keeps the procedure risk low.
• Call the office if you develop any rectal bleeding, belly pain, or a change in your bowel habits before the procedure.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Suleiman — thanks for coming in. You're here so we can set up your first screening colonoscopy. How are you feeling? Any stomach or bowel issues at all?
MA: Before the doctor goes on, let me get you roomed — you're 5 foot 10, 181 pounds, blood pressure 122 over 78, heart rate 72, temp 98.2, respirations 14, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: No, I feel fine, honestly. No pain, no bleeding, nothing like that. My doctor just said at fifty I should get it done.
DR: That's right — average-risk screening, exactly on schedule. Let me confirm a few things. Any family history of colon cancer, or anyone with polyps?
PT: No, none. My father had diabetes, the type 2, but no cancer that I know of. Nobody with colon problems.
DR: Good. And you take no medications — nothing for blood pressure, no aspirin, no blood thinners, no supplements or herbal things?
PT: Nothing at all. I don't even take vitamins.
DR: Perfect, that makes the prep simpler — nothing to hold. Any allergies to medications?
PT: No, none that I know of.
DR: Great. So the main thing today is planning. Was there something about the timing you wanted to ask about?
PT: Yes — I fast on Mondays and Thursdays, for religious reasons. And I wasn't sure how that works with the prep, because I know you can't eat, and there's the liquid you drink. I don't want to schedule it on a fasting day and then have a problem.
DR: That's a really good thing to raise. The prep itself is a big volume of liquid, and clear liquids the day before — so we'll pick a procedure day that isn't one of your fasting days, and I'll have the team walk you through exactly when to start and finish the prep so it fits around everything.
PT: That would help a lot. One more thing — please call me. I got a message on that online portal thing but I never check it. My phone is best.
DR: Noted — we'll do everything by phone. No portal. The scheduler will call you with the date and the prep instructions, and I'll flag your chart so it's phone contact only.
PT: Thank you. That's all my questions.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Suleiman is a 50-year-old man presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consultation to plan his first, average-risk screening colonoscopy. He is asymptomatic — denies rectal bleeding, hematochezia, melena, change in bowel habits, abdominal pain, or unintentional weight loss. No prior colonoscopy. He confirms no family history of colorectal cancer or polyps (father with type 2 diabetes only). He takes no prescription medications, no over-the-counter medications, no aspirin/antiplatelet or anticoagulant agents, and no supplements. No known drug allergies. He raised two logistical points: he observes voluntary weekly religious fasts on Mondays and Thursdays and asked how the clear-liquid diet and split-dose bowel prep should be timed around them; and he does not use the patient portal and requests phone contact for all scheduling and instructions.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 82 kg (181 lb), BMI 25.9. Recent labs (2026-06-25): hemoglobin 15.1 g/dL, hematocrit 44.6% — within normal limits, no evidence of anemia. Well-appearing, no distress.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Average-risk colorectal cancer screening in an asymptomatic 50-year-old — proceed with screening colonoscopy per guideline-based indication.
- No medications to hold; no anticoagulation/antiplatelet or anesthesia-clearance concerns identified.
- No allergies; no family-history modifiers — standard screening interval applies pending findings.
- Scheduling logistics: schedule the procedure on a non-fasting day (avoid Mondays/Thursdays); coordinate split-dose prep and clear-liquid timing around the patient's fasting practice.
- Communication preference: phone contact only — patient is a portal non-responder; route scheduling and prep instructions by phone and flag chart accordingly.
- Bowel prep instructions and return precautions to be provided by phone.
after_visit_summary:

• You do not need to stop or hold any medications, because you take none.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Doris. You're here so we can plan your surveillance colonoscopy — you're due from the polyps we found back in 2021. How have you been feeling?
MA: Before you got started — I roomed her and took vitals: height 5 foot 4, weight 146 pounds, blood pressure 122 over 76, heart rate 62, temp 36.8, respirations 14, oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Pretty well, thank you. No stomach troubles, no bleeding, bowels are regular. I just want to get the scope done and over with.
DR: That's what I like to hear. Before we schedule, I need to sort something out. The referral note that came over says 'no anticoagulation' — no blood thinner. But when I look at your pharmacy records, they show you filling warfarin. Can you help me understand which is right?
PT: Oh — no, I definitely take warfarin. Five milligrams, every night with dinner. I've been on it for years for my heart, the atrial fibrillation.
DR: Okay, that's very important. So the referral is wrong — you ARE on a blood thinner.
PT: Yes. The anticoagulation clinic manages it. I was just there two weeks ago for my INR check, they said the number was fine, in range.
NURSE: I have that here — INR 2.6 on the third, that's therapeutic.
DR: Perfect. And you're also on metoprolol for the heart rate?
PT: That's right, fifty milligrams, morning and night.
DR: Good. This changes how we prep for the colonoscopy. Because you're on warfarin and your stroke risk is on the higher side, we can't just stop it casually — we'll coordinate with your anticoagulation clinic on exactly when to hold it and whether you need bridging, and we'll recheck your INR close to the procedure date.
PT: So I shouldn't just quit it on my own?
DR: Correct — please don't stop anything until we give you a written plan. I'll flag the referral so nobody schedules you thinking you're off blood thinners. Any allergies to medications?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: Great. I'll get the anticoagulation plan set up, then we'll book you.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Kowalski is a 68-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic to plan surveillance colonoscopy (tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia on 2021 colonoscopy, 5-year interval due 2026). She is asymptomatic — no hematochezia, melena, change in bowel habits, or abdominal pain. She has long-standing atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VASc 4) and confirms she takes warfarin 5 mg every evening, managed by the anticoagulation clinic, with an INR check ~2 weeks ago reported in range. She also takes metoprolol 50 mg BID for rate control. No known drug allergies.

**Chart reconciliation:** The referral narrative states 'no anticoagulation,' which directly contradicts the medication reconciliation and pharmacy fill history (active warfarin 5 mg daily) AND the patient's own account. Patient's spoken history is the ground truth: she IS anticoagulated. The referral statement is erroneous and must not be used to protocol the procedure.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 62, RR 14, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 66 kg (146 lb), BMI 24.8. Labs 2026-07-03: INR 2.6 (therapeutic for AF target 2.0-3.0), hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, platelets 232 x10^3/uL. Consistent with active, therapeutic warfarin and no anemia.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Surveillance colonoscopy indicated (prior tubular adenomas, LGD; due 2026). Asymptomatic.
- Atrial fibrillation on warfarin, CHA2DS2-VASc 4 — HIGH thromboembolic risk. Referral 'no anticoagulation' is INCORRECT; warfarin confirmed active by pharmacy and patient.
- Peri-procedure anticoagulation: DO NOT protocol until managed. Coordinate hold timing (and bridging assessment) with anticoagulation clinic; recheck INR within 24-72 h of procedure; plan same-day resumption vs. bridging per bleeding/thrombosis risk. Counsel patient not to self-discontinue.
- Continue metoprolol 50 mg BID.
- Correct medication reconciliation source of truth: warfarin ACTIVE; flag referral error in chart.
- Provide written prep + anticoagulation hold instructions before scheduling; then book colonoscopy.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Planning your follow-up (surveillance) colonoscopy — you're due from the 2021 polyps
• Your blood thinner (warfarin) for atrial fibrillation
• How we will safely manage your medicines around the procedure

Important correction to your record
• The referral said you were NOT on a blood thinner. That was incorrect. You confirmed you take warfarin 5 mg every evening, and your pharmacy records agree. We corrected your chart.

Next steps
• Do NOT stop your warfarin or any medicine on your own. We will send you a written plan for exactly when to hold it before the colonoscopy.
• We will coordinate with your anticoagulation clinic and check your INR close to the procedure date.
• Keep taking your metoprolol as usual.
• Once the blood-thinner plan is set, our team will schedule your colonoscopy and send prep instructions.
• Call us for any rectal bleeding, black stools, lightheadedness, or a fast/irregular heartbeat.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. DiMarco. I'm the gastroenterologist — we're meeting today to plan your colonoscopy. Your stool test, the FIT, came back positive, so we do a scope to look for the cause. How are you feeling overall?
MA: Before the doctor goes on — I got your vitals: you're 5 foot 9 and 185 pounds today, temperature 98.1, heart rate 72, respirations 14, blood pressure 128 over 78, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Honestly, fine. That's the strange part. No pain, no bleeding, nothing I've noticed. My doctor just said the test was positive and I should come see you.
DR: That's actually the most common situation — a positive FIT with no symptoms. Let me ask specifically: any blood in the stool, any dark or black stools?
PT: No, none of that.
DR: Weight loss, belly pain, change in your bowel habits?
PT: No. Everything's normal there.
DR: Good. Now, the chart has something I want to check — it lists a C. diff infection for you. Have you had diarrhea or been treated for that?
PT: Oh — no, that's my wife. She had the C. diff and just finished her antibiotics. I never had it. I feel completely fine, no diarrhea at all. Someone must have mixed that up.
DR: Thank you for clarifying — that's important, I'll correct it. It's you we're scoping, and you've had no infection. Let me go through your medications. I have rosuvastatin, the cholesterol pill.
PT: Yes, I take that every night.
DR: Anything else, including anything over the counter?
PT: Just a baby aspirin. I take one every day for my heart — I buy it myself, the little 81 milligram one.
DR: That's good to know — it's not in your chart, so I'll add it. Aspirin can thin the blood a little, which matters if we remove a polyp. We'll decide whether to hold it. Any heart history I should know — a heart attack, any stents?
PT: No heart attack, no stents. I have the coronary artery disease but it's managed. I had a stress test last year and they said it was normal.
DR: Perfect, that helps with clearing you for sedation. So: routine colonoscopy for the positive FIT, no symptoms, and we'll sort out the aspirin. The team will send prep instructions.
PT: Sounds good.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. DiMarco is a 63-year-old man referred to GI clinic for a diagnostic colonoscopy after a positive screening FIT (2026-06-15). He is asymptomatic — denies hematochezia, melena, abdominal pain, weight loss, or change in bowel habits. On medication reconciliation he reports taking rosuvastatin 20 mg nightly (confirmed) and, in addition, OTC low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily for cardiac reasons that is not documented in the chart. The chart carries an active Clostridioides difficile infection; the patient clarifies this belongs to his WIFE (recently treated), and he himself has had no diarrhea and was never diagnosed. Cardiac history: CAD, medically managed, with a normal exercise stress test in 2025; he denies any prior MI and denies coronary stents.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 128/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 84 kg (185 lb), BMI 27.4. FIT positive (2026-06-15). Hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL, platelets 244 x10^3/uL — normal; no anemia. Well-appearing, no acute distress.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Positive FIT, asymptomatic — proceed with diagnostic colonoscopy, routine priority per FIT-positive protocol.
- Correct problem list: remove/reclassify Clostridioides difficile infection — mis-attributed household exposure (wife treated); patient asymptomatic, no diarrhea. No stool testing or isolation indicated.
- Add medication: OTC aspirin 81 mg daily (previously undocumented). Given planned possible polypectomy, will advise on periprocedural hold; low cardiac risk supports brief hold if needed.
- CAD — stable, no prior MI, no stents, normal stress test 2025; acceptable for sedation/anesthesia clearance.
- Hyperlipidemia — continue rosuvastatin 20 mg nightly.
- Bowel prep instructions and anticoagulation/antiplatelet hold guidance to follow; return precautions for GI bleeding.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your stool test (FIT) was positive, so we will do a colonoscopy to find the cause
• You feel well with no symptoms
• Your heart history and medications

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy (routine). The team will send bowel prep instructions and tell you what to stop beforehand.
• We updated your record: the C. diff infection listed under your name was actually your wife's and has been corrected; we added your daily 81 mg aspirin.
• We will let you know whether to pause your aspirin before the procedure.
• Keep taking your rosuvastatin each night.
• Call for any rectal bleeding, black stools, belly pain, or lightheadedness.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Brooks. Your primary doctor referred you for an upper endoscopy because the reflux hasn't settled down. I want to go over everything before we book it. Tell me how it's been.
MA: Before I step out — I got your vitals: height 5 foot 5, weight 205 pounds, temp 36.8, heart rate 76, respirations 14, blood pressure 126 over 80, and oxygen 97 percent on room air.
PT: The heartburn is still there most days. Burning in my chest, and sometimes stuff comes back up, especially at night or if I eat late.
DR: Any trouble swallowing, food sticking, or pain when you swallow?
PT: No, nothing like that. Swallowing's fine.
DR: Losing any weight without trying?
PT: No, I wish. My weight's been steady.
DR: And any black or tarry stools, or blood?
PT: No, nothing like that.
DR: Good, that's reassuring. Let me look at your medicines. The chart has you on pantoprazole forty twice a day. How are you actually taking it?
PT: I take one in the morning. Honestly I forget the evening one most days, so it's really just once a day. And I usually take it with my breakfast, or right after.
DR: That's really helpful to know. It works best about half an hour to an hour before you eat, and it's meant to be twice a day. There's a decent chance the reflux hasn't been fully treated rather than not treatable. What about the famotidine at bedtime?
PT: I stopped that maybe two months ago. It wasn't doing anything, so I quit it.
DR: Okay, I'll take it off the list. Do you take anything for pain, headaches, anything over the counter?
PT: Just ibuprofen. I get headaches a lot, so I take a couple two hundreds, maybe three or four days a week.
DR: That's important. Ibuprofen can irritate the stomach and esophagus, so that could be adding to this. Any drug allergies?
PT: A sulfa antibiotic gave me a rash once. I don't think it's written down.
DR: I'll add that. Here's my thinking: let's still do the endoscopy to look for erosions or any Barrett's changes, since you're due for a proper look, but we'll also fix the medication timing and cut back the ibuprofen.
PT: That makes sense. I didn't realize I was taking it wrong.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Brooks is a 44-year-old woman referred for pre-procedure evaluation for esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) for GERD labeled refractory to BID PPI x4 months; indication is to rule out erosive disease/Barrett's esophagus. She reports persistent daily heartburn and regurgitation, worse at night and with late meals. She denies dysphagia, odynophagia, unintentional weight loss, and melena. On medication review, she reports she takes pantoprazole only once daily (morning) and typically with or after food rather than before meals, and frequently misses the prescribed second daily dose. She stopped famotidine approximately 2 months ago because it was ineffective. She reports regular OTC ibuprofen use (200 mg, 2-3 tablets several days per week) for chronic headaches. She reports a prior rash to a sulfa antibiotic, not previously documented.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 76, RR 14, BP 126/80, SpO2 97% on RA; Ht 165 cm (5'5"), Wt 92.8 kg (205 lb), BMI 34.1. Recent labs (2026-07-02): hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL (normal); H. pylori stool antigen negative.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- GERD labeled refractory but with suboptimal PPI use (once-daily, not pre-meal) plus regular NSAID use — reflux is likely undertreated rather than truly PPI-refractory.
- Optimize therapy: pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily taken 30-60 minutes before breakfast and dinner; reinforce adherence and lifestyle measures (weight loss, avoid late/large meals, elevate head of bed).
- Reduce/avoid ibuprofen; recommend acetaminophen for headaches and evaluate headache pattern with PCP.
- Proceed with EGD as planned to evaluate for erosive esophagitis and Barrett's given persistent symptoms; elective/routine (no alarm features).
- Update medication list: famotidine discontinued per patient; document NSAID (ibuprofen) use.
- Add allergy: sulfa antibiotic (rash).
- Anesthesia/sedation: no anticoagulants or antiplatelets reported; standard pre-procedure instructions to follow.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Ongoing heartburn and reflux
• How and when you take your reflux medicine
• An upper endoscopy (EGD) to look at your esophagus and stomach
• Pain medicine (ibuprofen) and its effect on reflux

Next steps
• We will schedule your upper endoscopy (EGD); the team will send preparation instructions and what to stop before the procedure.
• Take pantoprazole twice a day, 30-60 minutes before breakfast and dinner — the timing matters.
• Try to avoid ibuprofen; use acetaminophen for headaches instead and follow up with your primary doctor about the headaches.
• We updated your record: famotidine marked stopped; sulfa antibiotic allergy (rash) added; ibuprofen use noted.
• Keep working on lifestyle steps: weight loss, avoiding late or large meals, and raising the head of your bed.
• Call for trouble swallowing, vomiting blood, black stools, or unintended weight loss.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Osei — thanks for coming in. Your doctor sent you over so we can plan an upper endoscopy for the swallowing trouble. Tell me what's been going on.
MA: Before you dig in, doctor — I roomed him and took his vitals. Height's five foot ten, weight 160 pounds, temp 98.2, blood pressure 126 over 78, heart rate 78, respirations 14, and oxygen 97 percent on room air.
PT: It's the swallowing, doc. For a couple months now, when I eat solid food it feels like it sticks right here, in the middle of my chest. Bread, meat especially.
DR: Is it getting worse, or about the same?
PT: Worse. At first it was once in a while. Now I keep a glass of water at the table to push it down. Soft stuff and liquids are fine.
DR: Any pain when you swallow? Any vomiting blood, or black stools?
PT: No, no pain, none of that. But I've dropped weight. My pants are loose — I'd say fifteen pounds since spring.
DR: Fifteen pounds — the note I have said about five, so that's more than we had written down. That's important. Let me check your medicines. It shows none on file. Anything at all, even over the counter?
PT: I take a blood thinner.
DR: Okay — which one? Do you have the name?
PT: The fish oil. The omega-3 capsules. My neighbor said it thins the blood, keeps things flowing.
DR: Ah — fish oil isn't actually a blood thinner in the way we worry about for a procedure, so that's good to clear up. Anything else, any aspirin?
PT: Oh — yeah, a baby aspirin every morning. I don't really count that as medicine, I just take it for my heart.
DR: That one I do need to know about — we may hold it before the scope since we might take biopsies. Now, drinking — the chart says about four a day?
PT: Something like that. A few beers.
FAMILY: Charles, come on. It's more like six or eight, and weekends it's more.
PT: ...Yeah. Okay, more than four.
DR: Thank you both — that helps me plan the sedation safely. And you're still smoking?
PT: Yeah, about a pack a day, forty years now.
DR: Understood. Given the swallowing getting worse with the weight loss, we're going to move quickly on the endoscopy. My team will call with the prep and what to hold.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Osei is a 59-year-old man referred to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan expedited EGD. He reports approximately 2 months of progressive solid-food dysphagia, with a sensation of food sticking mid-sternum; symptoms have worsened such that he now needs to drink water to help push solids down. Liquids and soft foods are tolerated. He denies odynophagia, hematemesis, and melena. He reports unintentional weight loss of ~15 lb over this period (chart documented 5 lb). Medication reconciliation: chart lists no medications; on interview he takes OTC aspirin 81 mg daily (which he did not consider a medication) and fish oil / omega-3 supplement, which he described as 'a blood thinner' — clarified to be a supplement, not a true anticoagulant. Social history: active smoker, ~1 pack/day for ~40 years; alcohol intake charted at ~4 drinks/day but collateral history from spouse indicates ~6-8 drinks/day, more on weekends.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 78, RR 14, BP 126/78, SpO2 97% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 72.5 kg (160 lb), BMI 22.9 (prior Wt ~79.3 kg / 175 lb, consistent with reported loss). No hematemesis or melena reported. Alarm features present: progressive solid-food dysphagia, significant weight loss, and tobacco/alcohol risk factors.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Progressive solid-food dysphagia with weight loss in a patient with heavy tobacco and alcohol use — concerning for an obstructing esophageal lesion (malignancy must be excluded). Proceed with EXPEDITED EGD with biopsy.
- Medication reconciliation: add OTC aspirin 81 mg daily to the record (not previously listed); plan to hold aspirin periprocedurally per bleeding-risk protocol given anticipated biopsies. Fish oil is a supplement, NOT an anticoagulant — no procedural delay warranted on that basis.
- Alcohol use greater than charted (~6-8 drinks/day per collateral) — flag for anesthesia/sedation planning and monitor for withdrawal risk; counsel on reduction; consider esophageal varices in differential.
- Update social history: alcohol intake ~6-8/day; active 1 ppd smoker (~40 pack-years by history).
- Pre-procedure: labs (CBC, coagulation) and anesthesia coordination; team to provide bowel/NPO prep and medication-hold instructions.
- Return precautions: inability to swallow liquids/saliva, chest pain, vomiting blood, or black stools.
after_visit_summary:

What we discussed

• Weight loss

Next steps
• We will schedule your upper endoscopy (EGD) soon; the team will call with instructions on when to stop eating and which medicines to hold.
• We added your daily baby aspirin to your record — we will likely ask you to stop it for a few days before the test. We will tell you exactly when.
• Your fish oil is a supplement, not a true blood thinner, so it does not delay your procedure.
• Cutting back on alcohol and stopping smoking will help — we can help you with this.
• Call right away if you cannot swallow liquids or your own saliva, if you have chest pain, or if you vomit blood or have black stools.

transcript:
DR: Hi Nancy, good to meet you. You're here so we can plan the upper endoscopy — and stretch the esophagus again if it's narrowed. Tell me what's been happening.
MA: Before we get started, I got your vitals — you're 5 foot 3, 120 pounds, blood pressure 148 over 86, temp 36.7, heart rate 78, breathing 14, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Well, the swallowing trouble came back. Meat and bread, mostly — it feels like it hangs up right here and I have to wash it down. It's been maybe six, eight weeks. Same as before the last time you all fixed it.
DR: Okay, that's the stricture acting up again. Any weight loss, or food coming back up?
PT: A little — I've been picking softer foods. No throwing up.
DR: Got it. Now the important part before we scope you: your medicines, because a dilation can bleed. The chart has you on apixaban, five milligrams twice a day. Is that right?
PT: No, they changed that. My heart doctor cut it down — I'm on the two-point-five now, the smaller one, twice a day. He said my kidneys and my weight, so he lowered it. That was, oh, three months ago.
DR: That's really important, thank you — two-point-five twice daily. And when did you last take a dose?
PT: This morning, with breakfast. I always take it with breakfast.
DR: Perfect, that tells me what I need. Do you know your recent kidney numbers — any bloodwork lately?
PT: Not since last year, I think. Nobody's checked since.
DR: We'll draw that before we schedule — the kidney function decides how long you hold the blood thinner. Anything else you take that I might not have?
PT: I take a baby aspirin every day. I started that myself, for my heart. And the amlodipine, five, for my blood pressure.
DR: Good to know — the aspirin matters for bleeding too, so we'll sort that out with your cardiologist. And you're still on the omeprazole?
PT: Every morning, yes. That one helps.
DR: Great. So: get current kidney labs, coordinate holding the apixaban and aspirin with cardiology, then EGD with dilation. My team will call with the exact stop dates and prep.
PT: Thank you. I just want to swallow normally again.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Whitehorse is a 66-year-old woman presenting for pre-procedure consultation for EGD with possible esophageal dilation. She has a known distal peptic esophageal stricture, last dilated to 15 mm in May 2025 with good relief. She reports recurrent solid-food dysphagia over the past 6–8 weeks (meats and breads "hanging up," washing food down, shifting to softer foods), similar in character to her prior episodes, with mild self-limited dietary change and no vomiting or significant weight loss.

Medication reconciliation performed. She reports her apixaban was reduced by cardiology from 5 mg BID to 2.5 mg BID approximately 3 months ago, attributed to low body weight and reduced renal function — the chart still lists 5 mg BID (including a legacy duplicate order). She took her most recent apixaban dose the morning of this visit. She also reports daily aspirin 81 mg (self-started, for cardiac reasons) and amlodipine 5 mg daily for hypertension, neither of which is on the chart medication list. She confirms omeprazole 40 mg every morning.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 78, RR 14, BP 148/86, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 160 cm (5'3"), Wt 54.5 kg (120 lb), BMI 21.3. Most recent renal labs are stale (2025-06-03): creatinine 1.5 mg/dL, eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m2; no current CrCl on file. Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL (2025-06). Prior EGD (2025-05-14): distal peptic stricture dilated to 15 mm, LA grade A esophagitis, benign biopsies.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Recurrent peptic esophageal stricture with dysphagia — plan EGD with esophageal dilation.
- Periprocedural anticoagulation (HIGHER-BLEED-RISK procedure):
  - Correct medication list: apixaban is 2.5 mg BID per patient/cardiology, NOT 5 mg BID; remove duplicate legacy order.
  - Obtain CURRENT renal function (creatinine/CrCl) before scheduling — hold interval is CrCl-dependent; on-file eGFR is >1 year old.
  - Hold plan keyed to last-dose date: last apixaban dose was the morning of 2026-07-18. Coordinate hold duration with cardiology once CrCl known.
  - Reconcile aspirin 81 mg daily (not previously charted); decide on periprocedural hold with cardiology given additive bleed risk.
- Reconcile amlodipine 5 mg daily (not previously charted); BP mildly elevated today.
- GERD — continue omeprazole 40 mg every morning.
- Nurse/scheduling to arrange labs, confirm anticoagulant/antiplatelet stop dates, and send bowel/EGD prep and NPO instructions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Swallowing trouble that has come back (narrowing in the esophagus)
• Plan for an upper endoscopy (EGD) with stretching if needed
• Your blood thinner (apixaban) and baby aspirin, and how to stop them safely before the procedure
• Blood pressure and reflux medicines

Next steps
• We will schedule your EGD with possible dilation; the team will call with the date and prep/eating instructions.
• You will get a blood test to check your kidney function BEFORE the procedure — this decides how long to stop the blood thinner.
• We corrected your record: apixaban is 2.5 mg twice daily (not 5 mg). We also added your daily aspirin 81 mg and amlodipine 5 mg.
• Do NOT stop your apixaban or aspirin on your own — wait for the exact stop dates we send after talking with your heart doctor.
• Keep taking your omeprazole each morning.
• Call us for trouble swallowing liquids or your own saliva, chest pain, vomiting blood, or black stools.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Fuentes. I'm one of the GI doctors — you were sent over because your blood work came back strongly pointing toward celiac disease, and we want to confirm it with a scope and a few small biopsies. Sound right?
MA: Before the doctor came in, I grabbed your vitals — height 5 foot 10, weight 176 pounds. Temp 36.8, heart rate 72, blood pressure 122 over 76, respirations 14, oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Yes, that's what my regular doctor told me. My tests were really high, she said.
DR: They were — the celiac antibody was more than ten times the normal limit, so that's a strong signal. The way we confirm it for sure is an upper endoscopy where we take tiny samples from the top of your small intestine. Before that, one really important thing: have you changed how you eat? Cut out bread, wheat, that kind of thing?
PT: No, no. She told me to keep eating normally until the test, so I did. I still have toast in the morning, pasta a couple nights a week. Nothing changed.
DR: That's exactly what we needed to hear. If you'd gone gluten-free already, the biopsy could look normal even if you have it, and we'd be stuck. So please keep eating gluten right up until the procedure.
PT: Okay, got it. I'll keep eating my bread.
DR: Perfect. Let me check your medicines. I have a multivitamin with iron — for the low iron, right?
PT: Yes, one every morning.
DR: Anything else? Any blood thinners? That matters because we sometimes take biopsies.
PT: Well — I do take a blood thinner. The fish oil ones? My cousin said they thin the blood.
DR: Ah — fish oil, the omega-3 capsules. That's actually not a true blood thinner, so it won't be a problem for the biopsies, and you don't need to stop it. But I'm glad you mentioned it — I'll add it to your list. No prescription blood thinners like warfarin or aspirin daily?
PT: No, nothing like that.
DR: Any allergies to medicines?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: Good. One more — any family history of celiac?
PT: Actually yes, my older sister has it. She had a biopsy years ago.
DR: That's helpful, thank you. So the plan: schedule the scope with biopsies, keep eating gluten, keep the vitamin. The team will send prep instructions.
PT: Sounds good.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Fuentes is a 38-year-old man referred to GI for a pre-procedure consult and diagnostic esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) with duodenal biopsy. Primary care evaluation for months of bloating, intermittent loose stools, and fatigue revealed markedly elevated tTG-IgA (>10x ULN) with normal total IgA, plus mild microcytic iron deficiency (Hgb 12.9, ferritin 18). He confirms he has continued a normal gluten-containing diet (daily toast, pasta several nights weekly) and has NOT started a gluten-free diet — essential for diagnostic yield of the biopsy. He takes a daily multivitamin with iron. On review of medications he reported a "blood thinner," which on clarification is over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3); he denies any prescription anticoagulant or antiplatelet (no warfarin, DOAC, or daily aspirin). No known drug allergies. He reports an older sister with biopsy-confirmed celiac disease.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 80 kg (176 lb), BMI 25.2. Labs (2026-06-20): tTG-IgA 152 U/mL (ref <15; >10x ULN), total IgA 214 mg/dL (normal, result interpretable), hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL (mildly low, microcytic), ferritin 18 ug/L (low). Well-appearing, no acute distress.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Serology-suspected celiac disease (tTG-IgA >10x ULN, IgA-sufficient), histologically unconfirmed — proceed with diagnostic EGD and duodenal biopsies for confirmation. Update problem list to reflect suspected/provisional status pending pathology.
- Continue gluten-containing diet until after the procedure — reinforced with patient; do not initiate gluten-free eating pre-biopsy.
- Periprocedural anticoagulation: no true anticoagulant/antiplatelet. "Blood thinner" clarified as OTC fish oil (omega-3) — no hold required; add omega-3 to medication list.
- Iron deficiency (Hgb 12.9, ferritin 18) — continue multivitamin with iron; reassess iron studies after diagnosis; likely secondary to malabsorption if celiac confirmed.
- New family history: sister with biopsy-confirmed celiac — add to record.
- Schedule EGD with biopsy; bowel/pre-procedure and fasting instructions to follow.
