transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Lindqvist — thanks for coming in. And you brought your daughter, good. We're here to plan the upper endoscopy, the camera test to check the veins in your esophagus. How have you been feeling?
MA: Before the doctor started — I got your vitals: height 5 foot 4, weight 187 pounds, temperature 98.2, heart rate 78, respirations 16, blood pressure 124 over 76, and oxygen 95 percent on room air.
PT: Tired, but okay. The belly's been steady, not swelling up like before.
DR: That's good. The referral says you've never had any bleeding — no vomiting blood, no black stools. Is that right?
PT: No vomiting blood, never. Although... there was one time a couple weeks ago, the stool was real dark, almost black. I figured it was something I ate and didn't think much of it.
DR: I'm glad you mentioned it — that's exactly the kind of thing we look for. We'll keep that in mind. Let me go over your medicines. The chart has spironolactone 100 milligrams and furosemide 40. Still taking both?
PT: The water pill, the furosemide, yes, every morning. The other one — the spironolactone — I cut it in half a few weeks ago. It was making my chest sore and I felt lightheaded standing up. So I've been taking 50.
DR: Okay, that's important, we'll note the actual dose. And the rifaximin?
FAMILY: She takes that twice a day. That started after she got confused back in May — she didn't know what day it was, kind of out of it for an evening. The liver doctor put her on it.
DR: Thank you, that helps me understand why it's there. Any blood thinners, aspirin, anything like that?
PT: Just my blood thinner — the fish oil. My daughter buys me the omega-3 capsules, says it thins the blood.
DR: Ah — fish oil, the over-the-counter kind. That's not a prescription blood thinner, so that's good to clarify. You're not on warfarin or anything like that?
PT: No, nothing like that.
DR: And any alcohol?
PT: No, never really drank.
FAMILY: One more thing — last time she had anesthesia, for her gallbladder years ago, she was awfully sick after and took forever to wake up.
DR: Very useful — I'll flag that for the anesthesia team. So we'll get the endoscopy scheduled, review your blood counts and clotting beforehand, and I'll pass all of this along. The team will send prep instructions.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Lindqvist is a 72-year-old woman with newly diagnosed NASH cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B), complicated by ascites (controlled) and thrombocytopenia, presenting to GI clinic for pre-procedure consultation prior to EGD for variceal screening. She denies hematemesis. However, she reports one episode of dark, tarry stool approximately 2 weeks ago that she dismissed at the time — possible prior upper GI/variceal bleed, contrary to the 'no prior bleed' referral. Ascites has been stable without increasing girth. She reports self-reducing spironolactone from 100 mg to 50 mg daily several weeks ago due to breast tenderness and orthostatic lightheadedness; she continues furosemide 40 mg every morning. Family reports an episode of acute confusion/disorientation in May that prompted initiation of rifaximin, consistent with hepatic encephalopathy not currently on the problem list. She takes OTC fish oil (omega-3), which she refers to as a 'blood thinner'; she is on no prescription anticoagulant or antiplatelet. She denies alcohol use, consistent with NASH etiology. Daughter reports prior severe postoperative nausea and prolonged emergence after anesthesia (cholecystectomy).

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 78, RR 16, BP 124/76, SpO2 95% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 85 kg (187 lb), BMI 32.0. Recent labs (2026-07-06): platelets 88 x10^3/uL (low), INR 1.4 (elevated, consistent with hepatic synthetic dysfunction — not anticoagulation), total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL, albumin 2.9 g/dL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL. Findings consistent with Child-Pugh B cirrhosis. Liver biopsy (2026-04-28): steatohepatitis with cirrhosis.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- NASH cirrhosis, Child-Pugh B — proceed with EGD for variceal screening per AASLD.
- Possible prior UGI bleed (single melenic stool ~2 weeks ago) — raises pre-test probability of varices; will heighten vigilance and expedite scheduling; counsel on urgent return for recurrent black stools or hematemesis.
- Coagulation/hematology for procedure: thrombocytopenia (88k) and INR 1.4 reflect cirrhosis, NOT anticoagulation. No prescription anticoagulant/antiplatelet. OTC fish oil documented — does not require holding for anticoagulation. Anesthesia review indicated given Child-Pugh B, thrombocytopenia, prior adverse anesthesia reaction, and possible encephalopathy.
- Update medication list: spironolactone actual dose 50 mg daily (patient self-reduced) — reassess ascites control and electrolytes/volume; furosemide 40 mg confirmed.
- Add problem: hepatic encephalopathy (episode May 2026) — explains rifaximin; relevant to sedation planning.
- Flag anesthesia: history of severe PONV and delayed emergence.
- Continue rifaximin 550 mg BID.
- EGD scheduling + pre-procedure labs and prep instructions to follow.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Planning your upper endoscopy (EGD) to check the veins in your esophagus
• A dark/black stool you had about two weeks ago
• Your water pills and the spironolactone dose
• The rifaximin and the confusion episode in the spring
• Getting the anesthesia team to review your case

Next steps
• We will schedule your EGD; the team will send prep instructions and tell you what to stop beforehand.
• We updated your record: spironolactone marked as 50 mg (the dose you're actually taking), and we noted your fish oil is over-the-counter, not a prescription blood thinner.
• We added a note about the confusion episode and your past reaction to anesthesia so the sedation team can plan safely.
• Keep taking your furosemide each morning and the rifaximin twice a day.
• Call right away for black or tarry stools, vomiting blood, lightheadedness, or a swelling belly.

transcript:
DR: Hi Tommy, thanks for coming in. You were referred after that episode where food got stuck last month. Walk me through what happened.
MA: Before the doctor started — let me get you roomed. You're 5 foot 10, 172 pounds. Temp 98.1, heart rate 72, respirations 14, blood pressure 122 over 76, oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Yeah — I was eating steak and a piece just would not go down. It was stuck in my chest, I couldn't even swallow my own spit. I spat into a cup for like three hours before I gave up and went to the ER.
DR: The ER — okay. The referral I got made it sound like it just passed on its own.
PT: I mean it passed eventually, but they had to give me something in the IV, glucagon I think, and they were talking about putting a scope in to pull it out. It cleared right before that. And honestly it almost happened again last week with chicken — I caught it in time, drank a bunch of water.
DR: That's really important, thank you. So this isn't a one-time thing. Has swallowing always been a little off?
PT: My whole life, yeah. Solids get stuck. I eat slow, cut everything small, always have water with meals. I just thought that was normal.
DR: That fits with what we're checking for. Let me go through your medicines. I have cetirizine as needed?
PT: I actually take that every day right now, it's allergy season.
DR: Good to know, I'll fix that. Anything else — even over-the-counter?
PT: Oh — I started omeprazole, the drugstore one, about three weeks ago. My chest was burning so I figured heartburn.
DR: That one matters a lot for us. The medication can quiet down the exact cells we're trying to sample on the biopsy, so we need to talk about the timing before the scope.
PT: Got it. I can stop it if you want.
DR: We'll decide together. Any allergies?
PT: Peanuts — my mouth gets itchy, lips tingle. Nothing in my chart? I've got asthma and eczema too.
DR: I'll add the peanuts. So the plan is an upper endoscopy with biopsies, and given the impactions we won't wait long. The team will send prep instructions.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Nguyen is a 29-year-old man presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult for EGD after an esophageal food impaction. History clarified today: the index event (2026-06-14) involved a steak bolus lodged for ~3 hours with inability to manage secretions, requiring an ED visit and IV glucagon, with endoscopic disimpaction nearly performed before spontaneous passage — this was more severe than the 'self-resolved' description on the referral. He reports a second near-impaction with chicken last week, aborted by drinking water. He describes lifelong intermittent solid-food dysphagia with adaptive behaviors (eating slowly, cutting food small, drinking water with meals). Strongly atopic: asthma, childhood eczema, seasonal allergic rhinitis. He reports taking cetirizine 10 mg DAILY through allergy season (charted as PRN). He self-started OTC omeprazole 20 mg daily ~3 weeks ago for burning chest discomfort. He reports peanut allergy (oral pruritus, lip tingling) not previously documented.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 78 kg (172 lb), BMI 24.6. Labs (2026-07-06): absolute eosinophil count 0.62 x10^3/uL (mildly elevated), total IgE 288 kU/L (elevated), hemoglobin 15.1 g/dL (normal). Well-appearing, no acute distress, tolerating secretions.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Recurrent esophageal food (bolus) impaction with lifelong solid-food dysphagia in a highly atopic young man — suspected eosinophilic esophagitis; diagnosis NOT yet confirmed, pending histology.
- Plan EGD with esophageal biopsies per EoE protocol (proximal and distal, >=6 samples). Given two impaction events including one ED presentation, schedule semi-urgently — do not defer.
- OTC omeprazole started ~3 weeks ago: PPI can suppress esophageal eosinophilia and confound biopsy interpretation. Discussed timing; will coordinate whether to hold PPI to characterize baseline eosinophilia vs. continue, and document PPI exposure for the pathologist.
- Medication reconciliation: cetirizine is daily (not PRN); update frequency.
- Add allergy: peanut (oral allergy symptoms).
- Atopy (asthma, eczema, allergic rhinitis) confirmed; continue albuterol PRN.
- Bowel/EGD prep and hold instructions to follow; return precautions for recurrent impaction or inability to swallow secretions (present to ED).
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Food getting stuck in your esophagus (including the ER visit) and lifelong trouble swallowing solids
• Planning an upper endoscopy (EGD) with biopsies to look for eosinophilic esophagitis
• Your allergies and daily allergy medicine
• The over-the-counter omeprazole you started

Next steps
• We will schedule your upper endoscopy soon — because food has gotten stuck more than once, we do not want to wait long. The team will send prep and instructions on what to stop before.
• Do not change or stop the omeprazole on your own yet — we will tell you exactly when to hold it, because it can affect the biopsy results.
• We updated your record: cetirizine marked as daily; peanut allergy (mouth itching, lip tingling) added.
• Until the scope, chew thoroughly, take small bites, and drink fluids with meals.
• Go to the ER if food gets stuck again and you cannot swallow your own saliva.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Vance — and you must be her daughter, thank you for coming in. We got the note from urgent care about yesterday. Can you tell me what happened?
MA: Before you get started, doctor — I roomed her and got her vitals: blood pressure 130 over 76, heart rate 74 and irregular, temp 36.8, breathing 16, oxygen 98 percent on room air. Height 5 foot 3, weight 137 pounds.
PT: I threw up once, in the afternoon. It looked odd — dark, sort of grainy. It scared my daughter more than me.
FAMILY: It really did look like coffee grounds. Just the one time, though. Nothing since, and her stools have been normal color — I've been checking.
DR: That's exactly what I need to know. No black or tarry stools, no more vomiting, no dizziness or feeling faint?
PT: No, I feel like myself today.
DR: Good. You're stable and your blood count only dipped a little, from 11.8 to 11.2. We do want to look inside with a camera — an EGD — to be safe. Now, the important part: are you on any blood thinners?
PT: No, no blood thinners. I don't take anything like that.
FAMILY: Mom, yes you do — the rivaroxaban, for your heart rhythm. She takes it every day, I give it to her with breakfast.
DR: Thank you — that's a critical one. And the chart has it as 20 milligrams. Is that still the dose?
FAMILY: No, that changed. The heart doctor dropped it to 15 a couple months ago, something about her kidneys. I don't think it got updated anywhere.
DR: I'm very glad you told me. Did she take a dose today?
FAMILY: Yes, this morning with breakfast, before we knew we'd be coming.
DR: Okay — that affects our timing. One more thing: any other pills, even over-the-counter?
FAMILY: Just her donepezil at night. Oh — and she's been taking ibuprofen for her knee, most days the last couple weeks.
DR: That's important too — ibuprofen can irritate the stomach and adds to bleeding risk with the rivaroxaban. Please stop that today. We'll hold the rivaroxaban, coordinate with cardiology, and get the scope scheduled once it's safe.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Eleanor Vance is an 84-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation on rivaroxaban, mild dementia (lives with and cared for by her daughter, who manages medications and provides collateral history), and CKD stage 3, presenting for urgent pre-procedure GI consultation after a single episode of coffee-ground emesis yesterday. The episode was self-limited; no recurrent vomiting, no melena, no lightheadedness or syncope, and she feels at baseline today. Urgent care documented hemodynamic stability with hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (baseline 11.8).

Medication reconciliation with the daughter revised the chart in several ways. The patient herself denied taking any blood thinner; her daughter confirmed she takes rivaroxaban daily. The chart lists rivaroxaban 20 mg daily, but cardiology reduced it to 15 mg daily approximately 2 months ago for declining renal function — not reflected in the record. The patient took her rivaroxaban this morning (dose not held). The daughter also reports the patient has been taking OTC ibuprofen most days for ~2 weeks for knee pain, not previously documented. Donepezil 10 mg nightly confirmed.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 74 (irregular), RR 16, BP 130/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 160 cm (5'3"), Wt 62 kg (137 lb), BMI 24.2. Hemodynamically stable, afebrile. Labs 2026-07-17: hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (baseline 11.8, 2026-05-08), hematocrit 34.6%, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, eGFR 44 mL/min/1.73m2. Alert, no acute distress.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Suspected upper GI bleed (single coffee-ground emesis, now resolved) in an 84-year-old on a DOAC — hemodynamically stable with only mild interval hemoglobin decrease. Urgent-tier EGD indicated.
- Anticoagulation: rivaroxaban dose is 15 mg daily per daughter (cardiology renal dose reduction), NOT the charted 20 mg — correct medication list. Last dose taken this morning; EGD cannot proceed same-day. Hold rivaroxaban and coordinate periprocedural timing/bridging decision with cardiology given afib and CKD.
- NSAID: stop OTC ibuprofen today — likely mucosal culprit and additive bleeding risk with DOAC; add to medication list, then reconcile as discontinued.
- Update medication list: rivaroxaban 15 mg daily (not 20 mg).
- Start PPI (pantoprazole) pending endoscopy.
- CKD stage 3 (eGFR 44) — relevant to DOAC dosing and bleeding risk; recheck CBC.
- Cognition: obtain history and consent through daughter; confirm donepezil 10 mg nightly.
- Return precautions reviewed with daughter: recurrent vomiting, black/tarry stools, lightheadedness — go to ED.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• A single episode of coffee-ground vomiting yesterday, now resolved
• Your blood count, which is only slightly lower and stable
• An upper endoscopy (camera test, 'EGD') to look for a cause
• A careful review of your medicines with your daughter

Next steps
• We will schedule your EGD on an urgent basis; the team will call with the date and the instructions.
• Do NOT take your rivaroxaban until we tell you it is safe — we are holding it and checking with your heart doctor first.
• Stop the over-the-counter ibuprofen starting today; it can irritate the stomach and add to bleeding risk.
• We corrected your record: your rivaroxaban is 15 mg daily (not 20 mg).
• Keep taking donepezil at night as usual.
• Start the stomach-protecting medicine (pantoprazole) as directed.
• Go to the emergency room right away for more vomiting, black or tarry stools, or feeling faint or dizzy.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Adebayo. We're meeting before scheduling an upper endoscopy so we can look at your stomach and take biopsies to figure out the best antibiotics for the H. pylori. Sound right?
MA: Before the doctor comes in, let me get your vitals. Height's 5 foot 10, weight 181 pounds. Blood pressure 122 over 78, heart rate 72, temp 36.8, and your oxygen's 98 percent. All looks good.
PT: Yes. The stool test kept coming back positive, so they said I need the camera test.
DR: Exactly — positive stool antigen, and we'll culture from the biopsy this time. Let me walk through the history. It says here you failed a course of treatment. Tell me what happened.
PT: Well — the first set of pills, I only made it a few days. Maybe four? They made me so nauseous I couldn't keep them down, so I stopped. I didn't really finish that one.
DR: That's important — so that first course wasn't completed, it wasn't that it didn't work. What about the second set, the four-drug course with the pink bismuth?
PT: That one I finished. All two weeks. I wrapped that up about three weeks ago now.
DR: Good, so you're not on any antibiotics right now?
PT: No, nothing. Just the pantoprazole every morning for my stomach.
DR: You're still taking the pantoprazole daily? Every day?
PT: Every day, yeah, it helps the burning.
DR: Okay — I'll want you to hold that for a couple of weeks before the scope, because it can hide the bacteria on the biopsy. We'll go over timing. Now, any drug allergies?
PT: Penicillin. Amoxicillin gave me an itchy rash years back. I don't think it's in your computer though.
DR: It isn't — thank you, I'll add it. That actually matters a lot for picking your next antibiotics. Any bleeding — blood in the stool, black tarry stools, vomiting blood?
PT: No, never. No bleeding, nothing like that.
DR: And no blood thinners — aspirin, fish oil, anything like that?
PT: No, none of that.
DR: Perfect. Let's get the EGD scheduled and I'll give you instructions on holding the pantoprazole.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Adebayo is a 47-year-old man referred to GI for a pre-procedure consult before EGD for treatment-refractory Helicobacter pylori infection, to obtain gastric biopsy with culture/susceptibility to guide therapy. He presents with a ~7-month history of dyspepsia (epigastric burning, early satiety), partially controlled on pantoprazole 40 mg daily. H. pylori stool antigen remains positive (2026-06-10). Regarding prior eradication: he clarifies the first (clarithromycin-based triple) course was stopped after ~4 days due to nausea and was never completed — i.e., non-adherence rather than a documented treatment failure. He subsequently completed a full 14-day bismuth quadruple therapy course approximately 3 weeks ago and is currently on no antibiotics. He reports an itchy rash reaction to penicillin/amoxicillin, not previously in the chart. He denies any GI bleeding, melena, or hematemesis at any time; no prior ulcer or ulcer complications. He is still taking pantoprazole daily. No anticoagulants, antiplatelets, or fish oil.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 82 kg (181 lb), BMI 25.9. Labs (2026-06-10): H. pylori stool antigen POSITIVE; hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL (normal). No alarm features. Hemodynamically stable per chart.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Refractory H. pylori infection with persistent dyspepsia — plan EGD with gastric biopsy and culture/susceptibility to guide next-line eradication.
- Reframe treatment history: first-line course incomplete (stopped early for nausea, non-adherence), bismuth quadruple therapy completed ~3 weeks ago. Confirmatory/eradication testing to await ≥4 weeks off antibiotics.
- HOLD pantoprazole ~2 weeks before EGD to avoid false-negative biopsy/culture; provide alternative symptom control (antacid/H2 as needed) during the hold.
- Add allergy: penicillin/amoxicillin (rash) — critical for salvage antibiotic selection; avoid amoxicillin-based regimens.
- Update medication list: clarithromycin and metronidazole courses completed/discontinued — not active.
- No GI bleeding or ulcer complications — no evidence to code a bleed; documented as absent and confirmed.
- Pre-procedure: no anticoagulation; low bleeding risk. Schedule EGD; provide prep and pantoprazole-hold instructions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Stomach infection (H. pylori) that has not cleared with the first treatments
• A planned upper endoscopy (EGD) to take small samples and grow the bacteria so we can pick the right antibiotics
• Your stomach discomfort (dyspepsia) and the pantoprazole you take
• Your allergies and medicines before the procedure

Next steps
• We will schedule your EGD; the team will send instructions.
• IMPORTANT: Stop your pantoprazole about 2 weeks before the endoscopy — it can hide the bacteria on the samples. We will tell you what you can use for symptoms in the meantime.
• We added a penicillin (amoxicillin) allergy — rash — to your record. This helps us choose safe antibiotics.
• We updated your record: your antibiotic courses are finished (not ongoing).
• You confirmed no blood thinners, aspirin, or fish oil.
• Call for any black or bloody stools, vomiting blood, severe stomach pain, lightheadedness, or fever.

prior_notes.text: Surveillance colonoscopy to cecum. Adequate prep (Boston 8). Findings: single 15 mm sessile polyp in the ascending colon, removed by piecemeal EMR/snare polypectomy; prophylactic hemostatic clip placed at the resection base given lesion size and anticoagulation status. Warfarin was bridged with therapeutic enoxaparin peri-procedurally per cardiology, INR confirmed <1.5 on day of procedure. Pathology (separate report): tubulovillous adenoma with low-grade dysplasia, margins indeterminate at piecemeal margins. Recommendation: repeat surveillance colonoscopy in 3 years (2026), sooner if symptoms. No immediate complications; no post-polypectomy bleeding.

74F with mechanical (bileaflet) mitral valve replacement 2015 and permanent atrial fibrillation. On lifelong warfarin, target INR 2.5-3.5 given mechanical MITRAL position (higher thromboembolic risk than aortic). Rate control with metoprolol and digoxin, stable. Anticoagulation managed by our anticoag clinic; INRs at goal. Any invasive procedure requires advance planning and heparin/LMWH bridging — standard warfarin interruption without bridging is contraindicated. Please contact cardiology before scheduling any procedure requiring anticoagulation hold.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Castellano. We're meeting today to plan your follow-up colonoscopy — you're due from the 2023 polyp. Before anything, I want to go carefully through your heart situation, because that shapes everything here. Tell me about your valve.
MA: Before we start, let me get your vitals down, Ms. Castellano — you're 5 foot 3 and 137 pounds, temperature 98.2, blood pressure 124 over 76, heart rate 60, respirations 14, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Yes, I had my mitral valve replaced back in 2015. It's the mechanical kind — the one that clicks, you can hear it if it's quiet. And I've had the irregular heartbeat, the atrial fibrillation, for years.
DR: That's exactly what I needed to confirm. A mechanical valve in the mitral position is the higher-risk kind, so we can't just stop your blood thinner for a few days the way we might for other people. You're on warfarin?
PT: Every day. The anticoagulation clinic manages my dose, they check my blood all the time. My last check was about a week ago.
DR: I see it — your INR was 3.1. For most people that would look a little high, but your target with a mechanical mitral valve is actually two-point-five to three-point-five, so you're right where you should be.
PT: They told me it runs a bit higher for me on purpose.
DR: Correct. Now, because you may need a polyp removed, and because of the valve, we cannot simply hold the warfarin. You'll need bridging — a short-acting injectable blood thinner — and I'm going to coordinate the whole plan directly with your cardiologist. No standard hold.
PT: Okay. Whatever is safest.
DR: Let me also confirm your other medicines — metoprolol and digoxin?
PT: Yes, both, for the heart rate.
DR: Anything else at all, even over-the-counter?
PT: Well, I do take a baby aspirin every morning. I started it myself — I always heard it's good for the heart.
DR: I'm really glad you mentioned that — it isn't in your chart. On top of warfarin, that adds to bleeding risk, so I'll fold that into the plan with cardiology too. We'll tell you exactly what to hold and when. Nothing gets stopped until that plan is set.
PT: Thank you. I feel better knowing you're checking with them.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Castellano is a 74-year-old woman presenting for a pre-procedure GI consult to plan surveillance colonoscopy with likely polypectomy (2023 ascending-colon 15 mm tubulovillous adenoma with low-grade dysplasia, piecemeal EMR with clip; due 2026). She has a mechanical (bileaflet) MITRAL valve prosthesis (2015) and permanent atrial fibrillation, on lifelong warfarin managed by anticoagulation clinic (target INR 2.5-3.5). She confirms warfarin, metoprolol, and digoxin. She additionally reports self-directed OTC aspirin 81 mg daily, not previously documented. No current GI bleeding or new symptoms.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 60, RR 14, BP 124/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 160 cm (5'3"), Wt 62 kg (137 lb), BMI 24.2. Labs 2026-07-10: INR 3.1 (therapeutic for mechanical mitral target 2.5-3.5, NOT supratherapeutic); hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL. Mechanical mitral valve prosthesis and atrial fibrillation on exam history.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Surveillance colonoscopy with anticipated polypectomy in a patient with a mechanical MITRAL valve — HIGH thromboembolic risk. Standard warfarin-hold protocol is contraindicated.
- RED-tier anticoagulation: plan for peri-procedural heparin/LMWH BRIDGING, coordinated with and co-managed by cardiology/anticoagulation clinic. Warfarin will not be interrupted without a bridging plan.
- INR 3.1 is in-range for a mechanical mitral valve (target 2.5-3.5); will be brought to procedure-safe level per bridging protocol, not treated as supratherapeutic.
- NEW: undocumented OTC aspirin 81 mg daily — reconcile onto medication list; hold decision to be made with cardiology given additive bleeding risk.
- Continue metoprolol and digoxin for rate control.
- Do NOT schedule or issue standard prep/hold instructions until cardiology anticoagulation-bridging plan is finalized; team to contact cardiology and anticoag clinic before scheduling.
- Counsel patient on bridging logistics and return precautions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Planning your follow-up colonoscopy (due this year), with a polyp likely to be removed
• Your mechanical mitral heart valve and blood thinner (warfarin)
• How to safely manage your blood thinners around the procedure

Next steps
• Because of your mechanical mitral valve, we will NOT simply stop your warfarin. You will need 'bridging' with a short-acting injectable blood thinner, and we will plan this together with your cardiology/anticoagulation team.
• Your INR of 3.1 is right where it should be for your valve (goal 2.5–3.5).
• We added the daily baby aspirin you take on your own to your record; please do not stop or change it until we tell you, as it affects bleeding risk.
• Keep taking your metoprolol and digoxin as usual.
• Do NOT stop any blood thinner until we send you the exact bridging plan and dates. Our team will call you after we coordinate with cardiology.
• Call for chest pain, palpitations, unusual bleeding or bruising, or lightheadedness.

transcript:
DR: Afternoon, Mr. Park. I'm the gastroenterologist — your primary care doctor sent you over because your stool test, the FIT, came back positive, and we want to schedule a colonoscopy. How are you doing?
MA: Before we get started, I've got your rooming vitals down — height five foot nine, weight one eighty-five pounds, blood pressure 128 over 78, heart rate 58, temp 36.7, respirations 14, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Doing all right, thanks. Bit nervous about all this after the heart business.
DR: Right — tell me about that. The chart says you had a heart attack and stents in December?
PT: Yeah, right after Christmas. Two stents. The cardiologist put me on the blood thinners and told me flat out — do not stop those without calling him first.
DR: That's exactly right, and I completely agree — those stents are only about seven months old, so we can't just hold your antiplatelets on our own. Let me make sure I have your medicines right. Chart shows aspirin and clopidogrel — Plavix. Still both?
PT: The aspirin, yes, every morning. But they switched me off the Plavix — now it's the other one, tica-something, twice a day. Started that maybe six weeks ago.
DR: Ticagrelor — Brilinta. Good to know, because the chart still lists Plavix. I'll fix that. You said blood thinners, plural — anything else along those lines, like warfarin, or Eliquis?
PT: No, nothing like that. Just the aspirin, the Brilinta, and I take a fish oil I buy myself — I call that a blood thinner too, I guess.
DR: Understood — fish oil's over-the-counter, that's different from a prescription anticoagulant, and your blood tests look normal there. Metoprolol and the atorvastatin, still taking those?
PT: Yes, both daily.
DR: One more thing — any bleeding you've noticed?
PT: Now that you ask, a little bright red on the paper, on and off the last few weeks. I didn't think much of it.
DR: I'm glad you mentioned it — that's important with the positive test. Here's the plan: I'll coordinate directly with your cardiologist about whether and when any medication can be adjusted for the procedure. We will not set a hold date until they clear it.
PT: Okay, that's a relief.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Park is a 62-year-old man referred to GI for a pre-procedure consult for colonoscopy after a POSITIVE screening FIT (2026-06-25). Cardiac history is central: NSTEMI in December 2025 treated with PCI and two drug-eluting stents (LAD, circumflex), LVEF 50%. He remains on dual antiplatelet therapy. He confirms daily aspirin 81 mg but reports his cardiologist switched him from clopidogrel to ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily approximately 6 weeks ago (chart still lists clopidogrel). He describes his medications as "blood thinners"; on questioning this comprises aspirin, ticagrelor, and OTC fish oil — he is NOT on warfarin or a DOAC. He additionally reports new intermittent bright red blood per rectum over the last few weeks, not previously documented. He was explicitly instructed by cardiology not to interrupt antiplatelet therapy without their input. Continues metoprolol succinate 50 mg and atorvastatin 80 mg daily.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 58, RR 14, BP 128/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 84 kg (185 lb), BMI 27.4. Labs 2026-07-02: hemoglobin 12.3 g/dL, hematocrit 37.4% (mild anemia). INR 1.0 (consistent with no systemic anticoagulation). LVEF 50% (echo 2025-12-26). FIT positive 2026-06-25.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Positive FIT + new hematochezia with mild anemia — diagnostic colonoscopy indicated. High pretest concern for a colonic source; polypectomy likely.
- HIGH-RISK antiplatelet situation: drug-eluting stents placed <12 months ago (Dec 2025), on DAPT. Premature interruption risks stent thrombosis. **No hold dates will be issued by GI.** Will coordinate periprocedural antiplatelet management directly with cardiology; aspirin to be continued through the procedure per standard practice; any decision on ticagrelor hold or bridging to be made by cardiology.
- Reconcile medication list: DISCONTINUE clopidogrel in chart; ADD ticagrelor 90 mg BID (per patient / to be confirmed with cardiology). Continue aspirin 81 mg.
- Add OTC fish oil to medication list; clarified this is not a prescription anticoagulant.
- Continue metoprolol succinate 50 mg and atorvastatin 80 mg.
- Mild anemia — will trend CBC; colonoscopy will address likely source.
- Return precautions for heavy/ongoing rectal bleeding, lightheadedness, or chest pain. Scheduling and bowel prep instructions to follow AFTER cardiology clearance on antiplatelet management.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your positive stool test (FIT) and plan for a colonoscopy
• Your recent heart attack and two heart stents (December 2025)
• Your blood-thinning medicines and why we must be careful before the procedure
• Some new bright red bleeding you noticed

Next steps
• Because your heart stents are still new, we will NOT tell you to stop your blood thinners on your own. We will talk directly with your heart doctor first and only set a procedure date after they say it is safe.
• We corrected your medicine list: you are now on ticagrelor (Brilinta) twice a day, not clopidogrel (Plavix). Keep taking your aspirin every morning.
• We noted the fish oil you buy yourself. It is not a prescription blood thinner.
• Keep taking your metoprolol and atorvastatin.
• Call us or seek care for heavy or ongoing rectal bleeding, feeling faint, or any chest pain.
• Our team and your cardiologist will contact you about timing and bowel prep instructions.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Simmons — I'm Dr. Alvarez. You're here so we can plan your follow-up colonoscopy. It's been three years since the polyp in 2023, so you're due. How have you been feeling?
MA: Before we get started, let me get your vitals down, Ms. Simmons. Height's 5 foot 4, weight 150 pounds today. Blood pressure 128 over 78, heart rate 72, temp 98.2, respirations 14, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Pretty good, thank you. No stomach troubles, no bleeding, nothing like that. I just got the reminder that I was due, so here I am.
DR: That's exactly what we like to hear. Before we schedule, I need to go carefully through your medicines, because a couple of them affect the timing of the prep. The chart here shows you on apixaban — the blood thinner — five milligrams twice a day, and it looks like the pharmacy still has active refills. Are you taking that?
PT: No, no. I finished that a while ago. I only took it because I got a clot in my leg after my knee replacement, back in the summer of '24. The hematologist said it was because of the surgery, a one-time thing, and I'd take it for a few months and then stop.
DR: So you did stop it — do you remember roughly when?
PT: Around January this year. They told me the course was done and I could quit. I haven't taken one since. I actually still have half a bottle sitting in the cabinet, and the pharmacy keeps sending me refills I don't need — I keep meaning to call them.
DR: That's really helpful, and it changes the plan quite a bit. So to be clear — no apixaban since about January, roughly six months, and nothing else that thins the blood? No aspirin, no fish oil, nothing like that?
PT: Correct. Nothing. Just my water pill for the blood pressure — the hydrochlorothiazide, every morning.
DR: Perfect, and you're still taking that one daily?
PT: Every morning, yes.
DR: Good. Then since you're not anticoagulated, we don't need to arrange any bridging or a hold — that simplifies things. I'll have the pharmacy record corrected so the apixaban shows as stopped, and the team will send your prep instructions.
PT: Oh, good. That's a relief. Thank you.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Simmons is a 69-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan surveillance colonoscopy (tubular adenoma with low-grade dysplasia, ascending colon, 2023; due 2026). She is asymptomatic — denies hematochezia, melena, abdominal pain, or change in bowel habits. Medication reconciliation is the key issue of the visit: the chart and pharmacy list apixaban 5 mg BID as active with refills, but the patient reports she completed her provoked-DVT anticoagulation course and stopped apixaban approximately 6 months ago (January 2026). She has taken none since; leftover tablets remain unused at home and auto-refills have continued without use. She denies any other antiplatelet/anticoagulant agents (no aspirin, no fish oil). She confirms hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg every morning for hypertension. DVT in 2024 was provoked by right total knee arthroplasty, no recurrence.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 128/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 68 kg (150 lb), BMI 25.6. Labs (2026-07-02): hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL, platelets 244 x10^3/uL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, INR 1.0. No active bleeding. Hemodynamically stable per intake.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Surveillance colonoscopy indicated (prior tubular adenoma, low-grade dysplasia, 2023; 3-year interval). Asymptomatic.
- Anticoagulation status reconciled: patient is NOT on apixaban — completed provoked-DVT course ~January 2026 and discontinued. No periprocedural anticoagulation hold or bridging required. Update medication list to mark apixaban discontinued; advise patient to notify pharmacy to stop auto-refills and safely dispose of remaining tablets.
- Provoked DVT 2024 — resolved, transient risk factor (arthroplasty), no indication for indefinite anticoagulation.
- HTN — continue hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily; hold on morning of procedure per anesthesia/prep protocol.
- Schedule surveillance colonoscopy; send bowel prep instructions and pre-procedure guidance. Return precautions for rectal bleeding or abdominal pain in the interim.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your follow-up (surveillance) colonoscopy, which is due this year
• Reviewing your medicines before the procedure
• The blood thinner (apixaban) from your 2024 leg clot
• Your blood pressure medicine

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy; the team will send prep instructions and what to do the day before.
• We confirmed you are NOT taking the blood thinner (apixaban) — you finished that course around January. Because of that, you do not need any special stopping or bridging plan before the procedure.
• We updated your record to show apixaban as stopped. Please call your pharmacy to cancel the automatic refills, and you can safely dispose of the leftover tablets.
• Keep taking your hydrochlorothiazide each morning; we will tell you whether to hold it on the morning of the procedure.
• Call us if you notice rectal bleeding, black stools, or belly pain before your visit.

transcript:
DR: Good afternoon, Mr. Osgood — and Mrs. Osgood, thanks for coming in. Your kidney doctor and your primary doctor sent you to us to look into the low blood count with a colonoscopy. How are you feeling?
MA: Before the doctor started, let me get you roomed — you're 5 foot 9 and 176 pounds today. Temp 36.8, heart rate 82, respirations 14, blood pressure 128 over 76, and oxygen 97 percent on room air.
PT: Tired, mostly. They keep telling me my blood is low even with those injections.
DR: That's the EPO — the epoetin injections at the kidney clinic. You're still getting those?
PT: Yes, at the nephrology visits. And I take the sevelamer with my meals, the phosphate pills.
DR: Good, I have both of those. Now, one thing that's very important before any scope — are you on a blood thinner?
FAMILY: He is. He takes a blood thinner every day, and his fish oil — I call that his other blood thinner.
DR: Let's separate those. The fish oil is a supplement — we'll have you hold it before the procedure, but the one I'm most concerned about is the prescription. That's the dabigatran, for your atrial fibrillation?
PT: That's the one. Twice a day. But the kidney doctor changed it a few weeks ago — I'm on the smaller ones now, seventy-five, twice a day. It used to be the bigger dose.
DR: That's a key detail — the chart still lists you at 110 twice daily, so I'll correct that to 75. And it matters because dabigatran clears through the kidneys, and with your kidney function we have to stop it well ahead of the colonoscopy and coordinate with nephrology.
PT: Whatever's safest.
DR: One more thing — have you noticed any change in your stools? Any black or tarry bowel movements?
PT: Now that you mention it, yes. On and off the last three weeks or so, dark, almost black. I didn't think much of it.
FAMILY: He didn't tell me that.
DR: I'm glad you did. In someone on a blood thinner, that can mean bleeding somewhere in the gut, and it fits the low blood count. That makes the colonoscopy more important. We'll plan the prep, hold the dabigatran and fish oil on a schedule, and I'll talk with your kidney doctor and anesthesia before we set the date.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Osgood is a 78-year-old man with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22), atrial fibrillation, and anemia of CKD, referred to GI for evaluation of persistent anemia with a planned colonoscopy. He reports ongoing fatigue and, on direct questioning, intermittent black/tarry stools for approximately 3 weeks (not previously documented) — concerning for occult GI blood loss in an anticoagulated patient. He confirms he is anticoagulated for atrial fibrillation with dabigatran; he reports his nephrologist recently reduced the dose to 75 mg twice daily (the chart lists 110 mg BID — stale). Wife reports he also takes over-the-counter fish oil daily, which she describes as a 'blood thinner'; this is a supplement, distinct from the clinically significant DOAC. He confirms ongoing epoetin alfa injections through nephrology and sevelamer with meals.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 82, RR 14, BP 128/76, SpO2 97% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 80 kg (176 lb), BMI 26.1. Labs (2026-06-30): hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL (low), hematocrit 28.4%, creatinine 2.9 mg/dL, eGFR 22 mL/min/1.73m2 (CKD-EPI) — advanced CKD with anemia; hemoglobin below EPO target.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Anemia with new melena in an anticoagulated CKD4 patient — proceed with diagnostic colonoscopy to evaluate for GI bleeding source; consider EGD if colonoscopy non-diagnostic.
- HIGH-RISK ANTICOAGULATION: dabigatran is renally cleared; with eGFR 22 (CKD4) it accumulates, requiring an EXTENDED periprocedural hold. Coordinate hold schedule directly with nephrology; obtain anesthesia clearance before scheduling. Do NOT use standard normal-renal-function hold intervals.
- Reconcile medication list: correct dabigatran dose to 75 mg BID per patient/nephrology (chart shows stale 110 mg BID).
- Hold over-the-counter fish oil per pre-procedure instructions; documented as previously unlisted supplement.
- Anemia of CKD — continue epoetin alfa per nephrology; recheck CBC and reassess iron studies; transfusion threshold per bleeding.
- Continue sevelamer with meals.
- Team to send bowel prep and detailed hold instructions after nephrology/anesthesia coordination; return precautions for large-volume bleeding, dizziness, or worsening fatigue.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Low blood count (anemia) and new dark/black stools
• Planning a colonoscopy to find the cause
• Your blood thinner (dabigatran) and your kidneys
• Your kidney-related medicines (EPO injections, sevelamer) and fish oil

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy. Because your dabigatran works through your kidneys, it must be stopped earlier than usual. We will coordinate the exact stop dates with your kidney doctor and the anesthesia team before we set a date — do not stop it on your own.
• We corrected your record: your dabigatran is 75 mg twice a day (not 110 mg).
• Plan to hold your over-the-counter fish oil before the procedure; we will tell you when.
• Keep getting your EPO injections and taking sevelamer with meals.
• The team will send your bowel prep and a clear list of what to hold and when.
• Call right away for a lot of bleeding, black stools with dizziness, or worsening tiredness.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Reyes. You're here so we can get your first screening colonoscopy set up. How are you feeling about it?
MA: Before the doctor gets started, let me grab your vitals — you're 5 foot 4 and 143 pounds, blood pressure 124 over 76, temp 36.8, heart rate 72, breathing 14, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Fine, a little nervous about the prep, but okay. My doctor said I'm overdue since I turned fifty.
DR: That's right, and you're average risk, which is good. Before we schedule, I want to go over a few things — especially medications, because some can affect bleeding during the scope. The intake note says you don't take any blood thinners. Is that right?
PT: Right, no blood thinners. I'm not on anything like that.
DR: No warfarin, no apixaban or rivaroxaban, nothing like Eliquis or Xarelto?
PT: No, none of those. My heart's fine — I've never had that irregular heartbeat or a blood clot or anything.
DR: Perfect. Now, it also says you take a baby aspirin sometimes. Tell me about that.
PT: Yeah, just the little 81 ones. Not every day — maybe a few times a month if my knees or my back are really aching. Nobody prescribed it, I just buy it.
DR: Okay, so occasional over-the-counter low-dose aspirin, not a daily heart aspirin. And ibuprofen too?
PT: Ibuprofen for the knees, couple times a week. I've got arthritis in both of them.
DR: Got it. That all fits with what's in your chart. The good news is the occasional aspirin isn't a true blood thinner and shouldn't cause any problem for the procedure. We'll give you prep instructions that tell you when to hold the ibuprofen and aspirin beforehand, just as routine.
PT: So I don't have to do anything special ahead of time?
DR: Nothing unusual — no special bridging or clearance. Any bleeding, weight loss, changes in your bowels?
PT: No, nothing like that. Everything's normal that way.
DR: Great. Then this is a straightforward average-risk screening. We'll schedule it and send your prep.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Reyes is a 57-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to arrange her first screening colonoscopy (average risk, overdue since age 50). She is asymptomatic: denies rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits, or unintentional weight loss. Medication review: she denies any anticoagulant or prescription antiplatelet use and specifically denies warfarin, apixaban, and rivaroxaban. She takes over-the-counter low-dose aspirin (81 mg) only intermittently — a few times per month for musculoskeletal aches, not daily and not physician-directed — and OTC ibuprofen a few times weekly for bilateral knee osteoarthritis. No atrial fibrillation, no prior VTE.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 124/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 65 kg (143 lb), BMI 24.5. Recent labs (2026-05-04): hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL, platelets 262 x10^3/uL, INR 1.0 — all normal, no bleeding-risk abnormality.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Average-risk colorectal cancer screening — asymptomatic; proceed with screening colonoscopy.
- Anticoagulation review: no anticoagulant and no prescription antiplatelet therapy. Intermittent OTC low-dose aspirin is not an anticoagulant; no anticoagulation hold, bridging, or additional clearance required. Low-dose aspirin may be continued for screening colonoscopy.
- NSAID (ibuprofen) use for knee OA — routine periprocedural hold per standard prep instructions.
- Osteoarthritis — continue current OTC symptom management.
- Colonoscopy scheduling + bowel prep instructions to follow; standard periprocedural medication guidance.

• Keep managing your knee arthritis as you have been.
• Call us if you notice rectal bleeding, weight loss, or a change in your bowel habits before the procedure.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Fitzgerald. I'm the gastroenterologist. Your primary doctor sent you over because your blood count is low and there's been some blood in the stool. Before we plan the scopes, I want to go through everything carefully. How have you been feeling?
MA: Before the doctor digs in, let me get your vitals, Mr. Fitzgerald. Height's 5 foot 9, weight 172 pounds today. Blood pressure 132 over 76, heart rate 90, temp 98.2, respirations 16, and your oxygen is 96 percent on room air.
PT: Tired, mostly. I get winded going up the stairs now, which is new. My wife's the one who pushed me to go in.
DR: I'm glad she did. Have you noticed anything with your bowel movements — color, anything unusual?
PT: Now that you ask — yeah. The last couple weeks they've been dark. Black, really, and kind of sticky. I figured it was something I ate.
DR: That's important, thank you for telling me — black, tarry stools can mean bleeding higher up, in the stomach or upper intestine. That's part of why we want to look at both ends in one session. Let me go over your medicines. The chart shows you on edoxaban, sixty milligrams, for the atrial fibrillation.
PT: It's not sixty anymore. My heart doctor cut it down — thirty now. He said my kidneys weren't keeping up. That was a couple months back.
DR: Thirty milligrams — okay, that's a very important correction, thank you. And when did you last take it?
PT: This morning, same as always. Nobody told me to stop.
DR: Understood — so no doses held yet. We'll sort out exactly when to pause it before the procedure, and I'll coordinate that with your cardiologist because of your kidneys. Are you taking any other blood thinners?
PT: Just the fish oil. My neighbor swears by it — calls it a natural blood thinner. One a day.
DR: Good to know — the fish oil is different from the edoxaban, but I'll note it. Still taking the water pill, the furosemide?
PT: Every morning, yes. For the heart.
DR: And any allergies to medications?
PT: Codeine. It makes me sick to my stomach and itchy. It's never been a good one for me.
DR: I'll get that added — nothing's listed right now. So here's the plan: an upper scope and a colonoscopy together, fairly soon given the anemia. We'll talk about whether you need any blood beforehand, and I'll arrange the timing of holding the edoxaban with cardiology.
PT: Alright. Whatever we need to do.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Fitzgerald is an 81-year-old man referred for pre-procedure evaluation prior to urgent bidirectional endoscopy (EGD + colonoscopy) for iron-deficiency anemia and guaiac-positive stool. He reports progressive fatigue and new dyspnea on exertion. On directed questioning he reports ~2 weeks of black, tarry stools (melena, not previously documented), concerning for an upper GI source. Past history: permanent atrial fibrillation (followed by cardiology), HFpEF, CKD stage 3a.

Medication reconciliation revealed several discrepancies. The chart/pharmacy list shows edoxaban 60 mg daily; the patient reports his cardiologist reduced edoxaban to 30 mg daily approximately 2 months ago for declining renal function (consistent with the 2026-05-14 cardiology note). He took his edoxaban this morning — no dose has been held. He also takes OTC fish oil daily (which he describes as a 'natural blood thinner'), not on the medication list. He confirms furosemide 20 mg each morning. He reports a codeine intolerance (nausea/vomiting, pruritus) not previously documented.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 90, RR 16, BP 132/76, SpO2 96% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 78 kg (172 lb), BMI 25.5. Labs 2026-07-02: hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL (baseline ~12), hematocrit 26.1%, ferritin 8 ug/L, eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m2; stool guaiac positive. Ambulatory, no active chest pain reported.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Symptomatic iron-deficiency anemia (Hgb 8.4) with guaiac-positive stool and new melena — concern for chronic/subacute GI blood loss, upper source suspected. Plan urgent-tier bidirectional endoscopy (EGD + colonoscopy).
- Anticoagulation: CORRECT edoxaban dose to 30 mg daily per cardiology (chart shows 60 mg — stale). Patient took a dose this morning; hold not yet initiated. Coordinate periprocedural DOAC hold with cardiology, timing keyed to last dose and CKD3a-adjusted clearance (eGFR 48). Note concurrent OTC fish oil.
- Transfusion: discuss threshold with referring/hematology given symptomatic anemia; type and screen; consider pre-procedure transfusion if symptomatic or Hgb declines.
- Iron deficiency: begin/plan IV iron repletion given ongoing blood loss and poor oral absorption context.
- Update medication list: edoxaban 30 mg (not 60 mg); add OTC fish oil.
- Add allergy/intolerance: codeine (nausea/vomiting, pruritus).
- Continue furosemide 20 mg daily; monitor volume status periprocedurally.
- Anesthesia/sedation planning given age, HFpEF, CKD, and anticoagulation.

transcript:
DR: Hi Ms. Blackfeather, I'm one of the GI doctors. Your primary care sent a referral for a follow-up colonoscopy, and it came through flagged as urgent. Before we schedule anything, I want to understand what's going on. Are you having any symptoms — bleeding, pain, change in your stool?
MA: Before we get started, let me grab your vitals — you're 5 foot 5 and 154 pounds, temp 36.8, heart rate 62, blood pressure 122 over 76, breathing 14, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: No, nothing like that. I feel fine, honestly. I think this is just the regular check because I had polyps taken out a few years back. They told me to come back around now.
DR: That's what I see too — a couple of small adenomas removed in 2021, repeat due this year. So this is routine surveillance, not something acute. That changes things, because the bigger issue is your heart. Tell me about the stent.
PT: Yes, in May. I had a heart attack, and they put a stent in — the right artery, I think. My cardiologist put me on some new pills after.
DR: Right — the chart shows ticagrelor twice a day and a baby aspirin. Are you taking both of those?
PT: The ticagrelor, yes, twice a day, that's the blood thinner. But the aspirin — I actually stopped that one myself about two weeks ago. I didn't think I needed two blood thinners, it seemed like too much.
DR: Okay — I'm really glad you told me. That's important, and I don't want you stopping either of those on your own. That aspirin and the ticagrelor are working together to protect your new stent, and stopping one this early can be dangerous. I'll get a message to your cardiologist today.
PT: Oh — I didn't realize. I'll start it again.
DR: Please hold off until cardiology confirms, but yes, we'll sort it out quickly. And for the colonoscopy — because your stent is only two months old and you're on these medicines, doing an elective scope now carries real bleeding and clotting risk. This is one we should wait on, likely until around a year after your stent. It can safely wait.
PT: That's a relief, actually.
DR: One more — any medication allergies?
PT: Codeine makes me really nauseous and throw up. Not a rash, just sick.
DR: I'll note that. We'll coordinate with cardiology and reschedule the colonoscopy for the right time.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Blackfeather is a 54-year-old woman referred to GI for a surveillance colonoscopy (two tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia removed in 2021; 5-year interval due 2026). The referral was flagged urgent, but on interview she is entirely asymptomatic — denies hematochezia, melena, abdominal pain, weight loss, or change in bowel habits. This is routine surveillance, not a symptomatic evaluation.

Her relevant cardiac history dominates: NSTEMI on 2026-05-14 treated with a drug-eluting stent to the mid RCA (~2 months ago). She was started on dual antiplatelet therapy (ticagrelor 90 mg BID + aspirin 81 mg daily) plus rosuvastatin 20 mg nightly and metoprolol. She confirms taking ticagrelor twice daily and refers to it as her 'blood thinner.' Critically, she reports self-discontinuing the aspirin approximately 2 weeks ago because she did not think she needed 'two blood thinners'; cardiology was not informed. She confirms rosuvastatin nightly. She reports codeine causes nausea/vomiting (intolerance), not previously documented.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 62, RR 14, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 165 cm (5'5"), Wt 70 kg (154 lb), BMI 25.7. Recent labs (2026-07-06): hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL, platelets 244 x10^3/uL — no anemia. No bleeding on exam by history.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Surveillance colonoscopy request, asymptomatic — reclassify from urgent to elective. Guideline-appropriate; no red-flag symptoms to warrant expedited scope.
- DEFER elective colonoscopy: DES to RCA only ~2 months ago on DAPT. Elective endoscopy/polypectomy within the first months post-DES carries high stent-thrombosis and bleeding risk. Recommend rescheduling to approximately month 12 post-stent (~mid-2027), pending cardiology.
- Mandatory cardiology consultation before any procedure to define acceptable timing and peri-procedural antiplatelet management.
- URGENT: patient self-discontinued aspirin ~2 weeks ago — unsanctioned interruption of DAPT component 2 months post-DES. Counseled not to stop antiplatelets independently. Will message cardiology today for guidance on resuming aspirin; patient advised to await their instruction.
- Patient education: ticagrelor is an antiplatelet, not an anticoagulant/warfarin; clarified role of both agents in protecting the stent.
- Add intolerance: codeine (nausea/vomiting).
- Continue ticagrelor, rosuvastatin, metoprolol as prescribed by cardiology.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Sykes. I'm the gastroenterologist. Your primary care doctor sent you over because your stool test — the FIT — came back positive, so we want to plan a colonoscopy. How have you been feeling?
MA: Before the doctor goes on, let me get you roomed — height's 5 foot 10, weight 187 pounds, blood pressure 128 over 78, heart rate 72, temp 98.2, respirations 14, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Feeling fine, honestly. No pain, no bleeding that I've seen. I was surprised the test was positive.
DR: That's common — the FIT can pick up bleeding you'd never notice, so a colonoscopy is the right next step. Let me go over your history so we plan the procedure safely. I see you had a stent back in 2014?
PT: Yes, one stent, the bare-metal kind. Right after that I was on two blood thinners for about a year, but they stopped the second one a long time ago. No chest pain or anything since.
DR: Good, that's reassuring. Now the referral note says you're on a blood thinner — I want to nail down exactly what that means. What are you actually taking each day?
PT: Just my baby aspirin, the 81, every morning. And the lisinopril for my blood pressure. That's it.
DR: So when the note says 'blood thinner,' that's the aspirin — you're not on warfarin, or Eliquis, Xarelto, anything like that?
PT: No, nothing like that. People call the aspirin a blood thinner, but that's the only one. No Coumadin, no shots.
DR: Perfect, that's exactly what I needed to confirm. And no clopidogrel — no Plavix?
PT: No, they took me off that Plavix years ago.
DR: Great. So aspirin alone. For a colonoscopy we can usually keep low-dose aspirin going, so there's nothing complicated to arrange there. Any allergies to medications?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: Any family history of colon cancer or polyps?
PT: Not that I'm aware of.
DR: Okay. Given the stable heart history on aspirin only, we don't need a special cardiology sign-off — this is a routine pre-procedure plan. My team will send you the prep instructions and get you scheduled.
PT: Sounds good. Do I stop the aspirin?
DR: We'll confirm in the instructions, but with your stent history we generally continue the low-dose aspirin. I'll make that clear in your prep sheet.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Sykes is a 65-year-old man referred to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan colonoscopy following a positive screening fecal immunochemical test (FIT, 2026-06-24). He is asymptomatic — denies overt rectal bleeding, hematochezia, melena, abdominal pain, weight loss, or change in bowel habits. PMH notable for coronary artery disease status post bare-metal coronary stent (mid-RCA, 2014) with no interval cardiac events, and hypertension. The referral narrative described the patient as being 'on a blood thinner'; on direct medication reconciliation the patient reports taking only aspirin 81 mg daily and lisinopril 10 mg daily. He explicitly denies warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants (apixaban, rivaroxaban), clopidogrel, or injectable anticoagulants; dual antiplatelet therapy was completed years after the 2014 stent. No known drug allergies. No family history of colorectal cancer or polyps reported.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 128/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 85 kg (187 lb), BMI 26.8. FIT positive (2026-06-24). Hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL (2026-07-02) — no anemia. Hemodynamically stable, no signs of active GI bleeding.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Positive FIT in a 65-year-old man — plan diagnostic colonoscopy. No anemia; asymptomatic.
- Medication reconciliation: 'blood thinner' in referral narrative resolves to low-dose aspirin 81 mg only. No therapeutic anticoagulant and no second antiplatelet agent. No anticoagulation bridging required.
- CAD s/p bare-metal stent 2014, stable on aspirin monotherapy — routine pathway; no pre-procedure cardiology hold or clearance indicated. Continue low-dose aspirin through the colonoscopy per standard guidance.
- Hypertension — continue lisinopril 10 mg daily.
- Schedule colonoscopy; bowel prep and periprocedure medication instructions to follow. Return precautions for rectal bleeding, lightheadedness, or abdominal pain.

prior_notes.text: 61-year-old woman admitted with acute epigastric pain, nausea, and lipase 1180 U/L consistent with acute biliary (gallstone) pancreatitis. Total bilirubin peaked 5.6 mg/dL, ALP 420 U/L, ALT 180 U/L. Ultrasound: cholelithiasis without cholecystitis; mild CBD dilation. MRCP (2026-07-01): 7 mm stone in the distal common bile duct (choledocholithiasis) with upstream ductal dilation; gallbladder with multiple calculi. Pancreatitis managed conservatively with IV fluids and analgesia; pain resolved and diet advanced. Discharged in stable condition with GI referral for ERCP for duct clearance, followed by interval laparoscopic cholecystectomy. No antibiotics required; no cholangitis.

Post-discharge lab review prior to procedure planning. Patient feeling well, tolerating regular diet, no recurrent pain. Repeat labs (2026-07-14): total bilirubin 2.8 mg/dL (down from 5.6), ALP 310 U/L (down from 420), ALT 70 U/L (down from 180), lipase normalized at 45 U/L. Downtrending obstructive picture. Plan: proceed with ERCP for choledocholithiasis, semi-urgent; will see in clinic for pre-procedure consult and consent. Cholecystectomy to follow after duct clearance.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Marchetti — I'm the gastroenterologist who'll be doing your procedure. We're meeting today to go over the ERCP, the one to clear the stone in your bile duct. How are you feeling since the hospital?
MA: Before you got started I took her vitals — blood pressure 122 over 76, heart rate 72, temp 36.8, breathing 14, oxygen 98 percent on room air; height 5 foot 4, weight 146 pounds.
PT: Honestly, much better. That pain in June was the worst thing I've ever felt, but it's completely gone now. I'm eating normally, no more nausea.
DR: That's good to hear. Your labs back that up — your bilirubin came down from about five and a half to two point eight, and the pancreas enzyme is back to normal. The MRI in the hospital did show a stone sitting in the duct, which is why we still want to go in and clear it.
PT: Right. And then they take the gallbladder out after?
DR: Exactly — the ERCP first to clear the duct, then the gallbladder comes out a bit later. Now let me check a few safety things before we schedule. The referral says no important medications. Is that right — nothing you take regularly?
PT: Well, I do take a blood thinner.
DR: Okay, that's important. Which one — is it something like warfarin, or apixaban, Eliquis?
PT: Oh, no, nothing prescription. It's the fish oil. My daughter said it thins the blood, so I count it as my blood thinner.
DR: Ah — so a fish oil, omega-3 supplement, over the counter. That's helpful to know, but that's different from a true blood thinner. Anything else, even something small?
PT: Just a baby aspirin every day. I've done that for my heart for maybe three years. Nobody told me to, I just do it.
DR: Good that you mentioned it — the aspirin actually does matter for this procedure, so I'll make a note about that. Any allergies?
PT: I don't think so, except — one time I had a CT with the dye in my arm and I broke out in an itchy rash all over. They gave me something for it.
DR: That's a contrast reaction, and we use a similar dye during the ERCP, so I'm very glad you told me. I'll flag that and we'll pre-medicate. Nothing's on your chart for any of this, so I'll get it all added.
PT: I always forget to mention that stuff.
DR: You told me exactly what I needed. I'll coordinate with anesthesia and the team will call you with the date and prep.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Marchetti is a 61-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for pre-procedure consultation prior to ERCP. She was hospitalized in late June 2026 with acute gallstone pancreatitis; MRCP demonstrated a 7 mm distal common bile duct stone (choledocholithiasis) with cholelithiasis. Her pancreatitis resolved with conservative management. She now reports feeling well, tolerating a regular diet, with complete resolution of the epigastric pain and no recurrent symptoms. She understands the plan for ERCP to clear the duct followed by interval laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

Medication/allergy reconciliation surfaced several items not reflected in the chart. The patient described being "on a blood thinner"; on clarification this is an over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3) supplement rather than a prescription anticoagulant — no warfarin or DOAC. She additionally reports taking aspirin 81 mg daily (self-directed, cardioprotective) for approximately 3 years, which the referral characterized as "no relevant medications." She reports a prior diffuse pruritic rash following IV iodinated contrast during a CT, not previously documented.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 66 kg (146 lb), BMI 24.8. Labs 2026-07-14: total bilirubin 2.8 mg/dL (down from admission peak 5.6), ALP 310 U/L (down from 420), ALT 70 U/L (down from 180), lipase normalized at 45 U/L. MRCP 2026-07-01: 7 mm distal CBD stone with mild upstream dilation; cholelithiasis. Patient comfortable, non-icteric appearing, abdomen soft and non-tender per interview.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Choledocholithiasis with resolved gallstone pancreatitis — proceed with ERCP, sphincterotomy, and stone extraction, semi-urgent. Note downtrending LFTs (bili 5.6 -> 2.8) and clinical improvement raise the possibility of partial/spontaneous stone passage; ERCP remains indicated to confirm clearance, with occlusion cholangiogram to define residual stone burden.
- Anticoagulation/bleeding risk — "blood thinner" clarified as OTC fish oil, NOT a prescription anticoagulant; no bridging/hold of warfarin or DOAC required. Advise stopping fish oil supplement prior to procedure per periprocedural bleeding-risk practice.
- Aspirin 81 mg daily (new to record) — relevant to planned sphincterotomy; low-dose aspirin may generally be continued but confirm with proceduralist; document in medication list.
- Allergy — add iodinated contrast reaction (pruritic rash); plan pre-procedure corticosteroid/antihistamine premedication and notify anesthesia.
- Laparoscopic cholecystectomy to follow after duct clearance.
- Anesthesia: standard; coordinate given contrast reaction. Team to contact patient with date and bowel/NPO prep instructions.
after_visit_summary:

What we discussed

• Your recent hospital stay for gallstone pancreatitis, which has resolved
• Your medicines and allergies
• The plan to remove your gallbladder after the ERCP

Next steps
• We will schedule your ERCP soon; the team will call you with the date, prep, and when to stop eating and drinking.
• We updated your record: your "blood thinner" is actually a fish oil supplement — please stop it before the procedure as we discuss.
• We added your daily baby aspirin to your record; we will tell you whether to keep taking it before the procedure.
• We added an allergy note about the reaction (itchy rash) you had to the CT dye; we will give you medicine beforehand to prevent it.
• Your gallbladder removal will be planned for after the ERCP.
• Call us or seek care for belly pain, fever, chills, yellowing of the eyes or skin, or vomiting.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: MedicationRequest apixaban 5 mg BID active; last dose UNKNOWN, 'ED to confirm'
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient took his morning apixaban dose today at ~7 AM with breakfast, ~4 hours before the visit; daughter confirms
planted_discrepancies.note: Fills the load-bearing gap. Recent DOAC dose = near-peak anticoagulation for a same-day emergent ERCP with planned sphincterotomy; drives reversal (andexanet/PCC) vs delay decision NOW.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: INR 1.2 on referral, implied reassuring / near-normal coagulation
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: INR does not reliably reflect apixaban (factor Xa inhibitor) anticoagulant effect; a normal INR does NOT mean the patient is safe to scope
planted_discrepancies.note: Classic DOAC trap — INR/aPTT are unreliable for apixaban. The falsely reassuring INR must not override the timing/reversal decision from D1.

transcript:
DR: Mr. Chu, I'm the GI doctor — I know you came in through the emergency room this morning and you're feeling pretty rough. I'll be quick. Can you tell me what's been going on?
MA: Before we start, let me get your vitals down — temp's 102, that's 38.9, heart rate 96, breathing 20, blood pressure 128 over 76, and oxygen 96 percent on room air. You're 5 foot 9 and 176 pounds.
PT: The last couple days, chills, shaking. And this pain up here on the right side, under my ribs. My daughter said my eyes looked yellow.
FAMILY: They were yellow — and the whites too. That's why I made him come in.
DR: That combination — fever, that right-sided pain, and the jaundice — tells us the bile duct is likely blocked and infected. Your ultrasound shows a dilated duct, twelve millimeters, probably a stone. We need to go in with a scope today to clear it and drain it. This isn't something we wait on.
PT: Okay. Whatever you need to do.
DR: The big thing I have to sort out first is your blood thinner. The chart says apixaban, five milligrams, twice a day, for your irregular heartbeat — but nobody's been able to tell me when your last dose was. Do you remember?
PT: I took it this morning. Same as always, with breakfast.
DR: This morning — about what time?
PT: Around seven, when I had my toast. Before we drove over.
FAMILY: Yeah, I watched him take his morning pills at breakfast. The little peach one and the heart one.
DR: That's really important — thank you. So it's only been a few hours. Your INR came back at one-point-two, which looks normal, but that number doesn't actually measure this medication, so it doesn't tell me you're safe to scope. A recent dose means you still have active blood thinner on board, and I'm going to talk with anesthesia and pharmacy about whether we reverse it or wait a bit before we do the sphincterotomy.
PT: I didn't realize the pill would matter for that.
DR: It matters a lot for bleeding risk. One more — any allergies? Nothing's listed here.
PT: Contrast dye. A couple years ago on a CT scan I broke out in hives, all itchy and flushed.
DR: That's key — this scope uses contrast dye in the duct. I'll flag it and we'll premedicate. Alright, we're moving quickly. The team will get consent and prep started.
FAMILY: Thank you, doctor.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Chu is a 70-year-old man referred emergently from the ED this morning (2026-07-18) with a 1–2 day history of fever with rigors, right upper quadrant abdominal pain, and jaundice (yellow sclerae/skin noted by family). This constitutes Charcot's triad, confirmed on interview. He has a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on apixaban 5 mg BID and hypertension. Critically, the referral listed his last apixaban dose as unknown; on direct interview the patient and his daughter confirm he took his usual morning apixaban dose today at approximately 07:00 (~4 hours before evaluation). He also reports a prior reaction to iodinated CT contrast ~2 years ago (hives, itching, flushing), which was not documented in the allergy list.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 38.9 C, HR 96, RR 20, BP 128/76, SpO2 96% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 80 kg (176 lb), BMI 26.1. ED labs (2026-07-18): total bilirubin 6.2 mg/dL (H), alkaline phosphatase 342 U/L (H), WBC 15.8 x10^3/uL (H), INR 1.2, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL. RUQ ultrasound: dilated CBD 12 mm with intrahepatic biliary dilation and distal CBD echogenic focus suspicious for choledocholithiasis; cholelithiasis present. Findings consistent with obstructive acute cholangitis.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Acute (ascending) cholangitis secondary to suspected choledocholithiasis with CBD dilation to 12 mm and bilirubin 6.2 — urgent/emergent ERCP with biliary decompression and sphincterotomy indicated same-day. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and IV fluids; sepsis monitoring.
- Anticoagulation timing (apixaban): last dose today ~07:00 per patient/family — near-peak effect for an emergent procedure with planned sphincterotomy. INR 1.2 is NOT reassuring because it does not reflect factor Xa inhibitor activity. Urgent discussion with anesthesia and pharmacy regarding periprocedural bleeding risk and reversal (andexanet alfa or 4-factor PCC) versus short delay; obtain anti-Xa (apixaban-calibrated) level if available. Hold further apixaban doses.
- Iodinated contrast reaction (hives) — add to allergy list; premedicate per protocol before ERCP contrast injection.
- Atrial fibrillation — continue rate control with metoprolol; anticoagulation to be resumed post-procedure per bleeding risk.
- Hypertension — continue home lisinopril as tolerated with hemodynamics.
- Consent, NPO status, and same-day ERCP scheduling in progress; this is an emergent case, not a routine clinic pathway.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What is going on
• You have an infection caused by a blockage in your bile duct (cholangitis). Your ultrasound showed the duct is enlarged, likely from a stone.
• Because this can get dangerous quickly, we need to do a procedure today (ERCP) to open and drain the duct.

About your medications and safety
• You told us you took your blood thinner (apixaban) this morning around 7 AM. This is important — it means the medicine is still active, so our team is deciding whether to reverse it or wait a short time before the procedure to lower bleeding risk. Please do not take any more doses until we tell you.
• Your INR blood test looked normal, but that test does not measure this particular blood thinner, so it does not mean it is safe to proceed on its own.
• We added your contrast dye reaction (hives) to your record. The procedure uses dye, so we will give you medicine beforehand to prevent a reaction.

Next steps
• You will stay nothing-by-mouth (no food or drink). We are giving you IV fluids and antibiotics.
• The team will review consent and get you to the procedure area.
• Tell your nurse right away about worsening pain, trouble breathing, or any bleeding.

_note: MOCK GI patient in the style of synthetic-ambient-fhir-25. No real patient. For PreChart development/eval.
