transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Holm — thanks for coming in so quickly. I'm the gastroenterologist. I've reviewed your CT and labs, and I want to walk through the plan and hear your side of the story. How have you been feeling?
MA: Before the doctor stepped in I took your vitals, Ms. Holm — blood pressure 122 over 76, heart rate 76, temperature 36.8, breathing 14, oxygen 98 percent on room air; height 5 foot 5, weight 137 pounds today.
PT: Tired, mostly. My daughter noticed my eyes went yellow about two weeks ago — that's what scared us. My urine's been really dark and my stools almost white.
DR: Any belly pain with it?
PT: That's the strange part — no pain at all. It doesn't hurt anywhere.
DR: Painless jaundice — that's exactly what we take seriously. And I see you lost some weight?
PT: About twelve pounds since spring. I just haven't been hungry. And out of nowhere in January they told me I had diabetes — I was always healthy, so that surprised me.
DR: That timing is important; sometimes the sugar problem and this are connected. The scan shows a 3.2 centimeter mass in the head of the pancreas, and a tumor marker that's up. To be clear — we don't have a tissue diagnosis yet. The whole point of this procedure, the EUS, is to take a small sample so we actually know what it is.
PT: So it's not confirmed cancer yet?
DR: Correct — suspected, not proven. That's what the biopsy settles. Now, medications — the chart says none. Is that right?
PT: Well, my regular doctor started me on metformin, twice a day, for the diabetes. And I take fish oil every day — he called it a blood thinner, so I wasn't sure if I should stop it.
DR: Good that you mentioned both. Fish oil is fine — it isn't a true blood thinner, so it won't delay anything. We'll note the metformin and have you hold it the morning of. Any allergies?
PT: When I had the CT, I got a rash from the dye afterward.
DR: Thank you — that matters, because the second part may use X-ray dye to place a stent and open the bile duct. I'll flag it and we'll premedicate. So: EUS with a biopsy, and likely a stent the same session to fix the jaundice.
PT: Okay. I just want to know what we're dealing with.
DR: We'll get you scheduled this week and the team will call with prep and fasting instructions.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Holm is a 59-year-old woman referred for pre-procedure evaluation of a pancreatic head mass. She reports ~2 weeks of painless jaundice (scleral icterus noted by family), dark urine, and pale/acholic stools, without abdominal pain or fever. She notes ~12 lb unintentional weight loss over 3 months with anorexia, and new-onset hyperglycemia diagnosed in January 2026 — atypical given normal BMI and minimal family history, raising concern for a herald/paraneoplastic presentation. Medication reconciliation: the chart lists no medications, but the patient reports metformin 500 mg BID started by her PCP ~2 months ago and daily OTC fish oil (omega-3), which she believed was a 'blood thinner.' She reports a rash after IV contrast during her recent CT; no prior allergies were on file.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 76, RR 14, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 165 cm (5'5"), Wt 62 kg (137 lb), BMI 22.8 (prior 67.5 kg / 149 lb on 2026-05-14, consistent with documented ~12 lb loss). Labs (2026-07-03): total bilirubin 4.1 mg/dL, direct 3.2 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 412 U/L, CA 19-9 890 U/mL, lipase 48 U/L (normal); HbA1c 8.9% (2026-05-14). CT abdomen: 3.2 cm hypoenhancing pancreatic head mass with double-duct sign and biliary dilation; no definite metastases. No tissue diagnosis to date.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Pancreatic head mass, suspicious for adenocarcinoma but NOT tissue-confirmed — proceed with EUS-FNA for cytology. Chart currently overstates certainty (listed as confirmed malignancy); documentation corrected to provisional pending pathology.
- Obstructive jaundice (bilirubin 4.1) — plan ERCP with biliary stent for decompression, likely same session.
- Medication reconciliation: add metformin 500 mg BID (PCP-prescribed) — HOLD morning of procedure for NPO/contrast planning. Fish oil (omega-3) is not a clinically significant anticoagulant; no need to hold and does not alter bleeding risk for FNA.
- Add allergy: prior rash to iodinated IV contrast — plan pre-medication/mitigation for ERCP fluoroscopic contrast; notify anesthesia.
- New-onset diabetes (HbA1c 8.9%) — likely paraneoplastic/herald; continue metformin between procedures per PCP.
- Expedite oncology pathway; MDT/tumor board referral once cytology results. Staging and CA 19-9 trend to follow.
- Team to schedule EUS-FNA +/- ERCP this week with prep, fasting, and anticoagulation/anesthesia clearance instructions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Yellowing of the eyes and skin (jaundice) without pain, dark urine, pale stools
• A spot on the head of the pancreas seen on your CT scan — we do NOT yet know for certain what it is
• Recent weight loss and the new diabetes
• Your medicines and allergies

Next steps
• We will schedule an EUS with a biopsy to take a small sample and find out exactly what the spot is. Nothing is confirmed until we have that result.
• At the same time we will likely place a small stent (ERCP) to drain the bile and improve the jaundice.
• We updated your record: added metformin (hold it the morning of the procedure — the team will confirm), and added your reaction to CT/X-ray dye so we can premedicate you.
• Your fish oil is fine to keep taking — it is not a true blood thinner and does not need to be stopped.
• The team will call this week with prep, fasting, and what to hold before the procedure.
• Call for fever, chills, worsening yellowing, severe belly pain, or vomiting.

transcript:
DR: Good afternoon, Mr. Villanueva — thanks for coming in. I've read your file. This all started with the bicycle accident last month?
MA: Before the doctor gets started, let me grab your vitals — you're 5 foot 10 and 181 pounds today. Blood pressure 122 over 78, heart rate 72, temp 36.8, breathing 14, and your oxygen is 98 percent on room air.
PT: Yes. I went over the handlebars, landed on my side. They did a CT scan in the ER to make sure nothing was torn. Everything was fine — but they found this little spot on my pancreas by accident.
DR: That's right. A 14 millimeter cyst in the body of the pancreas, picked up incidentally. Just so I understand — have you had any belly pain, weight loss, yellowing of the eyes, changes in your stools, or new diabetes?
PT: No, nothing. Honestly I feel completely normal. If I hadn't crashed my bike I'd never have known it was there.
DR: That's very common — these are often found by chance. The referral says 'rule out malignancy,' and I want to be clear about what that means: it's the question we're trying to answer, not something you've been diagnosed with. Nothing on the CT says cancer. The scan is to characterize the cyst and make sure it's a benign type.
PT: Okay, that's a relief. My father had pancreatic cancer, so when I saw that word I got scared.
DR: I understand. That family history is worth noting — it's about your father, not a diagnosis for you, but it does make me want a careful look. When was he diagnosed?
PT: In his seventies, I think.
DR: Good to know. Now, any medications?
PT: No prescriptions at all. I do take fish oil every morning — is that a blood thinner? Should I stop it?
DR: Fish oil isn't a true blood thinner and won't stop the procedure. I'll note it, but you don't need to hold it. Any other medical problems or past surgeries?
PT: None. Never been in the hospital before this.
DR: Perfect. The plan is an endoscopic ultrasound — a scope that looks at the pancreas from the inside. If the cyst looks like it needs it, we sample the fluid with a fine needle during the same procedure. My team will call with prep and timing.
PT: Sounds good. Thank you for explaining it.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Óscar Villanueva is a 48-year-old man referred for pre-procedure evaluation of an incidental 14 mm pancreatic cystic lesion discovered on a trauma CT (2026-06-21) after a low-speed bicycle fall. He is asymptomatic: denies abdominal pain, weight loss, jaundice, steatorrhea, or new-onset diabetes. He takes no prescription medications and reports daily over-the-counter fish oil, which he asked about as a possible blood thinner. He has no chronic medical problems and no prior abdominal surgery. He volunteers a family history of pancreatic cancer in his father (diagnosed in his 70s).

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 82 kg (181 lb), BMI 25.9. Labs (2026-07-10): CA 19-9 17 U/mL (normal), lipase 38 U/L (normal), total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, hemoglobin 15.1 g/dL. Trauma CT (2026-06-21): 14 mm cystic lesion in the pancreatic body without mass, ductal dilation, or lymphadenopathy; no CT features diagnostic of malignancy.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Incidental 14 mm pancreatic cystic lesion of undetermined significance — asymptomatic. Malignancy is NOT established; 'rule out malignancy' is the referral question. Plan cyst-protocol EUS to characterize, with FNA and cyst-fluid analysis (CEA, cytology, and molecular testing) if the lesion meets criteria.
- Family history of pancreatic cancer (father) — noted as a risk factor; not a diagnosis for the patient.
- Medications: no prescription anticoagulants. OTC fish oil documented; not a therapeutic anticoagulant — no periprocedural hold required. Patient reassured.
- No significant PMH; no prior abdominal surgery.
- EUS scheduling, bowel-prep-free procedure instructions, and NPO/anesthesia guidance to follow. Return precautions for new abdominal pain, jaundice, or fever.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• A small cyst on your pancreas that was found by chance on your accident CT scan
• You have no symptoms from it
• The plan for an endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) to take a closer look
• Your family history and your questions about fish oil

What this means
• The word 'malignancy' on your referral is the question we are checking — not a diagnosis. Nothing on your scan points to cancer.

Next steps
• We will schedule an endoscopic ultrasound to look closely at the cyst. If needed, we can sample the cyst fluid with a tiny needle during the same procedure.
• You do NOT need to stop your fish oil — it is not a blood thinner.
• Continue taking no prescription medications, as before.
• The team will call with your procedure date, timing, and instructions about not eating beforehand.
• Call us for new belly pain, yellowing of the skin or eyes, or fever.

prior_notes.text: ERCP performed for benign distal common bile duct stricture (imaging and brushings negative for malignancy). Cholangiogram confirmed a short benign-appearing distal CBD stricture with upstream dilation. A 10 Fr x 7 cm plastic biliary stent was placed across the stricture with good bile drainage. No sphincterotomy performed this session (stent placed over guidewire after balloon dilation). Plan: scheduled plastic stent exchange in ~3 months to maintain patency and reassess stricture. Antiplatelet (clopidogrel, taken for prior TIA) was continued for this non-sphincterotomy procedure without bleeding complication.

Follow-up after transient ischemic attack (transient right hand weakness and dysarthria, fully resolved). MRI without acute infarct; carotids without high-grade stenosis. No atrial fibrillation on extended monitoring — anticoagulation not indicated. Started clopidogrel 75 mg daily as single-agent antiplatelet for secondary stroke prevention. Blood pressure controlled on amlodipine. Patient has no history of coronary artery disease or coronary stent.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mrs. O'Rourke. You're in today so we can plan the stent exchange — the one from back in April. How have you been feeling?
MA: Before we get started, let me grab your vitals. You're 5 foot 3 and 141 pounds today. Blood pressure 134 over 80, heart rate 72, temp 36.8, breathing 14, oxygen 97 percent on room air.
PT: Honestly, quite well. No fevers, no chills. My daughter keeps checking my eyes for the yellow color and there's been none of that. I feel normal.
DR: That's exactly what I like to hear. No belly pain, no dark urine, no itching?
PT: None of it. That's why I was a little surprised — the letter said it was urgent, but I don't feel like anything's wrong.
DR: You're right to flag that. This is a routine, scheduled exchange — we planned it back in April to swap the plastic stent before it clogs. It's not an emergency. The chart triage tag overstated it.
PT: Oh good. That's a relief.
DR: Let me go over your medicines. The chart has you on clopidogrel — it lists it as being for a heart stent. Is that right?
PT: No, no heart stent. I've never had anything done to my heart. That's my blood thinner — I take it because I had a mini-stroke a couple years back. The neurologist put me on it so I wouldn't have another.
DR: That's an important correction — so it's the clopidogrel for stroke prevention, on its own, and there's no coronary stent. I'll fix that in the record.
PT: Yes. Just the one pill for that. And the amlodipine for my blood pressure.
DR: Good — amlodipine daily, still taking it?
PT: Every morning.
DR: Now, the clopidogrel — whether we ask you to hold it before the procedure depends on exactly what we do. If we just slide the old stent out and put a fresh one in, that's low bleeding risk and you likely won't need to stop it. If we end up needing to cut the muscle at the opening — a sphincterotomy — then we'd want it held for several days beforehand. Because it's your only stroke-prevention medicine, I don't want you off it unnecessarily.
PT: So I shouldn't stop it yet?
DR: Correct — don't stop anything on your own. I'll confirm the plan with the endoscopist and the team will call you with exact instructions. Any allergies to medicines?
PT: No, none that I know of.
DR: Perfect. We'll get you scheduled and send the prep details.
note:
**Subjective:** Mrs. O'Rourke is a 77-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for pre-procedure consultation ahead of a scheduled plastic biliary stent exchange. A 10 Fr plastic biliary stent was placed 2026-04-16 for a benign distal CBD stricture, with a planned ~3-month exchange. She is asymptomatic: no fever, chills, jaundice, pruritus, abdominal pain, or dark urine, and feels well. She notes the referral was labeled urgent but she has no symptoms of obstruction/cholangitis. Medication review: she takes clopidogrel 75 mg daily as single-agent antiplatelet for secondary stroke prevention after a 2024 TIA; she refers to it as her 'blood thinner.' She has NO history of coronary artery disease or coronary stent — contrary to the charted indication. Amlodipine 5 mg daily for hypertension, confirmed. No known drug allergies.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 134/80, SpO2 97% on RA; Ht 160 cm (5'3"), Wt 64 kg (141 lb), BMI 25.0. Recent labs (2026-07-14): total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, ALP 92 U/L, ALT 26 U/L (all normal — no cholestasis), INR 1.0, platelets 244 x10^3/uL. Afebrile, non-icteric per history/exam.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Benign distal CBD stricture with indwelling plastic biliary stent — routine scheduled ERCP stent exchange; normal LFTs and asymptomatic status argue against urgent obstruction/cholangitis. Correct triage from urgent to elective/scheduled.
- Periprocedural antiplatelet management (nuanced): clopidogrel is SINGLE-AGENT for TIA secondary prevention, not DAPT for a coronary stent — correct charted indication. Hold decision is procedure-dependent: simple plastic stent exchange without sphincterotomy is low bleeding risk and clopidogrel can likely be continued; if sphincterotomy is anticipated, hold clopidogrel ~5 days pre-procedure and weigh stroke risk. Confirm planned technique with endoscopist BEFORE issuing hold instructions. Patient advised not to stop any medication on her own.
- Update medication list: clopidogrel indication corrected to TIA/secondary stroke prevention; remove erroneous coronary-stent/DAPT indication. 'Blood thinner' = antiplatelet clopidogrel already on list, not an anticoagulant.
- Hypertension — continue amlodipine 5 mg daily.
- Team to finalize scheduling, confirm sphincterotomy plan, and relay specific pre-procedure medication and prep instructions.

transcript:
MA: Hi Mr. Williams, let me room you and grab your vitals before the doctor comes in. Height's five foot ten, weight's one eighty-one pounds. Temp 36.7, heart rate 72, breathing 14, blood pressure 122 over 78, and your oxygen's 98 percent on room air.
DR: Good morning, Mr. Williams — I'm the advanced endoscopist. You were referred for a procedure to clear a stone in your bile duct. Tell me what's been going on.
PT: Yeah, I had this pain up here on the right side, comes in waves, and my urine went dark for a couple days. The scan showed a stone stuck in the duct.
DR: That fits with your labs — your bilirubin and liver numbers are up, and the MRCP shows a stone with the duct backed up. Now, the important part for me: you had gastric bypass surgery, is that right?
PT: Right, 2019. Best thing I ever did — I lost about a hundred and ten pounds and I've kept it off.
DR: That's great, and it's stayed stable. The reason I'm asking is that the bypass rearranges your anatomy. A regular ERCP goes down through the mouth to the duct, but after a Roux-en-Y that route doesn't reach anymore. So the standard scope they wrote on the referral won't work for you — we'd need either a scope-assisted approach that goes the long way around, or working together with a surgeon who makes a small opening in the old stomach so I can reach the duct that way.
PT: Oh. Nobody told me it'd be different.
DR: It changes the plan quite a bit. To pick the safest route I really need the operative report from your bypass — specifically how long the limbs of the bypass were. Do you know where that surgery was done?
PT: It was at a hospital across the bay, but I don't have any of the paperwork. I honestly wouldn't know the lengths, they never went into that with me.
DR: No problem — we'll request those records before we schedule. One more thing: any blood thinners?
PT: I take a blood thinner every day, yeah.
DR: Okay, which one — something like warfarin, or one of the newer ones?
PT: No, no, it's the fish oil. The omega-3 capsules. My last doctor called it a natural blood thinner so that's what I say.
DR: Ah — good to clarify. Fish oil isn't a true blood thinner, so there's nothing there we need to stop for the procedure. Are you on anything else?
PT: Just my bariatric multivitamin every day and the B12 shot once a month.
DR: Perfect. So the plan is: get your operative records, confirm the approach, and then schedule you. My coordinator will call you.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Williams is a 41-year-old man referred for ERCP for choledocholithiasis. He reports intermittent right-upper-quadrant colicky pain and a few days of dark urine. He has a history of Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (2019, outside facility) with sustained ~110 lb weight loss; obesity resolved and weight stable. He describes taking a daily "blood thinner," which on direct questioning is over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3), not a therapeutic anticoagulant or antiplatelet. He confirms daily bariatric multivitamin and monthly intramuscular B12. He does not know his bypass limb lengths and has no operative paperwork.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 82 kg (181 lb), BMI 25.9. Labs (2026-07-15): total bilirubin 3.1 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 268 U/L, ALT 142 U/L, INR 1.0 — obstructive pattern. MRCP (2026-07-06): 9 mm distal CBD calculus with CBD dilation to 11 mm; cholelithiasis; surgically altered Roux-en-Y anatomy.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction in a patient with Roux-en-Y gastric bypass — CONFIRMED indication for biliary drainage.
- ALTERED ANATOMY: standard per-oral ERCP is not feasible. Approach must be advanced — either device/enteroscopy-assisted ERCP or laparoscopic-assisted transgastric ERCP. Referral request for standard ERCP amended; case routed to advanced endoscopy.
- Operative report from 2019 RYGB (outside facility) NOT in chart — obtain to document limb lengths, which determine feasibility of enteroscopy-assisted vs laparoscopic-assisted approach. Records request initiated; multidisciplinary discussion with bariatric/general surgery.
- Medication reconciliation: daily "blood thinner" clarified as fish oil (omega-3), NOT a therapeutic anticoagulant — no periprocedural anticoagulation hold required. Continue bariatric multivitamin and monthly B12.
- Do not schedule until operative records reviewed and approach confirmed; coordinator to contact patient. Anesthesia clearance and periprocedural instructions to follow once approach selected.

Next steps

• Your daily fish oil is not a true blood thinner, so you do not need to stop it for the procedure.

• Our coordinator will call you once your records are reviewed. Call sooner for fever, worsening pain, or yellowing of the eyes or skin.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Grossman. Thanks for coming in. You were referred to us for an endoscopic ultrasound after an endoscopy showed something in the stomach. Can you walk me through how that came about?
MA: Before we start, let me get your vitals real quick. You're 5 foot 4 and 150 pounds today. Temp's 98.2, heart rate 72, blood pressure 124 over 78, and your oxygen is 98 percent. All looking good.
PT: Sure. I'd been having some heartburn, so my regular doctor sent me for a scope. That was at the imaging center across town, not here. Afterward they told me there was a little bump in the stomach wall — nothing they could feel from the outside, they said it was under the surface.
DR: That's the kind of thing we call a subepithelial lesion — it sits beneath the lining. Did they tell you a size, or whether they took a biopsy?
PT: They said it was about two centimeters. I don't think they biopsied it — they said it was too deep for the regular scope and that's why I'd need this ultrasound one. I don't have the paperwork with me, I'm sorry.
DR: No problem — we've requested the report and the images from them, but they haven't reached us yet. We'll chase those down before your procedure, because they'll tell us exactly where it is and what we're aiming at. Now, symptom-wise — any trouble swallowing, weight loss, black stools, feeling full early?
PT: No, none of that. Honestly if it weren't for the heartburn I'd never have known. I feel fine.
DR: Good. Let me check your medicines. I have levothyroxine for your thyroid — still taking that?
PT: Every morning, yes. My thyroid numbers were normal last time.
DR: Any blood thinners?
PT: I do take a blood thinner, yes — fish oil. I take it for my heart.
DR: Ah — fish oil, the omega-3? That's an over-the-counter supplement, not a prescription blood thinner like warfarin or a daily aspirin?
PT: Right, just the fish oil capsules. No prescription one.
DR: Good to clarify — that matters if we end up taking a needle sample during the ultrasound. Any allergies to medicines?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: Perfect. So the plan is: we get the outside report and images, then schedule the EUS to characterize the lesion, and we'll sample it if it looks like the kind that should be sampled. My team will call you with the prep and timing.
PT: Sounds good. Thank you.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Grossman is a 64-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for pre-procedure consultation prior to endoscopic ultrasound. She was referred after an outside upper endoscopy (performed ~2026-06-20 at another facility, done for reflux symptoms) reportedly identified an 18 mm subepithelial gastric lesion. She recalls being told the lesion was "about two centimeters" and that it was not biopsied because it was subepithelial. She does not have the outside report or images with her; these have been requested but not yet received. She is asymptomatic with respect to the lesion — denies dysphagia, weight loss, hematochezia/melena, early satiety, and abdominal pain; her only symptom was intermittent heartburn that prompted the endoscopy. Hypothyroidism is stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily with a recent normal TSH. On medication review she initially reported taking "a blood thinner," which on clarification is over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3), not a prescription anticoagulant or antiplatelet agent. No known drug allergies.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 124/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 68 kg (150 lb), BMI 25.6. Recent labs (2026-05-28): TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal), hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL (normal, no anemia). Outside EGD report and images not yet available for review. No confirmed pathology/biopsy result on file.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Incidental subepithelial gastric lesion (~18 mm per referral; size and location to be confirmed from outside report), asymptomatic. Plan EUS for characterization, with FNA if indicated by EUS appearance.
- RECORDS-HUNT: Obtain outside EGD report and endoscopic images before EUS to confirm lesion size, location, and whether biopsy was attempted — will guide EUS approach and need for FNA. Procedure scheduling pending these records.
- Medication reconciliation: only prescription medication is levothyroxine. "Blood thinner" clarified as OTC fish oil (omega-3), not a true anticoagulant/antiplatelet — no periprocedural hold anticipated on that basis; will reconfirm at scheduling.
- Hypothyroidism — continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; TSH normal.
- No known drug allergies documented.
- Team to contact patient with EUS prep instructions and timing once outside records are received.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Andrade. Thanks for coming in on short notice. Your hepatologist asked us to look at your bile ducts with an ERCP, and I want to walk through why and make sure the plan fits with what you're feeling.
MA: Before we start, let me get your vitals — height 5 foot 9, weight 176 pounds. Temp is 36.7, heart rate 72, respirations 14, blood pressure 122 over 76, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Morning. Yeah, Dr. Okafor called me herself. She said the MRI showed a new narrowing and she wants a closer look — and to take a brush sample to be safe.
DR: That's right. Your PSC has been stable for years, but this scan shows a new dominant stricture that wasn't there last summer, and your alkaline phosphatase has climbed — it was around 320 in January, it's 480 now, and your bilirubin is up a touch to 1.9.
PT: She mentioned the numbers. She was careful to say it's not cancer — but that they can't rule cancer out on the picture alone. That's the part that scares me.
DR: I want to be just as careful with the words. Right now cholangiocarcinoma is a possibility we have to exclude, not a diagnosis. The whole point of the brushings is to answer that question. So we're doing this promptly, but nobody is telling you that you have cancer today.
PT: Okay. That helps, honestly.
DR: Let me check your medicines. I have ursodiol three times a day.
PT: Yes, every day, no misses.
DR: What about for your colitis?
PT: The mesalamine — the 1.2 gram once a day. I take that too, every morning. My gut's been quiet, no bleeding, no rushing to the bathroom.
DR: Good, and I want that mesalamine on your list — it only had the ursodiol. Any blood thinners?
PT: Just fish oil. Somebody told me that thins the blood, so I count it.
DR: That's helpful, but fish oil isn't a true blood thinner — you're not on warfarin, Eliquis, aspirin, anything like that?
PT: No, nothing like that. Just the fish oil capsules.
DR: Perfect, that simplifies the procedure. Any allergies?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: We'll get the ERCP with brushings scheduled quickly, and the team will send prep and fasting instructions.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Andrade is a 55-year-old man with primary sclerosing cholangitis (diagnosed 2016) and associated quiescent ulcerative colitis, referred by hepatology for pre-procedure evaluation prior to ERCP. Surveillance MRCP (2026-07-02) shows a NEW dominant stricture of the common hepatic duct, increased from the prior stable 2025 study, with upstream dilatation; the radiologist states a superimposed cholangiocarcinoma cannot be excluded and recommends tissue sampling. The patient is aware and appropriately frames malignancy as a possibility being excluded, not an established diagnosis. He reports no jaundice, pruritus, fever/rigors, or weight loss. UC is quiescent — no rectal bleeding, urgency, or nocturnal stools. He confirms ursodiol 300 mg TID and mesalamine 1.2 g daily with good adherence. He reports taking OTC fish oil, which he refers to as a 'blood thinner'; he denies any prescription anticoagulant or antiplatelet (no warfarin, DOAC, aspirin, or clopidogrel). No known drug allergies.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 80 kg (176 lb), BMI 26.1. Cholestatic labs (2026-07-10): ALP 480 U/L (up from 320 in Jan 2026), total bilirubin 1.9 mg/dL, GGT 410 U/L, ALT 62 U/L, CA 19-9 68 U/mL. MRCP 2026-07-02: new dominant common hepatic duct stricture with upstream dilatation; malignancy cannot be excluded.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- PSC with NEW dominant biliary stricture and rising cholestatic labs (ALP 320 -> 480, bili 1.9) — progression; expedited ERCP with cholangiography and biliary brushing cytology indicated.
- Suspected cholangiocarcinoma — SUSPICION ONLY, not tissue-confirmed. MRCP cannot exclude malignancy; ERCP brushings (+/- FISH) are being performed to establish or exclude the diagnosis. Do not document as a confirmed cancer. Elevated CA 19-9 is nonspecific in the setting of cholestasis/stricture.
- Ulcerative colitis — quiescent, continue mesalamine 1.2 g daily. Add mesalamine to the active medication list (was missing; ursodiol only).
- Continue ursodiol 300 mg TID.
- Peri-procedure: no prescription anticoagulant/antiplatelet — patient's 'blood thinner' is OTC fish oil, not a true anticoagulant; no bridging or reversal required. Advise holding fish oil per pre-procedure instructions. No known drug allergies.
- Coordinate ERCP scheduling on an expedited basis; provide bowel/fasting prep and hold instructions; return precautions for jaundice, fever/chills, or right upper quadrant pain (cholangitis).
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your liver-duct condition (PSC) and a new narrowed area seen on your MRI scan
• Rising liver-duct blood tests (alkaline phosphatase and bilirubin)
• A planned procedure called ERCP, where we take a small brush sample of the narrowed duct
• Why we are doing this soon

Important
• The scan cannot tell us for certain what is causing the narrowing. Cancer is one possibility we need to rule out — it is NOT a diagnosis you have been given today. The brush sample is how we find out.

Next steps
• We will schedule your ERCP with brushings promptly; the team will send fasting and preparation instructions and tell you what to stop beforehand.
• Keep taking your ursodiol three times a day and your mesalamine each morning.
• We updated your record to include your mesalamine.
• Your fish oil is not a true blood thinner, but plan to pause it before the procedure per the instructions we send.
• Call right away for yellowing of the eyes or skin, fever or chills, or belly pain.

transcript:
DR: Afternoon, Ms. Baptiste — thanks for coming in. We're planning your follow-up colonoscopy from the polyps back in 2021. Before we schedule, I want to go over a couple of safety things, especially the sleep apnea. How are you doing?
MA: Before the doctor gets started, let me grab your vitals, Ms. Baptiste — height's 5 foot 4, weight's 240 pounds. Blood pressure 148 over 86, heart rate 78, temp's 36.8, breathing 16, and your oxygen's 94 percent on room air.
PT: I'm alright. A little nervous about being put under, to be honest.
DR: That's actually exactly what I want to talk through. Your chart says severe sleep apnea — the sleep study showed a pretty high number — and you're on the CPAP machine. The referral notes say you use it most nights. Tell me honestly, how's it really going with the machine?
PT: Well... I try. But the mask leaks and it wakes me up, so a lot of nights I end up pulling it off after an hour or two. If I'm being straight with you, I probably really keep it on maybe two, three nights a week.
DR: I appreciate you being straight — that matters a lot for the anesthesia. Do you have the card or the download from the machine? Sometimes it tracks the hours.
PT: No, nobody's pulled that off in, oh, over a year. Since the sleep doctor last time.
DR: Okay. So we can't confirm the hours, and by your own account it's a few nights a week, not most. That changes how we plan your sedation — I'm going to have anesthesia review you and we'll do the scope somewhere we can watch your airway closely. Bring the machine the day of.
PT: That's fine. Better safe.
DR: Let me check your medicines. I have losartan for blood pressure — still taking that?
PT: Yes, every day. And the little water pill in the morning too.
DR: A water pill — is that a separate tablet from the losartan?
PT: Separate, yeah, a small white one. Hydro-something.
DR: Good to know — that's not on my list, I'll add it. Any allergies?
PT: Not to medicine. But those latex gloves give me a rash, itchy, every time at the dentist.
DR: Thank you — I'll flag latex so the room's set up for that. We'll get you scheduled and send prep instructions.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Baptiste is a 66-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan surveillance colonoscopy (tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia on 2021 colonoscopy, surveillance due 2026). She has severe obstructive sleep apnea (diagnostic PSG AHI 42, nadir SpO2 81%) and morbid obesity (BMI 41). Although the referral records CPAP use "most nights," on direct interview she reports she wears CPAP only approximately 2-3 nights per week for a few hours at a time due to mask leak and discomfort, and no objective compliance download has been obtained in over a year. She reports taking losartan daily and, in addition, a daily morning diuretic ("water pill," hydrochlorothiazide) not present on the medication list. She reports a rash/pruritus with latex glove exposure, not previously documented as an allergy.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 78, RR 16, BP 148/86, SpO2 94% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 109 kg (240 lb), BMI 41.0. Prior diagnostic polysomnogram (2022-08-30): AHI 42/hr (severe OSA), oxygen nadir 81%. No current CPAP adherence data available.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Surveillance colonoscopy for prior tubular adenomas — indicated; proceed with scheduling.
- Severe OSA (AHI 42) + morbid obesity (BMI 41) with UNVERIFIED and by-report POOR CPAP adherence (2-3 nights/week, no download >1 year): elevated peri-procedural airway/sedation risk. Per protocol this meets the RED (not YELLOW) tier — refer to anesthesia for pre-procedure evaluation and perform the colonoscopy under anesthesia-directed monitored care in a setting equipped for airway rescue. Patient to bring CPAP machine day-of; request updated compliance download from sleep medicine.
- Update medication list: add hydrochlorothiazide (patient-reported daily use); continue losartan.
- Add allergy/intolerance: latex (contact rash/pruritus) — arrange latex-safe environment for procedure.
- Hypertension — BP mildly elevated today; continue current regimen, recheck.
- Bowel prep instructions and pre-procedure hold instructions to follow; monitor electrolytes given diuretic + prep.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Planning your follow-up colonoscopy (from the polyps in 2021)
• Your sleep apnea and how often you use the CPAP machine
• Your blood pressure medicines
• Keeping you safe during sedation

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy and the team will send prep and pre-procedure instructions.
• Because your sleep apnea is severe and the CPAP has been hard to use consistently, the anesthesia team will review you first and we will do the procedure where we can watch your breathing closely. Please bring your CPAP machine the day of the procedure.
• If you can, ask your sleep clinic to download the usage data from your machine before the procedure.
• We updated your record: added your daily water pill (hydrochlorothiazide) and noted a latex allergy (skin rash) so the room is set up latex-free.
• Keep taking your losartan and water pill as usual unless we tell you otherwise for the prep.
• Call us with any questions about the prep or your medicines.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Vogel. I'm the gastroenterologist — your pulmonologist and the hospital team sent you over so we can plan an upper endoscopy for the swallowing trouble. How are you doing today?
PT: Getting by. The breathing's still not back to where it was before the hospital.
NURSE: He's on his 2 liters, sat was 91 when we roomed him — he's five foot nine, weight's one fifty-one today, down from about one sixty-three a couple months ago; BP 128 over 76, heart rate 84, temp 36.7.
DR: Thank you. So tell me about the swallowing.
PT: It's food getting stuck — solid stuff mostly, bread, meat. Feels like it hangs up right here in my chest. Started a couple months ago and it's been getting worse.
DR: Any weight change with that?
PT: Yeah, I've dropped maybe twelve pounds since the spring. Not trying to. Clothes are loose.
DR: Okay, that's important — that's part of why we want to look. Let me go over your medicines. The chart has you on a prednisone taper, down to 10 milligrams. Is that right?
PT: No — lung doctor put me back up. I came down too fast and got worse, so last week he bumped me to 20 a day.
DR: Good to know, 20 milligrams currently. And it says apixaban, 5 milligrams twice a day, for the atrial fibrillation.
PT: Well... I've been taking it once a day, honestly. It's a blood thinner and I got nervous about bleeding, so I cut it back on my own.
DR: I appreciate you being straight with me. Did you take it today?
PT: Yes, this morning, same as always.
DR: Okay. For an endoscopy we usually need the blood thinner held ahead of time, and nobody's set that up yet — I'll coordinate that with your cardiologist and anesthesia.
PT: Whatever you think. One thing — years back I had a bad time with sedation, real groggy and sick for a day after. I don't remember what they gave me.
DR: That's worth flagging — I'll have anesthesia review your old records before we book you. Given your lungs and heart, we'll do this in the hospital with anesthesia present, not the usual suite.
PT: That's fine by me.
DR: I'll get the anesthesia review going and the team will call you with the plan and prep.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Vogel is a 73-year-old man referred to GI clinic for pre-procedure planning of EGD to evaluate dysphagia. He describes progressive solid-food dysphagia over ~2 months with a sensation of food hanging up substernally, accompanied by unintentional weight loss of approximately 12 pounds — both alarm features. He has severe COPD (FEV1 32% predicted) on 2 L/min continuous home oxygen, with a COPD exacerbation admission ~5 weeks ago and incomplete recovery; his pulmonologist increased prednisone back to 20 mg daily about one week ago (chart still lists a taper to 10 mg). He has chronic atrial fibrillation on apixaban but reports self-reducing the dose to once daily (chart: 5 mg BID) out of bleeding concern, and he took a dose this morning. He also reports a hedged history of a prior adverse reaction to sedation (prolonged grogginess and nausea, agent unknown).

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 84, RR 16, BP 128/76, SpO2 91% on 2 L/min O2; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 68.5 kg (151 lb), BMI 22.4 (down from ~73.9 kg/163 lb ~2 months prior). FEV1 32% predicted (2026-02). Hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL (2026-07-02). CXR (2026-06-08): severe emphysema with features of cor pulmonale/pulmonary hypertension.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Dysphagia to solids with unintentional weight loss — alarm features; proceed with EGD to evaluate for stricture, esophagitis, or malignancy.
- HIGH anesthesia/sedation risk: severe COPD on home O2, recent exacerbation, active systemic corticosteroids, chronic afib on DOAC, prior possible sedation intolerance. Refer for RED anesthesia review; perform EGD in hospital-based setting with anesthesia (MAC/GA as they advise); consider peri-procedure stress-dose steroids.
- Anticoagulation: reconcile apixaban — chart 5 mg BID vs patient-reported once daily; coordinate DOAC hold and afib bridging decision with cardiology/anesthesia before scheduling. Patient took a dose this morning; no hold plan was in place.
- Medication reconciliation: update prednisone to 20 mg daily per pulmonology; correct apixaban dosing per patient.
- Add to allergy/adverse-reaction review: reported prior adverse sedation reaction — obtain prior anesthesia records.
- Continue tiotropium, budesonide/formoterol, PRN albuterol; optimize pulmonary status pre-procedure.
- Team to contact patient with anesthesia review outcome, hold instructions, and prep.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Trouble swallowing solid foods and recent weight loss
• Planning an upper endoscopy (EGD) to find the cause
• Your breathing (COPD), oxygen, and recent hospital stay
• Your heart rhythm (atrial fibrillation) and blood thinner

Next steps
• Because of your lungs and heart, your endoscopy will be done in the hospital with an anesthesia team. A special anesthesia review will be arranged before we schedule it.
• Do NOT stop or change your blood thinner (apixaban) on your own — we will give you exact instructions on when to hold it before the procedure. Please tell us it has been taken today.
• We updated your records: prednisone is now 20 mg a day (per your lung doctor), and your blood thinner dose needs to be confirmed.
• We noted your past bad reaction to sedation so anesthesia can review it.
• Keep using your inhalers and home oxygen as usual.
• Call for worsening breathing, chest pain, inability to swallow liquids or saliva, black stools, or bleeding.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Al-Rashid. Thanks for coming in. Your stool screening test came back positive, so we want to plan a colonoscopy to take a proper look. Before anything, I want to go carefully through your heart history and your medicines, because there's a lot going on there.
MA: Before the doctor goes on, let me get your vitals — you're 5 foot 4 and 150 pounds today. Temp 36.8, heart rate 60, breathing 14, blood pressure 118 over 72, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Yes, my heart keeps everyone busy. I have the weak heart — they told me the pumping is about twenty-five percent — and I have the defibrillator, the device in my chest, since 2024.
DR: That's exactly what I have here. And you follow with your heart rhythm doctor for the device?
PT: Every few months. They checked it in the spring, said it was working fine.
DR: Good. Any chest pain, more shortness of breath, swelling, or fainting lately?
PT: No, actually I've been pretty steady. No worse than usual.
DR: That's reassuring. Now the medicine list I have is the Entresto, the carvedilol, spironolactone, and a baby aspirin. Is that still right?
PT: Mostly. The Entresto and carvedilol, yes, every day. But the water pill — the spironolactone — the cardiologist cut that in half last month. My potassium ran high, so I'm on the twelve-and-a-half now, not the twenty-five.
DR: Thank you, that's important — I'll correct that. And the aspirin?
PT: Yes, the little aspirin every day. And now the new blood thinner too.
DR: Wait — a new blood thinner? Tell me about that.
PT: About six weeks ago they found my heart was in that irregular rhythm — the atrial fibrillation. So they started me on apixaban, the twice-a-day one. I take that with the aspirin. I just think of them both as my blood thinners.
DR: I'm really glad you mentioned it, because that changes how we prepare you. The aspirin and the apixaban are two different things — the apixaban especially, we'll need to plan when to hold it around the procedure, and coordinate with your heart team.
PT: I see. I didn't realize it mattered that much.
DR: It matters a lot. Here's the plan: we'll schedule the colonoscopy, but first I'll get anesthesia to review you because of your heart, and your device doctor will give us a plan for the defibrillator during the procedure — usually a magnet or a reprogram. And I'll write out exactly which days to hold the apixaban. The aspirin we'll likely continue.
PT: Okay. That makes me feel better that you're checking all of it.
DR: That's the job. The team will call you with the prep and the medication schedule.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Al-Rashid is a 58-year-old woman referred to GI for a positive screening FIT (2026-06-28). She is asymptomatic from a GI standpoint — denies overt rectal bleeding, melena, weight loss, or change in bowel habits. Extensive cardiac history: ischemic cardiomyopathy with HFrEF (EF 25% on 2026-04 echo), single-chamber ICD implanted 2024, CAD. She denies current chest pain, worsening dyspnea, edema, or syncope and reports being at her cardiac baseline.

Medication reconciliation reveals two chart discrepancies. (1) NEW, not on the GI chart: she was started on apixaban 5 mg twice daily approximately 6 weeks ago by cardiology for newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation. She refers to both her aspirin and apixaban as her "blood thinners." (2) Spironolactone was reduced by cardiology from 25 mg to 12.5 mg daily ~1 month ago for hyperkalemia (K 5.4); she is taking the lower dose. She confirms ongoing sacubitril/valsartan, carvedilol, and low-dose aspirin 81 mg.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 60, RR 14, BP 118/72, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 68 kg (150 lb), BMI 25.6. Recent data — LVEF 25% (echo 2026-04-30); NT-proBNP 1420 pg/mL, K 5.4 mmol/L, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, Hgb 12.6 g/dL (2026-06-05); FIT positive (2026-06-28). Single-chamber ICD in situ, last interrogation spring 2026 reported normal. Appears euvolemic and at cardiac baseline by history.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Positive FIT — plan diagnostic colonoscopy with anticipated polypectomy.
- HIGH-ACUITY cardiac status (EF 25%, ICD): RED tier confirmed. Mandatory anesthesia/pre-anesthesia review prior to procedure. Obtain EP device-management plan for periprocedural ICD (magnet vs reprogram per policy).
- NEW anticoagulation: apixaban 5 mg BID for new atrial fibrillation — NOT previously on GI chart. Plan periprocedural hold schedule for apixaban in coordination with cardiology/EP; no bridging anticipated for AFib with DOAC but confirm with cardiology given EF. Add atrial fibrillation and apixaban to problem/med list.
- Low-dose aspirin 81 mg — cardioprotective; plan to continue through colonoscopy/polypectomy. Explicitly distinguished from apixaban.
- Update med list: spironolactone 12.5 mg daily (not 25 mg); note hyperkalemia — recheck electrolytes before prep given bowel-prep-related shifts and renal function.
- Continue sacubitril/valsartan and carvedilol.
- Team to contact patient with bowel prep, anesthesia scheduling, and explicit medication hold instructions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your stool test (FIT) was positive, so we are planning a colonoscopy.
• Your heart history: weak heart pump (EF 25%) and your defibrillator (ICD).
• Your medicines, including a new blood thinner (apixaban) started for an irregular heartbeat.

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy. Because of your heart, anesthesia will review you first, and your heart-rhythm doctor will give a plan for your defibrillator during the procedure.
• We will send you exact instructions for when to STOP your apixaban before the procedure. Do not stop it on your own — wait for our instructions.
• You will likely keep taking your baby aspirin — we will confirm in your instructions.
• We updated your record: spironolactone is now 12.5 mg (the lower dose your cardiologist set), and we added your atrial fibrillation and apixaban.
• Keep taking your Entresto and carvedilol as usual.
• Call your care team or seek care for chest pain, severe shortness of breath, fainting, or any bleeding.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Stanton. I'm the gastroenterologist. You were referred for an upper endoscopy and a colonoscopy to figure out why your blood count is low. Before we schedule anything, I want to go through everything carefully — there are a few things here I need to get right.
MA: Before we dive in, let me get your vitals down, Mr. Stanton — height's five foot ten, weight's three seventy-seven, blood pressure 128 over 80, heart rate 84, breathing 16, temp 36.8, and your oxygen's 94 percent on room air.
PT: Sure. Yeah, they said I'm anemic. I've been really dragging, tired all the time.
DR: Any bleeding you've noticed? Blood in the stool, or dark stools?
PT: Now that you mention it — yeah, my stools have been black. Kind of tarry, the last few weeks. I didn't think much of it.
DR: That's important, that actually points us upstream, toward the stomach — good that you told me. Let me go through your medications. The chart has you on semaglutide, the weekly weight-loss shot?
PT: Yep, the Wegovy. I do the injection once a week.
DR: When was your most recent dose?
PT: This past Wednesday.
DR: Okay — that's key. That medication slows how fast your stomach empties, so for the endoscopy we have to hold it well ahead of time so your stomach's empty for the sedation. Nobody's mentioned that to you?
PT: No, first I'm hearing of it.
DR: We'll build the timing around that. Now the referral mentions you're on a blood thinner — I want to make sure I have that right.
PT: A blood thinner? No... the only thing like that is fish oil. The omega-3 capsules. That's it.
DR: Good — that's over-the-counter, that's not a true blood thinner, so no special clearance needed for that. The chart also lists sleep apnea, on CPAP. Are you using the CPAP machine?
PT: No. They gave me the prescription but I never got it. I couldn't deal with the mask, so I declined it. Never used it.
DR: Understood — so the apnea's untreated. And back in 2022, your gallbladder surgery — do you remember anything about the breathing tube?
PT: Oh, yeah. They told me afterward they had a lot of trouble getting the tube in.
NURSE: I'll flag anesthesia for all of that.
DR: Please. Given the airway history, the untreated apnea, and the body weight, this needs to be done with anesthesia present, not routine sedation. Let me set that up.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Stanton is a 51-year-old man referred to GI clinic for pre-procedure consultation and planning of EGD + colonoscopy to evaluate iron-deficiency anemia. He reports several weeks of fatigue and, on direct questioning, intermittent black, tarry stools over the past few weeks (previously undocumented) — concerning for an upper GI bleeding source. He confirms weekly semaglutide 2.4 mg (Wegovy), with most recent injection this past Wednesday (~5 days ago); he was never counseled to hold it before an endoscopic procedure. He denies any prescription anticoagulant or antiplatelet; the only agent is OTC fish oil (omega-3). He reports his obstructive sleep apnea is untreated — CPAP was prescribed but he declined it and has never used it. He confirms a difficult airway during 2022 laparoscopic cholecystectomy ('trouble getting the tube in').

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 84, RR 16, BP 128/80, SpO2 94% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 171 kg (377 lb), BMI 54.0. BMI 54 (class III obesity). Recent labs (2026-06-27): hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL (low), ferritin 11 ug/L (low), hematocrit 30.1% (low) — iron-deficiency anemia. Chart: documented difficult intubation 2022 (Cormack-Lehane grade 3, video laryngoscopy required); severe OSA (AHI 42).

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Iron-deficiency anemia with new melena — proceed with EGD + colonoscopy; melena raises concern for upper GI source, EGD prioritized. Start oral iron and monitor; type-and-screen and CBC pre-procedure.
- TRIPLE PERIPROCEDURAL ANESTHESIA RISK: BMI 54 + untreated OSA (declined CPAP) + documented difficult airway (Cormack-Lehane 3, 2022). Refer to anesthesiology; procedure to be done with anesthesiologist-directed care and difficult-airway precautions (video laryngoscopy available), NOT routine moderate sedation.
- GLP-1 periprocedural timing: hold weekly semaglutide per current guidance ahead of the procedure (last dose Wednesday); coordinate procedure date so the weekly agent is held with adequate lead time, and plan clear-liquid diet/extended fast; anesthesia to assess aspiration risk. Consider point-of-care gastric ultrasound day-of.
- Correct chart discrepancies: OSA is UNTREATED (declined CPAP) — update problem list. 'Blood thinner' in referral is OTC fish oil, NOT an anticoagulant — no anticoagulation clearance/bridging needed.
- Bowel prep and scheduling to follow; return precautions for large-volume bleeding, hematemesis, lightheadedness.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Low blood count (iron-deficiency anemia) and why we need to look inside
• Black/tarry stools you've been having
• Planning your upper endoscopy (EGD) and colonoscopy
• Your sleep apnea, your weight, and a past problem with the breathing tube — important for a safe procedure
• Your weekly weight-loss injection (semaglutide) and the fish oil supplement

Next steps
• We will schedule your EGD and colonoscopy; the team will send prep and diet instructions.
• Because of your sleep apnea, weight, and prior difficult breathing-tube history, your procedure will be done with an anesthesia doctor present for safety. We are referring you to anesthesia.
• Do NOT take your weekly semaglutide (Wegovy) injection close to the procedure — we will tell you exactly which week to hold it and how long to fast. This keeps your stomach empty for safe sedation.
• Your fish oil is fine and is not a blood thinner — no special stopping needed for it.
• We updated your record: sleep apnea is untreated (you declined CPAP), and the 'blood thinner' note was actually fish oil.
• Start iron as directed for the low blood count.
• Call right away for vomiting blood, a lot of blood in the stool, dizziness, or fainting.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Beaumont — and you must be her daughter, thank you for coming. I'm the gastroenterologist. Your mom was referred because of a low blood count and some blood found in a stool test. Before we talk about any procedure, I want to understand the whole picture. How is she doing?
MA: Before you got started, doctor — I roomed her and got her vitals. She's 5 foot 2 and 104 pounds today, blood pressure 118 over 70, heart rate 80, temp 98.1, respirations 16, and oxygen 97 percent on room air.
FAMILY: She's frail, doctor. She's in assisted living, uses her walker, and her memory's not good — moderate dementia. I handle her medications and I have healthcare power of attorney.
PT: I'm all right. I get tired. I don't remember about any blood, dear — did they find something?
DR: The stool test did show some blood, and the anemia is why your daughter and your doctor were concerned. Let me confirm her medicines. The chart lists apixaban two-point-five milligrams twice a day. Is that right?
FAMILY: Let me check the bottle — I brought them. No... this says apixaban five milligrams, twice a day. She takes the five.
DR: That's important — thank you. The chart has the lower dose, but she's actually on the full five twice daily. I'll flag that; it changes how we'd manage bleeding risk, and I'll coordinate with her cardiologist. Anything else she takes?
FAMILY: The donepezil in the evening for her memory, and mirtazapine at night. And she takes a fish oil — that's a blood thinner too, isn't it?
DR: Fish oil is an over-the-counter omega-3 supplement — it's not a true blood thinner like the apixaban, so I wouldn't count it the same way, but I'll note it. The apixaban is the one that matters for bleeding.
FAMILY: Okay. And she's lost weight — about ten pounds over three months, even on that mirtazapine that's supposed to help her eat. She just picks at her food.
DR: That weight loss matters, and I've noted it. So here's where we are. A colonoscopy could find the source of the bleeding — but in someone who is 88, frail, with dementia and on a full-dose blood thinner, the sedation, the prep, and holding her anticoagulation all carry real risk. The referral asked us not to just schedule it, and I agree.
FAMILY: The family wants everything checked, though.
DR: I hear that, and it's a loving instinct. What I'd like to do is not book the scope today, but sit down with you, her cardiologist, and her primary doctor about what she'd want and what the results would change. I also want to be sure she can take part in that decision. Can we do that before committing to anything?
FAMILY: Yes. That's fair. I just want to do right by her.
DR: We will. I'll set up that conversation and we'll decide together.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Beaumont is an 88-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation (on apixaban), moderate dementia, and frailty, referred from primary care for evaluation of new microcytic anemia (Hgb 9.1, down from 11.8) with a positive fecal occult blood test. She resides in assisted living and ambulates with a walker. History is obtained primarily from her daughter, who holds healthcare POA, as the patient has moderate dementia and provides a hedged, unreliable history (she does not recall any bleeding). Daughter reports approximately 10 lb unintentional weight loss over 3 months despite mirtazapine, with reduced appetite. Family expresses a wish to 'have everything checked.'

Medication reconciliation (from daughter with pill bottles): apixaban is actually 5 mg PO BID per the current pharmacy bottle — the chart lists 2.5 mg BID (discrepancy, see plan). Donepezil 10 mg every evening and mirtazapine 15 mg nightly confirmed. Daughter additionally reports OTC fish oil, which she characterized as a 'blood thinner'; this is an over-the-counter omega-3 supplement, not a clinically significant anticoagulant.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 80, RR 16, BP 118/70, SpO2 97% on RA; Ht 157 cm (5'2"), Wt 47.2 kg (104 lb), BMI 19.1 (prior Wt ~51.7 kg / 114 lb three months ago, consistent with ~10 lb loss). Labs (2026-07-08): Hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL, Hematocrit 28.6%, MCV 76 fL, ferritin 11 ug/L (microcytic, iron-deficient pattern); creatinine 1.2 mg/dL. Fecal occult blood positive (2026-06-28). Appears frail; accompanied by daughter/POA.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- New iron-deficiency anemia with guaiac/FOBT-positive stool and unintentional weight loss in an 88-year-old — differential includes occult GI malignancy or other GI blood loss. Colonoscopy is the diagnostic study that would answer the question.
- HOWEVER, procedure is NOT being scheduled today. Given age 88, frailty, moderate dementia, and full-dose anticoagulation, the risks of bowel prep, sedation/anesthesia, and periprocedural anticoagulation interruption are substantial. Per referral flag, a goals-of-care and risk/benefit discussion must precede any decision.
  - Arrange a shared decision-making conversation with the daughter/POA, cardiology, and referring PCP regarding whether findings would change management and what the patient would want.
  - Assess decision-making capacity for informed consent to sedation/procedure; involve POA as surrogate as appropriate.
- Anticoagulation discrepancy: chart lists apixaban 2.5 mg BID but patient is taking 5 mg BID per pill bottle. Reconcile with cardiology — clarify intended dose (note low body weight ~47 kg and age >=80 would ordinarily favor the 2.5 mg reduced dose) and define any periprocedural hold plan only if procedure is pursued.
- Update medication list: correct apixaban dose; add OTC fish oil/omega-3 (clarified as supplement, not therapeutic anticoagulant).
- Iron-deficiency anemia — check iron studies/reticulocytes as needed; consider iron repletion (oral vs IV) independent of procedure decision; monitor Hgb; transfusion threshold per symptoms.
- Weight loss — flag to PCP; nutritional support in assisted living.
- Continue donepezil and mirtazapine.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• A low blood count (anemia) and blood found in a stool test
• Recent weight loss
• Whether a colonoscopy is the right choice for your mother right now
• Her medicines, including her blood thinner

What we decided today
• We are NOT scheduling the colonoscopy yet. Because of your mother's age, frailty, memory condition, and her blood thinner, the prep, sedation, and stopping the blood thinner all carry real risks.
• We will set up a conversation with you (as her healthcare decision-maker), her heart doctor, and her regular doctor to decide together what is best and what she would want.

Medicine notes
• Your mother's records showed apixaban 2.5 mg twice a day, but her bottle shows 5 mg twice a day. We will correct this and check with her heart doctor.
• The fish oil is an over-the-counter supplement, not a true blood thinner like the apixaban. We noted it.
• Keep giving donepezil in the evening and mirtazapine at night.

Next steps
• We will arrange the family/care-team discussion before any procedure.
• We may check iron and treat the low blood count regardless of the decision.
• Please call for any heavy bleeding, black stools, dizziness, fainting, or new weakness.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Thibodeaux. You're here so we can plan an upper endoscopy for the reflux and go over a few things beforehand. How are you doing?
MA: Before we get started, let me get your vitals down — height's 5 foot 10, weight 202 pounds, temp is 36.8, heart rate 74, respirations 14, blood pressure 128 over 78, and oxygen's 98 percent on room air.
PT: Doing alright, thanks. The heartburn's the main thing — it's most days, even on the omeprazole.
DR: And you're taking the omeprazole every day, the 20 milligram?
PT: Every morning, yeah. It helps but it doesn't knock it all out.
DR: Okay, that's useful — that's part of why we want to look. Now, the thing I really want to nail down before we sedate you: your chart has an allergy to propofol. Tell me what happened.
PT: Yeah, I'm allergic to propofol. When I had my shoulder done in 2021, I had a reaction — I was told it was anaphylaxis. That word stuck with me. I've told everybody since then that I'm anaphylactic to it.
DR: I appreciate you flagging it — that's exactly the kind of thing we can't get wrong. Do you remember what actually happened to your body? Trouble breathing, throat closing, blood pressure dropping?
PT: Honestly, no. I was out. When I woke up they said I'd broken out in hives and they gave me something for it. Nobody said my breathing stopped or anything like that. But 'anaphylaxis' is the word I remember hearing.
NURSE: I pulled the 2021 anesthesia note — it says urticaria, possible allergy, unconfirmed. No airway or blood pressure issues documented.
DR: That's an important distinction. Hives that were never confirmed is a very different situation than true anaphylaxis, and it changes what sedation is safe for you. I'm not going to dismiss it, but I'm also not going to just carry 'anaphylaxis' forward.
PT: So what does that mean for the scope?
DR: Before your procedure day, I'm going to have Anesthesia review that 2021 record and adjudicate this — decide whether propofol is genuinely off the table or whether we plan an alternative sedation. We won't schedule sedation until that's settled.
PT: That's fair. I'd rather know for sure.
DR: Exactly. One more — the asthma. Any recent flares?
PT: No, I use the albuterol maybe once or twice a month. Nothing lately.
DR: Good. I'll get the referral to Anesthesia in and the team will follow up.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Thibodeaux is a 46-year-old man presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consultation to plan an EGD for persistent GERD. He reports near-daily heartburn despite adherence to omeprazole 20 mg daily. He carries a reported allergy to propofol, which he verbally describes as 'anaphylaxis' sustained during a 2021 shoulder arthroscopy; however, he cannot recall any respiratory, airway, or hemodynamic symptoms and states he was told only that he 'broke out in hives' and was treated. The 2021 pre-anesthesia record on file documents scattered urticaria without bronchospasm, hypotension, angioedema, or airway compromise, with multiple agents administered and propofol not definitively implicated — labeled 'possible allergy, UNCONFIRMED.' Asthma is mild/intermittent; albuterol used ~1-2x/month with no recent flares.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 74, RR 14, BP 128/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 91.5 kg (202 lb), BMI 28.9. Hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL (2026-07-02). Reviewed 2021 anesthesiology pre-anesthesia note (urticaria, possible/unconfirmed).

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Persistent GERD despite PPI — plan diagnostic EGD.
- Reported propofol allergy — DISCREPANCY: patient states 'anaphylaxis'; sole source document (2021) describes unconfirmed isolated urticaria with no anaphylaxis features and propofol not definitively implicated. Do NOT day-of adjudicate. Refer to Anesthesiology PRIOR to procedure to review the 2021 record and determine whether propofol is contraindicated or whether an alternative sedation plan is required. Sedation not to be scheduled until adjudicated.
- Asthma, mild intermittent, well controlled — continue albuterol PRN; relevant to airway/sedation planning; no changes.

prior_notes.text: New patient consult for progressive dysphagia to solids and liquids over ~9 months with regurgitation of undigested food and nocturnal cough. Barium esophagram showed dilated esophagus with a 'bird-beak' tapering at the gastroesophageal junction and delayed emptying. High-resolution esophageal manometry: elevated integrated relaxation pressure with 100% failed peristalsis and pan-esophageal pressurization — consistent with achalasia, Chicago classification type II. EGD ruled out mechanical obstruction/pseudoachalasia. Weight down ~8 lb since symptom onset. Discussed treatment options including pneumatic dilation, POEM, and Heller myotomy. Plan: trial pneumatic dilation; refer for POEM evaluation if inadequate response.

Endoscopic pneumatic (balloon) dilation of the lower esophageal sphincter, 30 mm, for achalasia type II. Tolerated procedure; no immediate perforation on post-dilation esophagram. Patient reported transient improvement in swallowing for ~2 weeks followed by recurrence of dysphagia and regurgitation. Given failure of dilation and ongoing nutritional decline, refer for peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM).

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Cho — thanks for coming in. This is the visit where we go over the referral for the POEM, the procedure to loosen the muscle at the bottom of your esophagus, and I ask you a lot of questions. How are things?
MA: Before you get started, doctor — I roomed Ms. Cho and got her vitals: height 5 foot 6, weight 114 pounds, temp 36.8, blood pressure 118 over 74, heart rate 76, respirations 14, oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Honestly not great. Swallowing has gotten worse. I'm basically on soups and smoothies now — anything solid comes right back up.
DR: I'm sorry. The referral mentions about twelve pounds down. Does that sound right?
PT: It's more than that now. I've lost around twenty pounds since the spring, and it's still going. My clothes are hanging off me.
DR: Okay, that's important — we'll flag the nutrition side. Let me go through your medications. The chart actually lists nothing. Is that right, you take nothing?
PT: Well — I do bite one of those nifedipine capsules under my tongue before meals. A previous GI doctor gave them to me to help the food go down. And I take omeprazole from the drugstore most days for heartburn.
DR: That's really useful — neither of those is in our record, so I'll add them. Do you take any blood thinner?
PT: Yes, a blood thinner — well, fish oil. The omega-3 capsules. My sister said they thin your blood.
DR: Ah, that's a good thing to sort out. Fish oil is a supplement, not a prescription blood thinner — you're not on warfarin or anything like that?
PT: No, nothing like that. Just the fish oil.
DR: Got it. We'll still have you hold the fish oil before the procedure. Now the referral says you had one dilation that didn't hold. Anything else done before that?
PT: Yeah — a couple years ago at another hospital they did an injection, botox I think, into the muscle. It helped for a month, maybe.
DR: Thank you — that matters for how we do the POEM. And allergies? Nothing's listed.
PT: Penicillin gave me a rash as a kid.
DR: I'll add that. So: we'll expedite the POEM for the weight loss, do the pre-op liquid diet and a screen per protocol, and reconcile all this. The team will call with prep details.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Cho is a 36-year-old woman presenting for pre-procedure consultation for planned peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM). She has achalasia, Chicago classification type II, confirmed on high-resolution manometry (2026-02), with a barium esophagram showing bird-beak tapering and delayed emptying. She underwent one endoscopic pneumatic dilation (30 mm, 2026-04) with only ~2 weeks of relief. She now reports worsening dysphagia to solids and liquids, tolerating mostly liquids, with regurgitation of undigested food. She reports ~20 lb weight loss over the past ~3 months that is ongoing — greater than the 12 lb documented in the referral.

Medication reconciliation revealed several items absent from the chart: she takes nifedipine, biting a capsule sublingually before meals (prescribed by a prior GI for dysphagia), and OTC omeprazole 20 mg most days for heartburn. When asked about a blood thinner she reported taking a 'blood thinner,' which on clarification is an over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3) supplement, not a prescription anticoagulant. She additionally reports a prior botulinum toxin injection to the LES ~2 years ago at an outside facility, not previously documented. She reports a penicillin allergy (childhood rash), not previously on file.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 76, RR 14, BP 118/74, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 168 cm (5'6"), Wt 51.9 kg (114 lb), BMI 18.4. Labs — albumin 3.3 g/dL (2026-07-10), hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL. Manometry: IRP 22 mmHg, 100% failed peristalsis with pan-esophageal pressurization (type II). Esophagram: bird-beak GEJ with delayed emptying.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Achalasia type II, refractory to pneumatic dilation, with ongoing nutritional decline — appropriate POEM candidate; expedite given weight loss and low BMI/albumin.
- Nutrition: ~20 lb loss (understated in referral), BMI 18.4, albumin 3.3 — nutrition referral; monitor.
- Pre-op protocol: extended clear-liquid diet 48 h prior (esophageal food stasis); Candida esophagitis screen per POEM protocol.
- Medication reconciliation: ADD nifedipine (sublingual pre-meal) — hold/coordinate with anesthesia given hypotension risk under sedation; ADD OTC omeprazole 20 mg daily; document fish oil (omega-3) supplement and HOLD prior to POEM per bleeding protocol. Patient is NOT on a prescription anticoagulant.
- Document prior LES botulinum toxin injection (~2 yr ago) — anticipate submucosal fibrosis and increased POEM technical difficulty; counsel and plan accordingly.
- Add allergy: penicillin (childhood rash) — relevant to peri-procedure antibiotics.
- Anesthesia clearance and POEM scheduling; team to provide prep instructions and return precautions.

Findings: a single flat, slightly depressed 25 mm lesion in the gastric antrum with abnormal surface pattern on narrow-band imaging; margins marked. Biopsies obtained. Remainder of exam unremarkable. transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Dombrowski. We're here today to plan the resection of that lesion in your stomach. Before we schedule the ESD, I want to go carefully through your medicines, because this is a procedure where bleeding is the main thing we manage.
MA: Before the doctor gets into it, let me get your vitals down — you're 5 foot 10, 185 pounds today. Temp 36.8, pulse 72, breathing 14, blood pressure 132 over 80, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Sure. Whatever you need to know.
DR: The chart shows you take a baby aspirin, 81 milligrams. Remind me why you were started on that — did you ever have a heart attack, a stent, or bypass surgery?
PT: No, nothing like that. My regular doctor put me on it a few years back, just as a precaution. For the heart, just in case. My heart's never given me trouble.
DR: That's an important distinction. Because it's only precautionary and you've never had heart disease, we can safely pause it before the procedure. If you'd had a stent I'd need to loop in cardiology first, but that's not your situation.
PT: Makes sense.
DR: Are you on any blood thinners?
PT: Yeah, actually — I'm on a blood thinner.
DR: Okay, tell me exactly what that is. What's the name, is it a prescription?
PT: Well... it's the fish oil. The omega-3. I take it every day. My neighbor said it thins the blood.
DR: Ah — good that you mentioned it, but that's a supplement, not a prescription blood thinner. It's not the same as warfarin or one of the newer anticoagulants. We'll still have you hold the fish oil for a bit beforehand, but it doesn't need special clearance.
PT: Oh. Okay, good.
DR: Anything else you take, even over the counter?
PT: I take ibuprofen for my knee most days. And the amlodipine every morning for my pressure.
DR: Both worth noting — the ibuprofen we'll hold, the blood-pressure pill you keep taking. Any drug allergies?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: Perfect. Let's get you scheduled and I'll send the exact hold instructions.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Dombrowski is a 69-year-old man presenting to GI clinic for pre-procedure consultation prior to endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD) of a 25 mm gastric antral lesion showing high-grade dysplasia on biopsy (EGD 2026-06-02). Staging EUS (2026-06-23) demonstrated no submucosal invasion, favorable for en-bloc endoscopic resection. Visit focused on bleeding-risk and antithrombotic review for this high-bleed-risk resection.

Medication reconciliation: He takes low-dose aspirin 81 mg, which he confirms is for primary prevention only — he has never had myocardial infarction, coronary stent, or bypass, and it was started years ago by his PCP as a precaution. When asked about blood thinners he initially reported being 'on a blood thinner'; on clarification this is over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3), not a prescription anticoagulant. He additionally reports near-daily over-the-counter ibuprofen 400 mg for knee osteoarthritis (not previously documented) and amlodipine 5 mg each morning for hypertension (not on the chart medication list). No known drug allergies.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 132/80, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 84 kg (185 lb), BMI 26.5. Pre-procedure labs (2026-07-08): hemoglobin 13.9 g/dL, platelets 244 x10^3/uL, INR 1.0, creatinine 0.98 mg/dL — all within acceptable range for endoscopic resection. Pathology (2026-06-05): gastric antral adenoma with high-grade dysplasia, no invasive carcinoma in sampled tissue.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Gastric antral high-grade dysplasia, no submucosal invasion on EUS — proceed with elective ESD (en-bloc resection).
- Antiplatelet: aspirin is primary prevention with no coronary artery disease — HOLD per high-bleed-risk resection protocol; no cardiology clearance required as there is no secondary-prevention indication.
- Reconcile chart: 'blood thinner' clarified as OTC fish oil (supplement, not anticoagulant) — hold pre-procedure; no anticoagulation bridging or anesthesia anticoag clearance needed.
- Add undocumented OTC ibuprofen — hold NSAID pre-procedure per bleeding-risk protocol.
- Add amlodipine 5 mg daily to medication list; continue, take morning of procedure with sip of water.
- Hypertension — continue home regimen.
- Labs acceptable (Hgb 13.9, plt 244, INR 1.0). Schedule ESD; nursing to send exact medication-hold and diet instructions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Planning your stomach procedure (ESD) to remove the abnormal area
• Which medicines to stop before the procedure so bleeding risk is lower
• Your aspirin, fish oil, ibuprofen, and blood-pressure pill

Next steps
• We will schedule your ESD; the team will send the exact dates and instructions.
• Stop your baby aspirin before the procedure — it is only a precaution for you and is safe to pause. You do NOT need a heart doctor's sign-off.
• Stop the fish oil and the ibuprofen before the procedure (the team will tell you exactly when).
• Keep taking your amlodipine for blood pressure, including the morning of the procedure with a small sip of water.
• We added the amlodipine and ibuprofen to your record and confirmed you have no known drug allergies.
• Your pre-procedure blood tests looked good.
• Call us with any questions before your procedure date.

prior_notes.text: New consult referred from ENT/Radiation Oncology. 57-year-old woman s/p concurrent chemoradiation for T2 laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma completed 06/2023, now in remission. Progressive solid-food dysphagia over the preceding 2 months. Barium esophagram and EGD demonstrate a focal proximal-to-mid esophageal stricture consistent with radiation injury; lumen ~7 mm, non-traversable with standard adult scope on first pass. Anatomy noted to be difficult secondary to post-radiation changes and altered upper airway. Plan: enroll in serial dilation program under monitored anesthesia care (MAC). No anticoagulants or antiplatelets. Counseled on aspiration precautions.

Serial esophageal dilation #3 for radiation stricture. MAC anesthesia, uneventful. Stricture in proximal esophagus dilated with wire-guided (Savary) bougies to 15 mm in graded fashion; moderate resistance, no perforation, minimal mucosal trauma. Post-procedure tolerated sips. Difficult but stable anatomy. Plan: continue serial program, return in ~10-12 weeks or sooner if recurrent solid-food dysphagia. No antithrombotic agents; none to hold.
