transcript:
DR: Good morning, Renee. We're meeting today to go over the plan for your next esophageal dilation — this would be number four in the series. How have things been since April?
MA: Before we get started, I've got your rooming vitals — height 5 foot 4, weight 129 pounds, temp 36.7, heart rate 76, respirations 14, blood pressure 122 over 76, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Pretty good for a while, but the last couple of weeks the food's been catching again. Bread, chicken — it sticks and I have to wash it down. It's the same feeling as before.
DR: Okay, that's helpful. Any weight change?
PT: I think I've dropped a few pounds — maybe four? I've been eating softer things and smaller amounts because I'm nervous about it getting stuck.
DR: Thank you, that tells me the narrowing is coming back, which is exactly why we do these on a schedule. And the cancer side of things — you're still clear with ENT?
PT: Yes, all my checkups have been clean. They said remission and I just keep the surveillance appointments.
DR: That's great news. Now let me go through medications and allergies before the procedure. My list actually shows nothing on file for you. Is that right?
PT: Well — I do take fish oil every day. And I read that fish oil is a blood thinner, so I'm worried I need to stop it before the scope. Nobody's told me what to do.
DR: I'm glad you brought it up. Over-the-counter fish oil isn't a true blood thinner like warfarin or the newer ones — it doesn't meaningfully affect your bleeding for a dilation. You're not on any actual anticoagulant or aspirin?
PT: No, nothing like that. Just the fish oil and a multivitamin.
DR: Perfect, then there's nothing you need to hold for the bleeding side. I'll note the fish oil in your chart. Any drug allergies?
PT: Not a true allergy, but codeine makes me horribly nauseous and I throw up. I always mention it.
DR: Good to know — I'll flag codeine as an intolerance so anesthesia and the recovery team steer around it. Your prior three dilations went smoothly under sedation, and we'll plan the same monitored anesthesia this time given your anatomy.
PT: That sounds fine. It's always been a bit tricky but okay.
DR: Exactly. We'll get number four scheduled and send you the prep and fasting instructions.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Fontaine is a 60-year-old woman presenting for pre-procedure consultation to plan serial esophageal dilation #4 for a radiation-induced esophageal stricture (s/p concurrent chemoradiation for laryngeal carcinoma 2023, in remission). She reports recurrent solid-food dysphagia over the past 2-3 weeks (bread, meat sticking), the same quality as prior episodes, with an estimated ~4 lb weight loss from reduced/softer intake. Laryngeal cancer remains in remission with clear ENT surveillance. She takes daily OTC fish oil and a multivitamin; she believed fish oil was a 'blood thinner' requiring a pre-procedure hold. She denies aspirin, antiplatelet, or anticoagulant use. Reports codeine intolerance (severe nausea/vomiting), not previously documented.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 76, RR 14, BP 122/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 58.5 kg (129 lb), BMI 22.0 (prior 60.3 kg on 04/2026, ~4 lb loss). Recent labs (2026-07-10): hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, albumin 3.7 g/dL. Weight today 58.5 kg. No active bleeding. Prior three dilations (most recent 04/2026, Savary to 15 mm) uneventful under MAC; anatomy noted difficult from post-radiation changes.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Recurrent radiation esophageal stricture with interval solid-food dysphagia and mild weight loss — proceed with serial esophageal dilation #4 under monitored anesthesia care; known difficult post-radiation anatomy, graded dilation planned.
- Medication reconciliation: no prescription medications; documents daily OTC fish oil (omega-3) and multivitamin. OTC fish oil is not a therapeutic anticoagulant — no hold required for the procedure. No aspirin/antiplatelet/anticoagulant on board; no antithrombotic to manage.
- Add codeine as an intolerance (severe nausea/vomiting) — communicate to anesthesia/recovery for analgesia planning.
- Laryngeal carcinoma in remission — continue ENT surveillance; confirmed.
- Nutrition: monitor weight; encourage caloric intake pending dilation.
- Scheduling, fasting/prep instructions, and aspiration precautions to follow; return precautions for inability to swallow secretions, chest pain, or fever.

transcript:
DR: Good afternoon, Mr. Kagame — thanks for coming in, and hello to you too. You're here so we can plan the feeding tube, the PEG. I've read the notes from neurology and pulmonary. How are things going with swallowing?
MA: Before we get started, let me get your measurements down — height's five foot nine, weight's one thirty-five today, temp 36.8, heart rate 76, breathing 16, blood pressure 122 over 76, and your oxygen is 95 percent on room air.
PT: It's harder. Solid food I've mostly given up. Liquids are the tricky part now — I cough a lot.
FAMILY: That's what worries me. Last week he had a real choking spell with water, went on for a while, and then that night he ran a bit of a fever. It scared us both.
DR: I'm glad you mentioned that — a coughing spell with liquids and then a fever the same night is exactly the kind of thing that points to a little going down the wrong way. We'll factor that in. Have you been losing weight?
FAMILY: He has. About thirteen pounds in two months, maybe more. He just can't get enough in.
DR: That's a big part of why we want to get ahead of this. Let me go through your medicines. Chart shows riluzole, fifty milligrams twice a day — still on that?
PT: Yes, morning and night.
DR: Good. Anything else?
PT: I go to the infusion center for the other ALS one — the edaravone, Radicava. Every few weeks.
DR: Thank you — I don't see that on the list here, so I'll add it. Anything for the blood, a blood thinner?
PT: I take a blood thinner, yes.
DR: Okay — that's important before a procedure. Which one, do you know the name?
PT: It's the fish oil. The omega-3 capsules. My neighbor called it a blood thinner so that's what I say.
DR: Ah — that's helpful to clarify. Fish oil is over-the-counter, not the prescription kind that we'd have to stop and bridge. That changes the picture. No warfarin, no Eliquis or Xarelto?
PT: No, nothing like that.
DR: Perfect. Any drug allergies?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: One more thing — pulmonary saw you this week, and your breathing test came down to forty-three percent, a bit lower than the referral said. That's exactly why we don't want to wait. We'll do the PEG soon, keep the sedation light, and sit you up rather than flat, with the breathing team on hand. I'll coordinate the timing with pulmonary.
FAMILY: Thank you. Sooner is better for us.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Kagame is a 57-year-old man with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (diagnosed 2025, mixed bulbar/limb) presenting for pre-procedure consultation for elective PEG placement due to progressive dysphagia. He reports worsening swallowing, has largely stopped solids, and now coughs with thin liquids. He and his spouse report a prolonged choking episode with water last week followed the same night by a low-grade fever, concerning for microaspiration. Spouse reports ~13 lb (~6 kg) weight loss over ~2 months from poor intake. He confirms riluzole 50 mg BID. He additionally reports ongoing edaravone (Radicava) infusions at the infusion center, not currently on the clinic medication list. On questioning about a 'blood thinner,' he clarified this is over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3), not a prescription anticoagulant; he denies warfarin or any DOAC. No known drug allergies.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 76, RR 16, BP 122/76, SpO2 95% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 61.2 kg (135 lb), BMI 20.0. Most recent pulmonary function (2026-07-15): FVC 43% predicted, down from 48% last month and 52% in April. Weight 61.2 kg today. Albumin 3.4 g/dL (2026-07-16). SpO2 95% on room air. Nocturnal NIV being initiated per pulmonary.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- ALS with progressive dysphagia and inadequate oral intake / weight loss — indication for enteral access; proceed with PEG placement, expedited given declining respiratory reserve.
- Respiratory risk (FVC 43% predicted, falling): coordinate with pulmonary/anesthesia; plan minimized sedation, non-supine / head-of-bed-elevated positioning, respiratory support available; consider earlier procedure slot. Confirm NIV availability peri-procedure.
- Possible recent microaspiration (choking episode + fever): assess clinically before procedure; low threshold for chest imaging / delay if active aspiration pneumonia.
- Medication reconciliation: ADD edaravone (Radicava) infusions to medication list; continue riluzole 50 mg BID.
- Clarify 'blood thinner': confirmed OTC fish oil (omega-3) only — no prescription anticoagulant hold/bridge required; advise holding fish oil per standard pre-procedure guidance.
- Nutrition consult; monitor weight and albumin.
- NKDA confirmed.
after_visit_summary:

Next steps
• We will schedule your PEG soon and coordinate the timing with your lung doctors.

planted_discrepancies.note: Sending office label is clinically wrong. Escalate to urgent review; anticoagulated GI bleed with orthostasis warrants same-day/ED evaluation for hemodynamic stabilization before elective prep. Override the routine label.

planted_discrepancies.note: Confirmed active DOAC. Not a med-list error, but a critical peri-procedure trap: therapeutic anticoagulation on a bleeding patient requires held-dose planning, anesthesia/anticoagulation clearance, and cardiology input on afib stroke risk before any prep or scope.

prior_notes.text: Screening colonoscopy to cecum. Adequate prep. Findings: moderate diverticulosis throughout the sigmoid and descending colon without active inflammation or bleeding. No polyps or masses. Internal hemorrhoids, non-bleeding. Recommendation: routine screening interval; repeat in 10 years or sooner for new symptoms such as rectal bleeding or change in bowel habits. No complications.

Follow-up for nonvalvular atrial fibrillation. Rate-controlled, asymptomatic. CHA2DS2-VASc elevated; continue rivaroxaban 20 mg daily for stroke prophylaxis. Hypertension controlled on amlodipine 5 mg daily. Counseled that any invasive procedure requires advance coordination regarding anticoagulation hold. Return in 6 months.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Marsh — and you brought your daughter, welcome. The referral came in to plan a colonoscopy. Tell me what's been going on.
MA: Before we start, I've got her rooming vitals — height 5 foot 4, weight 141 pounds; temp 98.2, heart rate 94, respirations 18, blood pressure 128 over 78 sitting, and oxygen 97 percent on room air.
PT: Well, I've been seeing blood. When I go to the bathroom, there's bright red blood — in the bowl and on the paper. It's been about a week now, and it's most times I go, not just once.
DR: Bright red, every day, for a week. Any of it darker or tarry?
PT: No, bright red. It scared me, honestly.
FAMILY: I've been keeping her blood pressure log at home, and it's been dropping. This morning it was fine sitting but when she stood up she got dizzy — it went down into the low hundreds. That's happened a few days running.
DR: Thank you, that's important — our nurse just saw the same thing here, her pressure fell when she stood. Ms. Marsh, are you on any blood thinners?
PT: Yes, the rivaroxaban. For my heart — the irregular beat. I take twenty milligrams every morning. I took it today.
DR: You took it this morning. And the amlodipine for blood pressure — still taking that too?
PT: Yes, every day.
DR: Any allergies to medications?
PT: No, none that I know of.
DR: Okay. Here's my concern. You have active bleeding, you're on a blood thinner, and your blood pressure is dropping when you stand — your blood count today is low as well. The office marked this as a routine referral, but I don't consider this routine. This needs urgent attention today.
PT: Oh. I thought I'd just book the scope for later.
DR: We still need the colonoscopy, but first we make sure you're stable. I'm going to have you evaluated urgently — likely in the emergency department — for fluids and monitoring, and we'll coordinate holding the rivaroxaban with your cardiologist before any prep.
FAMILY: Should we go now?
DR: Yes. I'll call ahead so they're expecting her.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Marsh is a 79-year-old woman referred to GI clinic to plan a colonoscopy. On interview she reports approximately one week of bright red blood per rectum, occurring with most bowel movements and increasing — no melena. She takes rivaroxaban 20 mg daily for atrial fibrillation (took a dose this morning) and amlodipine 5 mg daily for hypertension; she confirms both. Her daughter reports a home blood-pressure log over the past several days showing readings that fall with standing along with lightheadedness. No known drug allergies. Prior colonoscopy (2019) showed diverticulosis without bleeding, no polyps.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 94, RR 18, BP 128/78, SpO2 97% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 64 kg (141 lb), BMI 24.1. In-clinic vitals demonstrate an orthostatic drop (sitting SBP 128 -> standing SBP 104, standing HR 98). Labs 2026-07-17: hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL (low), hematocrit 29.8% (low), consistent with acute/subacute blood loss.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Active hematochezia in a therapeutically anticoagulated patient (rivaroxaban) with orthostasis and anemia — this is an URGENT presentation. The sending office's "routine" label is overridden.
- Escalate today for hemodynamic evaluation and stabilization; refer to the emergency department for IV access, fluids, monitoring, serial hemoglobin, and type-and-screen. Called ahead.
- Anticoagulation management: coordinate holding rivaroxaban with cardiology given nonvalvular afib stroke risk; do NOT begin bowel prep until anticoagulation and hemodynamics are addressed and anesthesia clearance obtained.
- Colonoscopy remains indicated for the bleeding source (diverticular vs other) but is deferred until the patient is stabilized and anticoagulation is safely managed.
- Continue amlodipine; recheck BP after volume resuscitation.
- Return/ED precautions reinforced with patient and daughter for ongoing bleeding, syncope, chest pain, or worsening lightheadedness.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Bright red rectal bleeding for about a week
• You take a blood thinner (rivaroxaban) for your irregular heartbeat
• Your blood pressure dropping when you stand, and a low blood count today
• Planning for a colonoscopy

Next steps
• This is not a routine situation. Because you are bleeding while on a blood thinner and your blood pressure is dropping, we are sending you for urgent evaluation today — most likely the emergency department — for fluids and monitoring. We will call ahead.
• Do not start any colonoscopy prep yet. We will arrange the colonoscopy after you are stable.
• We will work with your cardiologist about safely holding the rivaroxaban before the procedure. Do not stop it on your own until you are told to.
• Keep taking your amlodipine unless told otherwise.
• Go to the emergency room right away for heavy bleeding, fainting, chest pain, or severe dizziness.

_note: MOCK GI patient in the style of synthetic-ambient-fhir-25. No real patient. For PreChart development/eval.

planted_discrepancies.note: narrative-vs-pharmacy anticoagulant conflict; periprocedural hold differs materially (warfarin ~5-day hold with INR normalization vs apixaban ~1-2 day hold, no bridging). Prior hematology note documents the switch but the active med list was never reconciled.

prior_notes.text: Surveillance esophagogastroduodenoscopy. Salmon-colored mucosa extending in a tongue-like fashion in the distal esophagus, consistent with Barrett's esophagus, Prague classification C2M4. Four-quadrant biopsies obtained every 2 cm per Seattle protocol; targeted biopsies of any mucosal irregularity — none seen. Stomach and duodenum normal. Impression: Barrett's esophagus C2M4. Await histology; if no dysplasia, surveillance EGD in ~2 years.

Follow-up for history of lower-extremity DVT (2020, provoked — occurred following right knee arthroscopy with post-operative immobilization). Patient has remained on long-term anticoagulation. Given provoked etiology and no recurrence, categorized as LOW thrombotic risk. Transitioning from warfarin to apixaban 5 mg PO twice daily for ease of monitoring; warfarin discontinued today, apixaban started after last therapeutic INR. No bridging indicated for future low-bleeding-risk procedures. For any upcoming endoscopy, hold apixaban per procedural bleeding risk; bridging is NOT required.

transcript:
DR: Morning, Mr. Ostrowski. You're in today so we can plan the upper endoscopy — the Barrett's surveillance scope. How have you been feeling?
MA: Before the doctor gets started, let me get your vitals down — you're 5 foot 10, 183 pounds. Blood pressure's 126 over 78, heart rate 72, temp 98.1, breathing 14, and oxygen's 98 percent on room air.
PT: Fine, honestly. No heartburn, no trouble swallowing, weight's steady. I feel like I'm only here because it's on the calendar.
DR: That's exactly right, and that's good news — this is routine surveillance. Your last scope showed the Barrett's, C2 M4, and the biopsies had no dysplasia, so we just keep an eye on it. You're still on the omeprazole?
PT: Every morning, forty milligrams. Never miss it.
DR: Perfect. Now the main thing I need to sort out is the blood thinner. The chart has you down for warfarin.
PT: Oh — no, they took me off the warfarin. Hematology switched me over to apixaban back in the spring, March I think. Twice a day now. Much easier, no more finger-stick checks.
DR: That's an important correction — so no warfarin, you're on apixaban. And your chart still has an INR from January, when you were on the warfarin. That number doesn't really apply anymore.
PT: Right, they don't check that number for me now.
DR: Understood. And remind me about the clot back in 2020 — was there a reason it happened?
PT: Yeah, it was after my knee surgery. The arthroscopy on the right knee. I was laid up for a couple weeks and then the leg swelled up.
DR: Okay — so it was provoked, related to the surgery. Hematology has you as low-risk, and their note says no bridging is needed, which fits. Any other medicines or supplements?
PT: Just a fish oil, my other blood thinner I guess. Over the counter.
DR: Good to know, but fish oil isn't an actual anticoagulant, so we don't hold it for procedures. I'll get the apixaban hold instructions squared away and the team will send your prep.
PT: Sounds good.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Ostrowski is a 64-year-old man presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan surveillance EGD for Barrett's esophagus (C2M4, no dysplasia on 2024 biopsies; surveillance due). He is asymptomatic — denies heartburn, dysphagia, odynophagia, and weight loss. He confirms daily omeprazole 40 mg. Regarding anticoagulation: chart and referral list warfarin, but the patient reports hematology transitioned him from warfarin to apixaban 5 mg twice daily approximately 4 months ago (~March 2026); he no longer takes warfarin. He clarifies his 2020 lower-extremity DVT was provoked, occurring after right knee arthroscopy with post-operative immobilization (charted as unprovoked). He also takes OTC fish oil, which he colloquially calls a 'blood thinner'; this is a supplement, not an anticoagulant. No drug allergies reported.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 126/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 83 kg (183 lb), BMI 26.2. Labs — INR 2.4 (2026-01-15, drawn while on warfarin, prior to DOAC switch; not a current monitoring parameter). Hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL and platelets 244 x10^3/uL (2026-06-30), both normal. No active anemia. Patient appears well.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Barrett's esophagus (C2M4, no dysplasia) — proceed with scheduled surveillance EGD with Seattle-protocol biopsies. Asymptomatic; low bleeding-risk procedure per ASGE.
- Anticoagulation reconciliation (HIGH PRIORITY): patient is on APIXABAN 5 mg BID, not warfarin — update active med list (discontinue warfarin, add apixaban). Periprocedural plan will be based on apixaban, not warfarin/INR. Hold apixaban per procedural protocol (typically 1-2 days pre-procedure for low-bleeding-risk endoscopy); the January INR is stale and will not be used.
- Thrombotic risk: 2020 DVT was PROVOKED (post-knee arthroscopy), not unprovoked — update problem list. Consistent with hematology's low-risk categorization; no bridging required per hematology note (2026-03-10).
- Fish oil is an OTC supplement, not an anticoagulant — no periprocedural hold needed; documented for completeness.
- GERD/Barrett's — continue omeprazole 40 mg daily.
- Scheduling + bowel/upper-endoscopy prep and anticoagulant hold instructions to follow from the team.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your scheduled upper endoscopy (EGD) to check your Barrett's esophagus
• You are feeling well with no heartburn or swallowing problems
• Your blood thinner and how to manage it around the procedure
• Your reflux medicine

Next steps
• We will schedule your upper endoscopy; the team will send prep instructions.
• Important: your record showed warfarin, but you are now on apixaban. We updated this. You will get specific instructions on when to stop the apixaban before the procedure. Please do not use the old warfarin/INR plan.
• Bridging (extra injections) is not needed — your clot in 2020 was related to your knee surgery and hematology considers you low risk.
• Fish oil is a supplement, not a true blood thinner, so it does not need to be stopped.
• Keep taking omeprazole each morning.
• Call us with trouble swallowing, vomiting blood, black stools, or new chest pain.

_note: MOCK GI patient in the style of synthetic-ambient-fhir-25. No real patient. For PreChart development/eval.

prior_notes.text: Surveillance colonoscopy to cecum, adequate prep. Findings: single 22 mm laterally spreading sessile polyp in the sigmoid colon, removed by piecemeal endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) after submucosal lift; several visible vessels treated with prophylactic clips. Retrieved for histology. No immediate bleeding at completion. Apixaban had been held for 48 hours pre-procedure per anticoagulation plan. Post-polypectomy bleeding precautions given. Plan: resume apixaban on post-procedure Day 2; low-residue diet; call clinic or present to ED for rectal bleeding, black stools, lightheadedness, or abdominal pain. Histology pending.

Pre-procedure visit for surveillance colonoscopy. History of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on apixaban 5 mg twice daily (CHA2DS2-VASc reviewed) and hypertension on metoprolol. Prior sigmoid adenoma. Periprocedural anticoagulation plan discussed: hold apixaban 48 hours before colonoscopy given anticipated polypectomy, resume ~48 hours after per bleeding risk. No chest pain, no prior GI bleeding. Cleared for procedure.

dosageInstruction.text: 5 mg by mouth twice daily; held 48h pre-procedure, resumed post-procedure Day 2 (2026-07-16)

transcript:
NURSE: Hi Camille, I have you down as a post-procedure follow-up after your colonoscopy last week. The doctor's going to join. Before that — what's going on today?
PT: So I had the polyp removed on Tuesday, the big one. Since this morning I've had two really dark stools — almost black, kind of tarry. And when I stand up I get dizzy, like the room tips for a second.
NURSE: Let me get your height and weight and a few vitals first — you're five foot five, a hundred and fifty pounds, temperature's normal at ninety-eight four, breathing's easy at sixteen, and your oxygen is ninety-eight percent on room air. Now let me take your pressure sitting and standing. Sitting one-eighteen over seventy-four... okay, stand up for me. Ninety-six over sixty-one, and your heart rate jumped to one-ten.
DR: Camille, thanks for coming in — this isn't a routine check-in anymore, so I want to be direct. Black tarry stools four days after a twenty-two millimeter resection, plus feeling faint when you stand, tells me you may be bleeding from where the polyp was removed. Any chest pain or trouble breathing?
PT: No chest pain, no. Just the dizziness and the dark stools.
DR: Good, no chest pain. And your blood thinner — the apixaban — did you restart it?
PT: Yes. The discharge sheet said to start it back Thursday, Day two, so I took it Thursday and I've been taking it twice a day since. Did I do something wrong?
DR: No — you followed the plan exactly. But being back on apixaban is why I'm not comfortable watching this at home. That combination — the resection site, the blood thinner, black stools, and your blood pressure dropping when you stand — needs to be evaluated in the emergency department right now, not here in clinic.
PT: Okay. That's scary, but okay.
DR: I'm going to call the endoscopist who did your procedure while you head over, so they know you're coming and can arrange a scope to stop the bleeding if needed. Do not take your next apixaban dose until the ED team clears it. Is someone able to drive you, or should we arrange transport?
PT: My husband is in the waiting room, he can take me.
DR: Perfect. Go straight there — I'll make the calls now.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Roux is a 53-year-old woman contacting GI clinic on post-procedure Day 4 after colonoscopy with piecemeal EMR of a 22 mm sigmoid polyp (2026-07-14). Although booked as a routine post-polypectomy follow-up, she reports two black, tarry stools since this morning and lightheadedness on standing. She denies chest pain and dyspnea. She confirms she resumed apixaban 5 mg twice daily on Day 2 (2026-07-16) exactly as directed on her discharge instructions. Longstanding atrial fibrillation and hypertension, on apixaban and metoprolol.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.9 C, HR 110 (elevated), RR 16, sitting BP 118/74, standing BP 96/61 with heart rate rising to 110/min — orthostatic, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 165 cm (5'5"), Wt 68 kg (150 lb), BMI 25.0. Pre-procedure baseline hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL (2026-07-10); no current CBC available in clinic. Patient alert, uncomfortable/anxious but oriented.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Delayed post-polypectomy hemorrhage — high concern. Melena plus orthostatic vital signs on Day 4 after 22 mm EMR while re-anticoagulated on apixaban. This is an emergent presentation, not a reassurance visit.
- Immediate referral to the emergency department for resuscitation, CBC/type & screen, hemodynamic monitoring, and likely repeat colonoscopy for hemostasis.
- Endoscopist who performed the EMR notified directly during this encounter.
- HOLD apixaban — no further doses until ED/anticoagulation team directs; discuss reversal (andexanet/4F-PCC) per ED if active bleeding.
- Continue metoprolol pending ED assessment; monitor for hypotension.
- Confirmed active problems: atrial fibrillation, hypertension. Pertinent negative: no chest pain.
- Arranged transport (private, husband driving); return precautions escalated to ED-now.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we found today
• You are having signs of bleeding from where your large polyp was removed 4 days ago: black, tarry stools and feeling dizzy when you stand.
• Your blood pressure dropped when you stood up, which can happen with blood loss.
• You are back on your blood thinner (apixaban), which you restarted exactly as instructed — this makes bleeding more concerning.

What happens next — go now
• Go straight to the Emergency Department today. This is urgent; do not wait or monitor at home.
• Your GI doctor is calling the specialist who did your procedure so they are ready for you and can repeat the scope to stop the bleeding if needed.
• Do NOT take any more apixaban until the emergency team tells you it is safe.
• Keep taking nothing by mouth until the ED evaluates you, in case a procedure is needed.

Call 911 or return immediately if you
• Faint or pass out, have large amounts of blood, severe belly pain, chest pain, or trouble breathing.

transcript:
NURSE: Mr. Blankenship, come on back. I've got you down as a routine post-scope check-in, is that right?
PT: Well — that's what the letter said, but I'm not doing great, to be honest. Something's not right since last night.
NURSE: Okay, tell me. I'll get the doctor in quickly. Let me get your vitals real quick — you're 5 foot 9, 185 pounds; temp's 98.6, heart rate 90, blood pressure 130 over 78, oxygen 97 percent on room air.
DR: Theodore, hi. I'm sorry you're feeling off. Your ERCP was yesterday for the bile-duct stone — walk me through what's been happening.
PT: The stone thing, yeah. I felt alright when I went home. But around midnight I got this pain up here, in the pit of my stomach, and it goes straight through to my back. It's maybe a six out of ten, comes in waves. And I threw up once, around two in the morning.
DR: Pain boring through to the back, started about eight hours after the procedure, with vomiting. Any fever or chills?
PT: No fever that I noticed. Just the pain and feeling sick.
DR: Have you taken anything for it?
PT: They sent me home with the oxycodone. I've taken two of them since — one last evening and one overnight — but it only takes the edge off for a little while.
DR: Okay. That's important. Any allergies to medications?
PT: No, none that I know of.
DR: Here's my concern, and I want to be straight with you: this pattern — new pain radiating to the back, vomiting, the day after a sphincterotomy — that's how inflammation of the pancreas can show up after this procedure. This is not a routine check today. I'm ordering blood work right now, a pancreatic enzyme called lipase and a blood count, and I want them run STAT.
PT: So it's not just soreness from the scope?
DR: It might be, but I'm not going to assume that. We'll get the lipase back within the hour and I'll call you the moment I have it. Depending on the number, you may need to come in for IV fluids rather than manage this at home.
PT: Alright. I'd rather know.
DR: Exactly. Don't take any more of the oxycodone until we talk — I don't want it masking things. Sit tight, the nurse will draw the labs now.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Blankenship is a 68-year-old man presenting on post-procedure day 1 after ERCP with biliary sphincterotomy and stone extraction (2026-07-17) for choledocholithiasis. Although the visit was scheduled/labeled as a routine post-procedure follow-up, he reports new epigastric pain that began around midnight (~8 hours after the procedure), rated 6/10, waxing and waning, and radiating through to the back, accompanied by one episode of emesis at approximately 02:00. He denies fever or chills. He has taken 2 tablets of discharge oxycodone 5 mg (one evening, one overnight) with only transient, partial relief. No known drug allergies.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 37.0 C, HR 90, RR 16, BP 130/78, SpO2 97% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 84 kg (185 lb), BMI 27.4. Post-ERCP day 1. Pre-procedure baseline labs (2026-07-17): lipase 42 U/L (normal), WBC 7.1 x10^3/uL, total bilirubin 1.6 mg/dL. STAT lipase and CBC ordered this visit — results pending at time of documentation. Clinical presentation notable for epigastric-to-back pain and vomiting following sphincterotomy.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Suspected post-ERCP pancreatitis (post-op day 1) — new epigastric pain radiating to back with emesis after biliary sphincterotomy. NOT a routine follow-up; managed as an escalation. Diagnosis suspected, not yet confirmed pending enzyme results.
  - STAT lipase + CBC ordered; clinician to review and call patient within the hour.
  - If lipase elevated (>3x ULN) or clinical deterioration, arrange same-day evaluation for IV crystalloid resuscitation and admission consideration.
- Analgesia: hold further oxycodone until labs reviewed to avoid masking symptom trajectory; reassess analgesic plan once diagnosis clarified.
- Choledocholithiasis s/p ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone clearance — biliary obstruction relieved per procedure report; monitor.
- Return/escalation precautions reviewed: worsening pain, persistent vomiting, fever, or lightheadedness — proceed to ED.
- Correct visit disposition in chart from routine follow-up to urgent post-procedure evaluation.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• You had your ERCP (bile-duct procedure) yesterday.
• Since midnight you have had stomach pain that goes to your back, and you vomited once.
• This can be a sign of irritation of the pancreas after this kind of procedure, so today is not a routine check-up.

Next steps
• We drew blood right away (a pancreas enzyme called lipase and a blood count). These are being run urgently.
• Your doctor will call you within the hour with the results and tell you what to do next.
• Depending on the results, you may need to come in for fluids through an IV.
• Please do NOT take any more oxycodone until we speak, so it does not hide your symptoms.
• Go to the emergency room now if your pain gets worse, you keep vomiting, you develop a fever, or you feel faint.

Esophagogastroduodenoscopy performed under monitored anesthesia. Findings: smooth tapered peptic stricture in the distal esophagus at ~36 cm, consistent with reflux-related stricture; LA Grade A esophagitis. No malignant-appearing tissue; biopsies obtained. Through-the-scope balloon dilation performed to 15 mm with good luminal result and no immediate bleeding or perforation. Stomach and duodenum unremarkable. No complications. Post-procedure: tolerated clears in recovery. Resume home medications including twice-daily PPI. Soft diet x48h, advance as tolerated. Follow up in clinic; return precautions reviewed.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Pemberton — how are you feeling two days out from the scope?
PT: Pretty good, honestly. My throat's a little sore, but nothing terrible. I was mostly worried whether that soreness means something went wrong.
DR: That's a really common question, and no — a mild sore throat for a few days is exactly what we expect after the dilation. How's eating going?
PT: Better than before, actually. I'm sticking to soft stuff — scrambled eggs, soup, yogurt — and it's going down fine. Before the procedure I felt food catching in my chest, and I haven't had that since.
DR: That's great to hear. Any trouble getting liquids down? Any chest pain, fever, chills?
PT: No, none of that. Water and tea are no problem. No fever, I checked. No chest pain.
DR: Perfect. Any vomiting, or bringing up blood, or black stools?
PT: No, nothing like that.
NURSE: Her temp today is normal, 36.7, heart rate 72, respirations 14, blood pressure 128 over 78, oxygen 98 percent on room air. Height's 5 foot 4, weight 150 pounds.
DR: Good. Let me confirm your medications. You restarted the pantoprazole?
PT: Yes, morning and evening like you said. And I went back on my blood-pressure pill, the amlodipine, the morning after.
DR: Exactly right. Keep the pantoprazole twice a day — it helps keep the stricture from tightening up again. You can use acetaminophen for the throat if you need it.
PT: Okay. When can I go back to normal food?
DR: Advance slowly over the next few days — soft foods now, then add regular textures as it feels comfortable, chewing well. The biopsies were benign-looking so far; we'll confirm the final report. Most people with this kind of narrowing need a repeat dilation down the line, so we'll keep an eye on your swallowing.
PT: That's reassuring. So I don't need to do anything urgent?
DR: No — you're right on track. Just call us if you get a fever, real chest pain, trouble swallowing liquids, or any black or bloody stools. Otherwise I'll see you for routine follow-up.
PT: Thank you, that puts my mind at ease.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Pemberton is a 59-year-old woman presenting for routine follow-up 2 days after EGD with balloon dilation of a distal peptic esophageal stricture (2026-07-16). She reports a mild sore throat, which is improving, and denies fever, chills, chest pain, odynophagia, hematemesis, or melena. She is tolerating a soft diet without difficulty and swallows liquids without problem; notably, the pre-procedure sensation of food catching in the chest has resolved. She resumed her home medications the morning after the procedure, including pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily.

**Objective:** Comfortable, no acute distress. Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 128/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 68 kg (150 lb), BMI 25.6. Recent hemoglobin (2026-07-10) 13.6 g/dL. No signs of bleeding or infection. Preliminary esophageal biopsy pathology: reflux-associated changes, no dysplasia or malignancy (final pending).

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Status post EGD with balloon dilation of peptic esophageal stricture, post-procedure day 2 — expected, uncomplicated course. No evidence of complication (no fever, chest pain, dysphagia to liquids, or bleeding). Reassurance provided; no escalation or urgent workup indicated.
- Peptic esophageal stricture / GERD — continue pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily to reduce risk of stricture recurrence. Anticipate possible need for repeat dilation in future; monitor swallowing.
- Diet — advance from soft to regular textures over the next several days as tolerated, chewing thoroughly.
- Symptom control — acetaminophen 500 mg PRN for throat discomfort.
- Hypertension — continue amlodipine 5 mg daily.
- Pathology — follow up final esophageal biopsy report.
- Return precautions reviewed: fever, chest pain, inability to swallow liquids, hematemesis, or melena. Routine clinic follow-up otherwise.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• You are 2 days out from your upper endoscopy (EGD) with stretching (dilation) of a narrowed area in your esophagus
• A mild sore throat right now is normal and expected
• Your swallowing is already better and you are eating soft foods without trouble
• Your recovery is on track — nothing urgent is needed

Next steps
• Keep taking pantoprazole 40 mg twice a day — this helps keep the area from narrowing again.
• Continue your amlodipine each morning as usual.
• Eat soft foods now and slowly add regular foods over the next few days, chewing well.
• You may use acetaminophen for throat discomfort if needed.
• We will follow up on your final biopsy results.
• Call us right away if you have a fever, chest pain, trouble swallowing liquids, vomiting blood, or black/bloody stools. Otherwise, we will see you at your routine follow-up.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Ibarra — how are you feeling after yesterday?
MA: Before the doctor gets started, let me grab your vitals — you're 5 foot 9, 176 pounds, temp's 36.8, heart rate 72, blood pressure 132 over 80, and oxygen's 98 percent on room air. All looks good.
PT: Pretty good, doctor. A little tired from the prep, but no pain, no bleeding, nothing like that. My wife drove me home and I slept fine.
DR: That's exactly what I like to hear. The scope went smoothly — we found two very small polyps and removed them with a cold snare, no complications, and no bleeding at the end. We'll send them to pathology just to check, and I'll let you know the results.
PT: Okay, good. So the main thing I wanted to ask — when can I go back on my blood thinner? I stopped it before the procedure like they told me.
DR: Let me make sure I have the right thing. When you say blood thinner, which medication do you mean?
PT: The fish oil. The omega-3 capsules. I take one every day — I've always called it my blood thinner because, you know, it thins the blood a little, that's what everybody says.
DR: Ah — thank you for clarifying. That's an over-the-counter supplement, the fish oil. It's not actually a prescription blood thinner like warfarin or one of the newer ones. Looking at your chart, you're not on any prescription anticoagulant at all.
PT: No, no — never have been. Just the fish oil and my blood pressure pill.
DR: Right, the lisinopril, ten milligrams. Still taking that each day?
PT: Every morning, yes.
DR: Perfect. So the good news is the fish oil never needed to be stopped for the colonoscopy — it doesn't carry a real bleeding risk. You can restart it today if you'd like, and keep taking your lisinopril as usual.
PT: Oh, that's a relief. I was worried I'd have to wait a week.
DR: Not at all. Everything looks great. Once pathology is back we'll set your next screening interval — likely a few years out given only small polyps.
PT: Thank you, doctor.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Ibarra is a 72-year-old man presenting for post-procedure follow-up, one day after an uncomplicated screening colonoscopy (2026-07-17) with cold snare removal of two diminutive polyps. He feels well: no abdominal pain, no rectal bleeding, no dizziness; mild residual fatigue from the prep. His chief question is when he may restart his 'blood thinner.' On clarification, this refers to his over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3) supplement, which he self-held before the procedure; he is not on and has never been on a prescription anticoagulant or antiplatelet agent. He confirms daily lisinopril 10 mg for hypertension.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 132/80, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 80 kg (176 lb), BMI 26.1. Post-procedure recovery uncomplicated. Most recent hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL (2026-06-30). Colonoscopy 2026-07-17: to cecum, adequate prep, two diminutive (3-4 mm) sessile polyps in ascending and sigmoid colon removed by cold snare, no active bleeding at completion; pathology pending.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Status post uncomplicated screening colonoscopy with cold snare polypectomy x2 — post-op course GREEN, no bleeding or complications.
- Medication clarification: patient's 'blood thinner' = over-the-counter fish oil supplement, NOT a prescription anticoagulant. No periprocedural hold was ever indicated. Reassured; may restart fish oil today.
- Hypertension — continue lisinopril 10 mg daily; BP at goal.
- Await pathology; will contact patient with results and finalize surveillance interval (likely extended given only diminutive polyps).
- Return precautions reviewed: call for significant rectal bleeding, severe abdominal pain, fever, or lightheadedness.

transcript:
DR: Hi Josie, thanks for coming in on short notice. The fellow asked me to see you before we scope you — you've been having a rough couple of weeks. Tell me what's going on.
MA: Before we get started, let me grab your vitals real quick. You're 5 foot 5, 141 pounds. Temp 37.3, heart rate's a little up at 100, blood pressure 116 over 74, breathing 18, oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: It's the colitis, it's flaring bad. I'm going to the bathroom six, seven times a day and there's blood every time. When I have to go, I have to go — I can't hold it. It started maybe two weeks ago.
DR: I'm sorry, that sounds miserable. Any fever, or belly pain?
PT: Some cramping, low-grade warm feeling on and off. Mostly it's just the urgency and the blood.
DR: Let me go through your medicines. The chart has you on mesalamine, the 4.8 grams a day. Are you taking that steady?
PT: So — that's the thing. I ran out. I went about a week without it right before this all started, before I got the refill sorted. I'm back on it now.
DR: Okay, that's really useful to know — a gap like that can set off a flare. And I see a note you were started on a steroid?
PT: Yeah, the fellow put me on prednisone, 40 milligrams. I've been taking it five days now. It's helped a little but not a lot.
DR: Good, I'll make sure that's in your list — it wasn't showing. Are you on any blood thinners?
PT: I take a blood thinner, yeah — the fish oil. My neighbor said it thins your blood.
DR: Ah — the fish oil, the omega-3. That's not a true blood thinner, so that's fine, nothing we need to stop for the biopsies. Any actual prescription anticoagulant, like warfarin or a Eliquis-type pill?
PT: No, nothing like that.
DR: Perfect. Now — the referral asked for a full colonoscopy, but with how active this is right now, doing a full prep and going all the way around is riskier than we'd like. I'd rather do a limited flex sig without the big prep, just to grade the severity and take biopsies, including for CMV given the steroids. And honestly, with these numbers, I want the IBD team involved and we may admit you rather than send you home.
PT: Whatever's safest. I just want to feel better.
DR: Understood. Let me make some calls.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Tallchief is a 47-year-old woman with ulcerative colitis (diagnosed 2019, left-sided; prior acute severe flare requiring admission in 2023) presenting for pre-procedure consultation during an active flare. She reports ~2 weeks of worsening bloody diarrhea, now 6 bloody stools per day with fecal urgency, intermittent cramping and subjective low-grade warmth. She reports she ran out of her maintenance mesalamine and missed approximately 1 week of therapy immediately preceding the flare; she has since restarted it. The GI fellow started prednisone 40 mg daily 5 days ago (not previously reflected on the medication list) with only partial response. On medication reconciliation she reports a 'blood thinner' which on questioning is over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3), not a prescription anticoagulant; she denies warfarin or a DOAC.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 37.3 C, HR 100, RR 18, BP 116/74, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 165 cm (5'5"), Wt 64 kg (141 lb), BMI 23.5. Labs 2026-07-15: CRP 48 mg/L (high), hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL (low), albumin 3.1 g/dL (low). Patient-reported 6 bloody stools/day with urgency. Presentation meets criteria for acute severe ulcerative colitis.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Acute severe ulcerative colitis flare — elevated CRP, hypoalbuminemia, anemia, high stool frequency with hematochezia. Inpatient-adjacent severity.
- Endoscopy plan: perform LIMITED, UNPREPPED FLEXIBLE SIGMOIDOSCOPY for severity grading and biopsies (including CMV immunostains) rather than full colonoscopy — full prep/colonoscopy carries elevated perforation/decompensation risk in acute severe colitis. Procedure request to be amended.
- Coordinate with IBD team + advanced endoscopy review; anticipate admission for IV corticosteroids and monitoring; reassess for rescue therapy if inadequate response.
- Medications: confirm/continue prednisone 40 mg daily (add to med list); continue mesalamine 4.8 g/day, counsel on adherence gap as flare trigger.
- Anticoagulation clearance: no true anticoagulant — reported 'blood thinner' is fish oil; no agent to hold for biopsy.
- Anemia — monitor; likely GI blood loss and inflammation; iron studies/transfusion threshold per inpatient course.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your ulcerative colitis is having a severe flare right now
• The best and safest camera test to do while you are this active
• Your medicines, including the new steroid
• Getting the IBD team involved

Next steps
• Because your colitis is very active, we plan a LIMITED camera exam (flexible sigmoidoscopy) without the full bowel prep, instead of a full colonoscopy — this is safer right now. We will take biopsies, including a test for a virus called CMV.
• We are contacting the IBD specialist team and expect you may be admitted to the hospital for stronger (IV) steroid treatment and close monitoring.
• Keep taking your prednisone 40 mg every day and your mesalamine — try not to run out again, as the missed week likely triggered this flare. We added the prednisone to your record.
• Your fish oil is not a true blood thinner, so it does not need to be stopped before the biopsies.
• Call or come in right away for heavy bleeding, severe belly pain, high fever, or lightheadedness.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Sloane. Thank you for coming in. I have our Cantonese interpreter on the line since your daughter couldn't be here today.
MA: Before you got started — I roomed him and took his vitals: height 5 foot 8, weight 172 pounds, blood pressure 132 over 80, heart rate 70, temp 36.7 Celsius, respirations 14, oxygen 97 percent on room air.
INTERPRETER: Good morning, this is certified interpreter ID 4471. I'll relay for Mr. Sloane.
DR: Perfect. We're planning your follow-up colonoscopy — you had polyps back in 2021, and you're due this year.
INTERPRETER (for PT): He understands. He feels well, no stomach problems, no bleeding, no weight loss. Bowels are normal.
DR: Good. I want to go carefully through his medicines because of the procedure. The chart shows clopidogrel — the blood-thinner-type pill — and atorvastatin. Is that right?
INTERPRETER (for PT): Yes. He takes the clopidogrel for his legs, the poor circulation. And the cholesterol pill every night.
DR: Very important question — has he ever had a stent placed, in the heart or the legs?
INTERPRETER (for PT): No. No stents, never. Only the circulation problem, the doctor gave him that pill.
DR: That's helpful — clopidogrel for PAD with no stents means we can hold it safely before the scope. Anything else he takes that we might not have written down?
INTERPRETER (for PT): He says yes — he buys a small aspirin himself, the baby aspirin, every day. He thought it was good for the heart.
DR: Okay, that's important — I didn't have the aspirin listed. And any vitamins or supplements?
INTERPRETER (for PT): He takes fish oil. He calls it his blood thinner.
DR: I'll note that. Fish oil isn't actually a blood thinner, but we'll have him pause it before the procedure just to be safe. Any allergies to medicines?
INTERPRETER (for PT): No allergies.
DR: Thank you. So — hold the clopidogrel and the aspirin per our protocol, pause the fish oil, continue everything else. We'll arrange a certified interpreter for the day of the procedure so consent is done properly, not through family. The team will send the prep and the exact stop dates.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Sloane is a 75-year-old Cantonese-speaking man presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan surveillance colonoscopy (tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia on 2021 colonoscopy, due 2026). Visit conducted with a certified Cantonese telephone interpreter; his daughter, who usually interprets, was unavailable. He reports no GI symptoms — no rectal bleeding, no weight loss, normal bowel habits. Medication reconciliation via interpreter: he confirms clopidogrel 75 mg daily for peripheral arterial disease and explicitly reports NO history of cardiac or peripheral stents; he confirms atorvastatin nightly. He additionally reports taking OTC aspirin 81 mg daily on his own (not previously documented) and an OTC fish oil supplement, which he refers to as a 'blood thinner.' No known drug allergies.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 70, RR 14, BP 132/80, SpO2 97% on RA; Ht 173 cm (5'8"), Wt 78 kg (172 lb), BMI 26.1. Pre-procedure labs (2026-07-10): hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL, platelets 244 x10^3/uL, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL — within normal limits.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Surveillance colonoscopy planning (prior tubular adenomas, low-grade dysplasia, 2021) — proceed with scheduling.
- Antiplatelet management: clopidogrel is monotherapy for PAD with NO stents — standard periprocedural hold applies safely; confirm hold interval per protocol. Newly disclosed daily OTC aspirin 81 mg — add to medication list and include in hold planning (patient on de facto dual antiplatelet therapy).
- Fish oil supplement (patient-labeled 'blood thinner') — clarified this is not therapeutic anticoagulation; advise pausing per standard supplement pre-procedure guidance. No formal anticoagulation reversal needed.
- Interpreter: certified interpreter REQUIRED for procedural consent — do not rely on family. Schedule certified Cantonese interpreter for day of procedure.
- Continue atorvastatin 20 mg nightly; continue antihypertensives.
- Update medication list: add OTC aspirin 81 mg and fish oil supplement.
- Bowel prep instructions and specific medication stop dates to be sent by team; return precautions reviewed.
after_visit_summary:

What we discussed
• Planning your follow-up colonoscopy (due this year)
• Your medicines and which to stop before the procedure
• Making sure a certified interpreter helps on the day of your procedure

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy. The team will send the prep instructions and the exact dates to stop medicines.
• Before the procedure, you will stop your clopidogrel and your daily aspirin as instructed. Because you have no stents, holding these is safe.
• Pause your fish oil supplement before the procedure. (Fish oil is not a true blood thinner, but we pause it to be safe.)
• Keep taking your atorvastatin (cholesterol pill) and your blood pressure medicine.
• A certified Cantonese interpreter will be arranged for the day of your procedure so your consent is done properly.
• We updated your record to add the daily aspirin and fish oil you buy yourself.
• Call us for any rectal bleeding, black stools, lightheadedness, or new belly pain.

transcript:
DR: Hi Priya, thanks for coming in. I'm the GI doctor — we're meeting to plan a procedure called an ERCP to deal with the gallstone that's blocking your bile duct. First things first, how far along are you?
MA: Before we get into it — I just roomed you and took your vitals: you're 5 foot 4, 152 pounds, temp 98.6, blood pressure 132 over 84, heart rate 88, breathing 16, and your oxygen's 98 percent on room air.
PT: I'm 24 weeks now. My MFM doctor knows I'm here — she's the one who's been managing everything.
DR: Good, and we'll keep her in the loop closely. Tell me what's been happening.
PT: It started a few weeks ago — really bad pain on the right side under my ribs after I eat, especially anything greasy. Then last week it got worse, I went to the ER, and they said a stone had moved into the duct. My blood tests for the liver went up.
DR: That's right — your bilirubin and liver numbers are elevated and climbing, which is why we don't want to wait. Anything new since the ER?
PT: Actually, yes — last night I had chills, I was shaking, and I felt feverish for a bit along with the pain. It settled by morning but it scared me.
DR: I'm really glad you mentioned that. Chills and fever with this kind of blockage is something we take seriously. Let me go through your medicines. The chart shows a prenatal vitamin — are you taking anything else?
PT: Yes — my prenatal every day. And my MFM started me on a baby aspirin, the 81, for my blood pressure, to prevent preeclampsia. I think of it as my blood thinner.
DR: That's important, thank you — it's not on our list. To be clear, that low-dose aspirin isn't a true blood thinner like warfarin; for this procedure we usually don't even need to stop it, but I'll confirm with the team.
PT: Oh, okay, that's a relief.
DR: Any allergies?
PT: I don't think it's written down, but during a CT scan a couple years ago the dye made me itch and break out in hives.
DR: That matters here — the ERCP uses an X-ray dye too, so we'll plan for that and keep the X-ray exposure to an absolute minimum to protect the baby. I'm going to get you scheduled urgently with an anesthesia team that's experienced with pregnancy, not in the routine line. We'll coordinate with your MFM.
PT: Thank you. I just want this handled safely.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Raghunathan is a 33-year-old woman (G2P1) at 24 weeks gestation presenting to GI clinic for pre-procedure consultation regarding urgent ERCP for symptomatic choledocholithiasis. She describes several weeks of severe postprandial right upper quadrant pain; an ER visit on 2026-07-14 identified a dilated common bile duct with a distal stone and rising LFTs. Overnight prior to this visit she reports an episode of rigors and subjective fever with RUQ pain that resolved by morning — concerning for evolving ascending cholangitis. She is followed by MFM. She confirms daily prenatal vitamin and reports she also takes low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily, started by MFM for preeclampsia prophylaxis (not currently on the clinic medication list); she refers to it as her 'blood thinner.' She reports a prior IV iodinated contrast reaction (pruritus and hives during a CT), not previously documented.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 37.0 C, HR 88, RR 16, BP 132/84, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 69 kg (152 lb), BMI 26.0. Labs (2026-07-16): total bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL (H), ALT 210 U/L (H), AST 168 U/L (H), alkaline phosphatase 265 U/L (H) — cholestatic pattern, rising. RUQ ultrasound (2026-07-14): cholelithiasis with dilated CBD (7 mm) and distal echogenic focus consistent with choledocholithiasis; single intrauterine pregnancy. Gestational age 24 weeks.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Symptomatic choledocholithiasis with obstructive/rising LFTs at 24 weeks gestation, now with an episode of rigors/fever concerning for early ascending cholangitis — proceed with URGENT ERCP; do not leave in routine outpatient queue.
- Pregnancy: coordinate with MFM and OB-experienced anesthesia; use fluoro-minimization protocol (lead shielding, wireless/no-fluoro or minimized-fluoro cannulation technique) to reduce fetal radiation.
- Iodinated contrast reaction (prior hives): plan minimized-contrast cholangiography and consider premedication per allergy protocol; add allergy to record.
- Medication reconciliation: add low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily (MFM-prescribed, prophylactic). Clarify with patient/team that low-dose ASA generally need not be held for ERCP with sphincterotomy; continue unless anesthesia/MFM advise otherwise.
- Continue prenatal vitamin.
- Counsel on urgent return for worsening fever, jaundice, persistent pain; scheduling and pre-procedure instructions to follow from the coordinating team.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• The gallstone blocking your bile duct and your rising liver blood tests
• Planning an urgent procedure (ERCP) to remove the blockage
• Keeping you and your baby safe during the procedure (you are 24 weeks pregnant)
• The chills/fever you had last night

Next steps
• We are scheduling your ERCP urgently — not in the routine line — with an anesthesia team experienced in pregnancy, and we will coordinate with your MFM doctor.
• During the procedure we will keep X-ray exposure to a minimum to protect your baby.
• We noted your prior reaction to CT/X-ray dye (itching and hives) and will plan around it.
• We added your low-dose aspirin (81 mg) to your record; you can keep taking it unless the team tells you otherwise, and keep taking your prenatal vitamin.
• Call or come in right away for fever, shaking chills, yellowing of your eyes or skin, or worsening pain.

transcript:
DR: Hi Otis, thanks for coming in. Your primary care doctor sent you over pretty quickly. Tell me what's been going on.
MA: Before the doctor dives in — let me get your vitals down. Temp's 98.2, heart rate 82, blood pressure 118 over 74, oxygen 98 percent. And you're 5 foot 10, 135 pounds today.
PT: Yeah, it's been rough. For like three months I've had diarrhea, five, six, sometimes seven times a day, and there's blood and mucus in it a lot. Bad cramps too. And I've dropped maybe twelve, thirteen pounds without trying.
DR: That's a real change. And I see a note about something near the anus?
PT: Right — there's this little spot that drains, kind of sore. It's been there a couple months now.
DR: Okay, that's important. Now the chart from the referral already lists you as having Crohn's disease. Has anyone actually confirmed that with a scope or biopsies?
PT: No, nobody's looked yet. My family doctor said it 'looked like' Crohn's because of the stool test being high, but that's why I'm here, right? Nothing's official.
DR: Exactly — that's a key point. We treat it as suspected until the colonoscopy and biopsies confirm it, and the MRI will map things out. Let me go over medicines. The referral says none. Is that right?
PT: Nothing prescribed. I do take ibuprofen though, like two or three times a week, four hundred milligrams, when the cramping and that sore spot get bad.
DR: I'm really glad you mentioned that — ibuprofen and drugs like it can actually make gut inflammation worse, so I'd like you to stop those and we'll use something safer. Anything else, any supplements?
PT: Just a blood thinner — well, my mom calls it that. It's the fish oil capsules.
DR: Ah, okay — fish oil, not an actual blood thinner. Good, that changes nothing for the procedure. Any family history?
PT: My dad has Crohn's, actually.
DR: That's useful to know. So the plan: we expedite your colonoscopy with biopsies, arrange the MR enterography, stop the ibuprofen, and confirm the diagnosis before we commit to treatment.
PT: Sounds good. What do I do for prep?
DR: The team will send full prep instructions and call you.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Redmond is a 19-year-old man presenting to GI clinic for pre-procedure consultation on an expedited basis. He reports ~3 months of frequent loose stools (5-7/day) with blood and mucus, crampy lower abdominal pain, and ~12-13 lb unintentional weight loss. He describes a tender, draining perianal opening present for about 2 months (fistulizing phenotype). He clarifies that no endoscopic or histologic confirmation of a diagnosis has been made — Crohn's disease has been suspected on the basis of an elevated fecal calprotectin but is NOT yet confirmed. He reports regular OTC ibuprofen use (400 mg, 2-3x/week) for pain, not captured in the chart. He takes OTC fish oil, which he/family referred to as a 'blood thinner'; he is on no true anticoagulant or antiplatelet. Family history notable for a father with Crohn's disease.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 82, RR 14, BP 118/74, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 61.2 kg (135 lb, down from ~67 kg/148 lb ~3 weeks prior), BMI 19.3. Referral labs — fecal calprotectin 890 ug/g (markedly elevated, 2026-06-22), CRP 28 mg/L (high), hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL (mildly low). Perianal external draining opening reported per history/PCP exam.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Suspected inflammatory bowel disease (likely Crohn's) with fistulizing perianal phenotype, chronic bloody diarrhea, weight loss, and markedly elevated calprotectin — diagnosis PROVISIONAL, not yet confirmed. Correct chart label from confirmed to suspected pending tissue diagnosis.
- Expedite diagnostic colonoscopy with ileal intubation and biopsies.
- Arrange MR enterography in parallel to assess small-bowel extent and perianal fistula anatomy.
- Stop NSAIDs: counseled to discontinue ibuprofen (may worsen intestinal inflammation); recommend acetaminophen for pain in the interim.
- Medication reconciliation: document OTC fish oil; clarified this is NOT an anticoagulant/antiplatelet — no periprocedural hold required.
- Add family history: father with Crohn's disease.
- Anemia and inflammatory markers to be rechecked; nutrition assessment given weight loss.
- Bowel prep instructions and scheduling to follow; return precautions for heavy bleeding, high fever, or severe abdominal pain.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Ongoing diarrhea with blood, belly cramps, and weight loss
• A sore, draining spot near the anus
• A stool test showing a lot of inflammation
• Whether you have Crohn's disease (not confirmed yet)

Next steps
• We will schedule your colonoscopy soon; the team will send prep instructions and call you.
• We are also arranging an MRI (MR enterography) of your belly.
• Your diagnosis is still being worked up — the colonoscopy and biopsies will tell us for sure before we start any treatment.
• Please stop taking ibuprofen (Advil/Motrin); it can make gut inflammation worse. Use acetaminophen (Tylenol) for pain instead.
• We noted your fish oil supplement — it is fine and does not need to be stopped for the procedure.
• We added that your father has Crohn's disease.
• Call for heavy bleeding, high fever, or severe belly pain.

No lesions could be excluded. Recommendation: repeat with an alternative low-volume split-dose preparation and pre-procedure antiemetic; consider extended 2-day prep given baseline constipation. No complications.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Applewhite — thanks for coming in early. We're here to plan your screening colonoscopy and figure out a prep you can actually get through this time. Tell me what happened back in 2018.
MA: Before the doctor goes on, let me get your vitals down — you're 5 foot 4, 146 pounds today; temp 98.2, blood pressure 126 over 78, heart rate 72, breathing 14, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Oh, it was awful. They gave me that big jug — the gallon of stuff — and I got maybe halfway through and I just started throwing up. I could not keep it down. They tried the next morning but they said my colon wasn't clean enough and they had to stop.
DR: I see it's in your chart as an allergy to the prep. Was it hives, swelling, trouble breathing — anything like that?
PT: No, no, nothing like that. It wasn't an allergy. I just couldn't stomach the volume. The nausea and the vomiting, that was the whole problem.
DR: That's a really important distinction — thank you. That's a tolerance issue, not a true allergy, which actually gives us more options. I'll correct that in your record. I also see you have ondansetron at home?
PT: Yes, the little tablets for nausea. I took one during the last prep but it didn't stand a chance against that much liquid.
DR: Understood. This time we'll use a low-volume, split-dose prep and pre-medicate you with the ondansetron. Any bleeding, weight loss, or change in your bowels?
PT: No bleeding, no weight change. I've always been on the constipated side, that's just me.
DR: Good — and the constipation is one reason your colon holds onto stool, so we may spread the prep over two days. Any family history of colon problems?
PT: My father had colon cancer, actually — in his early seventies.
DR: That's important, and it's not in your chart. That does bump you above average risk, so getting a complete, clean exam this time really matters. Let's book you an early morning slot and I'll send the low-volume prep instructions.
PT: Thank you. Early morning is better for me anyway.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Applewhite is a 62-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to plan colorectal cancer screening. She has never completed a colonoscopy — a 2018 attempt was aborted for inadequate preparation. She clarifies that her 2018 reaction to the high-volume polyethylene glycol prep was severe nausea and vomiting with inability to complete the prep (a tolerability/intolerance issue), NOT a true allergic reaction (no hives, swelling, or respiratory symptoms); the event is currently mislabeled as a PEG allergy in the chart. She confirms she has ondansetron 4 mg at home, which she used during the prior prep. She reports longstanding constipation. She newly reports a father diagnosed with colon cancer in his early 70s — not previously documented, elevating her risk above average. She denies hematochezia, melena, weight loss, or change in stool caliber.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 126/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 66 kg (146 lb), BMI 24.8. Recent labs (2026-06-25): creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, potassium 4.2 mmol/L — normal renal function and electrolytes, no contraindication to standard low-volume preps. No alarm features on review.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Average-to-slightly-elevated-risk screening colonoscopy, prior incomplete exam due to prep failure (2018) plus baseline constipation and now first-degree family history of CRC — inadequate-prep risk; prioritize a tolerable, effective prep.
- Correct allergy record: reclassify PEG bowel prep from 'allergy' to intolerance (nausea/vomiting); PEG-based low-volume preps remain acceptable.
- Prescribe low-volume split-dose bowel preparation; consider extended 2-day prep given constipation and prior failure.
- Pre-medicate with ondansetron 4 mg prior to and during prep for nausea; confirm patient has supply at home.
- Schedule early-morning procedure slot.
- Document new family history of colorectal cancer (father, ~70s) — may shorten future surveillance interval.
- Quality flag: inadequate-prep risk — reinforce clear-liquid diet timing, hydration, and split-dose adherence.
after_visit_summary:

What we discussed
• Planning your screening colonoscopy
• Making the prep easier after the trouble you had in 2018
• A correction to your record about the prep reaction
• New family history of colon cancer

Next steps

• Take your ondansetron for nausea before and during the prep as instructed — please make sure you have enough at home.
• We corrected your record: your 2018 reaction to the prep was nausea and vomiting (a tolerance problem), not a true allergy.
• We added that your father had colon cancer, which is important for your screening plan.
• The team will send detailed prep and diet instructions.
• Call us if you cannot keep the prep down, and we will adjust the plan.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Petrov. I'm the gastroenterologist — your primary doctor sent you over to talk about an upper endoscopy. How are you feeling?
MA: Before the doctor stepped in I roomed you and got your vitals, Mr. Petrov — you're 5'10" and 185 pounds, blood pressure 128 over 78, heart rate 88, temperature 98.2, breathing 16, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Honestly, I feel fine. I think this is a bit much, but my wife insisted I come.
FAMILY: He always says he's fine. Doctor, that's the problem — last week his stool was black. Twice. Really dark, almost tarry.
PT: It was probably something I ate. I had beets, and I take my supplements. It wasn't a big deal.
DR: I appreciate you both being here. When you say black — was it once, or did it happen more than once?
FAMILY: Twice in the same week. I saw it myself both times.
DR: And today, or the last day or two?
PT: Nothing today. Normal today. That's why I said I'm fine.
DR: Okay — that's an important distinction. Nothing today is good, but two episodes of black, tarry stools last week is exactly what we need to take seriously, even if today is normal. Black stools can mean bleeding higher up in the stomach. Let me ask about medications. The chart shows pantoprazole — the acid reducer your PCP started?
PT: Yes, I take that one every morning now. It does help the burning.
DR: Good. What about anything for your back? The chart doesn't list a pain medication.
PT: Oh — I just take naproxen. Over the counter. Two of the pills most days, for years, for my back. That's not really a medication, I buy it at the store.
DR: That actually matters a lot — daily naproxen can cause ulcers and bleeding in the stomach, and it's not in your record. I'd like you to stop it starting today. Any blood thinners, aspirin, fish oil?
PT: No, nothing like that. Just the naproxen.
DR: Good. Here's my plan: we'll draw a blood count today to check for blood loss, schedule the endoscopy soon to look at your stomach, and you stop the naproxen now. My nurse will set it up.
NURSE: I'll get the lab slip and the scheduling started before you leave.
PT: All right. If you both think it's necessary.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Petrov is a 56-year-old man referred to GI clinic for pre-procedure consultation regarding esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD). He presents with his wife. The patient minimizes symptoms and states he 'feels fine' with no black stools today. However, his wife reports witnessing two episodes of black, tarry stools in the past week; the patient attributes these to diet (beets/supplements). This collateral history of recent melena is treated as clinically significant despite the absence of symptoms today. He reports chronic low back pain for which he takes self-directed over-the-counter naproxen (~500 mg, two tablets most days) for years — this is NOT on the reconciled medication list. His PCP recently started pantoprazole 40 mg daily for reflux/dyspepsia, which he confirms taking each morning. He denies any anticoagulants, antiplatelets, aspirin, or fish oil. Denies hematemesis, lightheadedness, chest pain, or weight loss today.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 88, RR 16, BP 128/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 84 kg (185 lb), BMI 26.5. CBC ordered 2026-07-14 remains pending at time of visit; no current hemoglobin available. No prior endoscopy on file.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Suspected upper GI bleeding — collateral history of two melena episodes last week in the setting of chronic daily NSAID use. Absence of melena today does NOT exclude recent bleeding. Expedite EGD.
- Order CBC TODAY (hemoglobin/hematocrit) to assess for anemia/ongoing blood loss; follow up pending 07-14 CBC.
- NSAID cessation counseling — stop naproxen immediately; likely culprit for upper GI mucosal injury/bleeding. Add naproxen to medication list as reconciled/discontinued.
- Continue pantoprazole 40 mg daily (confirmed); consider dose escalation to twice daily pending EGD.
- No anticoagulant/antiplatelet use confirmed (only OTC naproxen, now held) — reassuring for peri-procedure bleeding risk; hold naproxen prior to EGD.
- Return precautions reviewed with patient and wife: recurrent black stools, vomiting blood, lightheadedness, or syncope warrant urgent/ED evaluation.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Planning an upper endoscopy (EGD) to look at your stomach
• Black stools your wife noticed twice last week (this can mean bleeding in the stomach, even though today was normal)
• Daily over-the-counter naproxen for your back
• Reflux and the pantoprazole your primary doctor started

Next steps
• Stop taking naproxen now — daily use can cause bleeding in the stomach. Talk to us about safer options for your back pain.
• We are drawing a blood count today to check for blood loss.
• We will schedule your upper endoscopy soon; the team will send prep and pre-procedure instructions.
• Keep taking pantoprazole each morning.
• We added naproxen to your medication record and noted you take no blood thinners.
• Call us or go to the ER right away for more black stools, vomiting blood, dizziness, or fainting.

transcript:
DR: Good afternoon, Ms. Marquez — thanks for coming in on short notice. Pulmonology asked us to see you quickly about a lymph node in your chest. How are you doing?
MA: Before the doctor gets started, let me grab your vitals — you're 5 foot 3, 119 pounds today, down a bit from before; temp's 36.8, heart rate 82, breathing 16, blood pressure 128 over 76, and your oxygen's 94 percent on room air.
PT: A bit nervous, to be honest. They found something on the scan and everyone's moving fast, so I keep thinking the worst.
DR: I understand. Let me tell you where things actually stand. The CT showed a spot in your right lung and an enlarged gland in the middle of your chest. What we don't have yet is a diagnosis — nobody has looked at the cells. That's the whole point of this procedure: we pass a scope down and take a tiny sample of that node to find out what it is.
PT: So it's not confirmed cancer? My daughter told me the paperwork already said tumor.
DR: The paperwork is getting ahead of itself — on our side it's suspicious on imaging, but suspected, not proven. The sample tells us for sure.
PT: Okay. That's a relief to hear it that way.
DR: Now, an important safety question. Are you on any blood thinners?
PT: I do a little shot in my belly every day, since I broke my hip. The nurse said it was to stop clots while I'm not moving much.
DR: That's the enoxaparin — and that's exactly what I want to get right. Is it the 40 milligram once-a-day dose, or a bigger dose twice a day?
PT: Just once a day, the small one. Same needle every morning.
DR: Perfect — so that's a preventive dose, not a full treatment-strength blood thinner. The instructions for holding it before the procedure are different and simpler for the preventive dose. I'll make sure the pre-op flag reflects that so nobody bridges you unnecessarily.
PT: Good, because I hate needles as it is.
DR: Understood. How's the hip and the breathing?
PT: Hip's still sore, I use a walker. The COPD is mild — I only puff the inhaler now and then, and I can still do my stairs.
DR: That helps with planning the sedation. We'll get you scheduled quickly, keep the oncology track moving, and I'll go over exactly when to skip that morning injection.
PT: Thank you. That makes me feel a lot better.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Marquez is a 70-year-old woman referred by pulmonology for expedited EUS-guided FNA of a subcarinal (station 7) lymph node. CT chest (2026-07-02) showed a 2.2 cm right upper lobe nodule with 1.8 cm station 7 lymphadenopathy, suspicious for primary lung malignancy with nodal involvement; there is no tissue diagnosis to date. Patient reports understandable anxiety and notes family/paperwork have referred to it as a 'tumor,' but she confirms no diagnosis has been given. She is on enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneous once daily started after a non-operative right hip fracture (June 2026) for VTE prophylaxis during reduced mobility; she confirms once-daily 40 mg dosing (not twice-daily/therapeutic). Mild COPD, uses tiotropium intermittently with preserved exercise tolerance (climbs stairs). Ambulates with a walker due to hip pain, takes acetaminophen.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 82, RR 16, BP 128/76, SpO2 94% on RA; Ht 160 cm (5'3"), Wt 54 kg (119 lb), BMI 21.1 (down from ~61 kg / 134 lb, consistent with reported weight loss). Recent labs (2026-07-15): hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, platelets 244 x10^3/uL, INR 1.0, aPTT 31 s, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (normal renal function). CT chest as above.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Mediastinal (station 7) lymphadenopathy with suspicious RUL nodule — malignancy SUSPECTED, NOT confirmed; no prior tissue diagnosis. Plan EUS-FNA of station 7 node for diagnosis and staging; oncology-expedite routing is appropriate.
- Correct the chart: lung malignancy is provisional/unconfirmed pending pathology from this procedure.
- Anticoagulation: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily is PROPHYLACTIC, not therapeutic. Apply prophylactic-LMWH periprocedural hold rules (single dose held morning of / evening dose before per protocol); do NOT apply therapeutic bridging algorithm. Renal function normal. Update pre-op flag accordingly.
- Mild COPD — stable, good functional status; relevant to sedation/anesthesia planning; continue tiotropium.
- Non-operative right hip fracture — limited mobility, continue VTE prophylaxis until mobility improves.
- Procedure scheduling expedited; patient to receive specific instruction on holding the morning enoxaparin dose and pre-procedure fasting.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• A lung spot and an enlarged gland in your chest seen on your CT scan
• The plan for a procedure (EUS-FNA) to take a small sample of that gland
• Your daily preventive blood-thinner shot and your breathing

Important points
• Your scan looks suspicious, but this is NOT a confirmed cancer diagnosis. The procedure is how we find out for sure.
• Your enoxaparin shot is a low, once-a-day PREVENTIVE dose (to stop clots while your hip heals), not a full-strength blood thinner. We will give you simple instructions on when to skip a dose before the procedure — do not stop it on your own.
• Your recent blood counts and clotting labs were normal.

Next steps
• We will schedule your EUS-FNA quickly and keep the cancer-care team informed.
• The team will call with the exact date, when to stop eating/drinking, and when to hold your morning injection.
• Keep using your inhaler as usual and take acetaminophen for hip pain.
• Call us for chest pain, trouble breathing, fever, or heavy bleeding/bruising.
